[]{#Frontmatter_004.xhtml}

:::: {.section .Preface .preface aria-labelledby="prefacetitle" role="doc-preface"}
# Introduction {#Frontmatter_004.xhtml_prefacetitle .PrefaceTitle lang="en"}

::: PrefaceBody
[]{#Frontmatter_004.xhtml_PBxv .pagebreak aria-label="xv"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The advent of generative AI is reshaping the way we
design our systems today. Unlike traditional AI/ML-based systems that
analyze and predict, generative AI creates, whether it\'s text, images,
video, audio, or code; it's changing the way we interact with data and
solve problems. Fueled by large language models (LLMs), it represents a
massive shift in the way enterprises now approach AI-based
problem-solving.

Much like the rise of big data in the early 2010s, generative AI, LLMs,
and agentic AI are not just buzzwords---it is a confluence of
advancements in foundation models, computing infrastructure, open source
ecosystems, and cloud platforms. While the capabilities are impressive,
the real value lies in applying it meaningfully within the fabric of
enterprise systems---where scalability, precision, explainability, and
data privacy are non-negotiable.

In this book, we explore how generative AI, when combined with
structured system design and responsible deployment, can become a
powerful accelerator for modern enterprise solutions. From design
patterns to architecture, prompt engineering to evaluation frameworks,
deployment pipelines, and best practices, this book aims to be your
practical guide in building production-ready GenAI applications. Our
experience has taught us that implementing GenAI at scale is not just a
technical challenge---it's a multidisciplinary journey that involves
thoughtful planning, risk awareness, and continuous optimization. We've
tried to cover the ground that matters most---what works, what doesn't,
and what to watch out for.

Time is valuable more so in today\'s fast-paced world. We thank you for
choosing to invest your valuable time in reading this book. We hope you
find its contents insightful and, most importantly, applicable to your
enterprise AI journey. Wishing you the best in building secure and
responsible AI solutions.
:::
::::

[]{#Frontmatter_005.xhtml}

[]{#Frontmatter_006.xhtml}

:::: {.section .BookAcknowledgments .acknowledgments aria-labelledby="acknowledgmenttitle" role="doc-acknowledgments"}
# Acknowledgments {#Frontmatter_006.xhtml_acknowledgmenttitle .BookAcknowledgmentsTitle lang="en"}

::: BookAcknowledgmentsBody
Writing this book, *Enterprise Guide for Implementing Generative AI and
Agentic AI*, has been a journey of exploration, collaboration, and
discovery. It would not have been possible without the support and
contributions of many individuals.

First and foremost, we would like to extend our deepest gratitude to one
another[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}coauthors and
collaborators in this journey. This book is a true reflection of shared
curiosity, collective insight, and the countless hours of exploration we
undertook together. Each perspective brought strength, each conversation
added depth, and every challenge was met with mutual respect and a
common goal---to create something meaningful. Working alongside has been
a privilege and an enriching experience, making this journey fulfilling.

We are immensely grateful to our organization for providing the
environment that fosters creativity and innovation. Your encouragement
and resources have empowered us to delve into the complexities and
potentials of generative AI and agentic AI, bringing forth this
comprehensive resource.

A special thank-you goes to our families and friends, who have been our
pillars of support throughout this endeavor. Your patience,
encouragement, and understanding have been our bedrock, enabling us to
dedicate ourselves to this project.

We also wish to acknowledge the broader AI community, whose pioneering
research and discussions have inspired and informed this guide. Your
contributions to the field are paving the way for groundbreaking
advancements and applications that will shape our future.

Lastly, we extend our gratitude to our readers. Your curiosity and
enthusiasm for AI drive us to explore, understand, and share knowledge.
We truly hope this book serves as a valuable tool in your journey to
implementing and harnessing the transformative power of generative AI
and agentic AI. **Thank you all for being a part of this journey**.
:::
::::

[]{#Frontmatter_007.xhtml}

<figure id="Figa" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO1" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Figa_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="A collage of images related to technology and data. The top left shows a digital interface with icons like AI, a globe, and a lightbulb. The top right features an Enigma machine with dials and keys. The bottom left displays a network of glowing nodes and lines. The center has a silhouette of a head with circuit patterns. The right side includes a flow chart with interconnected nodes labeled with terms like &quot;Genetics,&quot; &quot;Diabetes,&quot; and &quot;Hypertension,&quot; connected by arrows." />
</div>
</figure>

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par5 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB3 .pagebreak aria-label="3"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Artificial intelligence (AI) has been around for
decades, but the recent explosion of interest is largely driven by
[large language models (LLMs)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm1} and
[generative AI (GenAI)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm2}. Figure
[[1-1](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig1)]{.InternalRef} shows change in
interest and understanding regarding AI and its
[advantages]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm3}.[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB4
.pagebreak aria-label="4" role="doc-pagebreak"}

<figure id="Fig1" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO2" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig1_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Heatmap showing percentage point change in opinions about AI by country from 2022 to 2023. Rows represent statements about AI, such as understanding, benefits, trust, and nervousness. Columns represent countries, including Argentina, Australia, and the United States. Color intensity indicates the degree of change, with red for negative and blue for positive changes. Notable changes include a 24% increase in nervousness in Italy and a 9% decrease in understanding in Colombia. Data source: Ipsos, 2022-23." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-1</span>
<p>% change in acknowledgment of AI <span
id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm4">benefits</span> across
countries<sup><a href="#fn1" class="footnote-ref" id="fnref1"
role="doc-noteref"><sup>1</sup></a></sup></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
<section id="footnotes" class="footnotes footnotes-end-of-document"
role="doc-endnotes">
<hr />
<ol>
<li id="fn1"><p><span class="ExternalRef"><a
href="https://aiindex.stanford.edu/report/"><span class="RefSource">AI
Index Report 2024–Artificial Intelligence Index</span></a></span><a
href="#fnref1" class="footnote-back" role="doc-backlink">↩︎</a></p></li>
</ol>
</section>
:::

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par7 .Para}
The above figure shows how perception is changing toward
[AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm5}---specifically, the interest
in understanding AI and its potential benefits. It highlights a shift
from initial curiosity or uncertainty to awareness. Generative AI and
large language models are accelerating the general public\'s shifting
perception of AI while also impacting the AI adoption across various
sectors and industries, pushing the boundaries of what an AI-enabled
system can achieve. Investments in AI space are increasing, and
projections indicate that it will surpass \$1 trillion in the coming
year.

::: OrderedList
1.  ::: ItemNumber
    1\.
    :::

    :::: ItemContent
    ::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par8 .Para}
    > *The market size in the Generative AI market is projected to reach
    > US\$36.06bn in 2024.*
    :::
    ::::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

2.  ::: ItemNumber
    2\.
    :::

    :::: ItemContent
    ::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par10 .Para}
    > *The market size is expected to show an annual growth rate (CAGR
    > 2024-2030) of 46.47%, resulting in a market volume of US\$356.10bn
    > by 2030.*
    :::
    ::::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::

3.  ::: ItemNumber
    3\.
    :::

    :::: ItemContent
    ::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par12 .Para}
    > *In global comparison, the largest market size will be in the
    > United States (US\$11.66bn in 2024).*
    :::
    ::::

    ::: ClearBoth
     
    :::
:::
::::

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par14 .Para}
> ---Generative AI worldwide^[^1]^
:::

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB5 .pagebreak aria-label="5"
role="doc-pagebreak"}AI has come a long way from the foundational work
of researchers and visionaries to the present-day transformative power
of generative [AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm6}. The field has
witnessed remarkable breakthroughs over the years. Each milestone has
led to the current state and is paving the way to a future filled with
infinite possibilities. It continues to redefine the way we interact,
work, and communicate with machines. To appreciate the present and
future state of AI, it's important to understand its history.
Understanding the developments that happened over decades and led us to
generative AI is essential to grasp what lies ahead.

In this chapter, we will explore several important developments;
however, before we delve deeper into history, let's first briefly define
AI.

::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec1Heading"}
## Artificial Intelligence (AI) {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1Heading .Heading}

Artificial [intelligence]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm7} is a
machine's ability to perform human-like cognitive functions and actions.
Machines demonstrate skills that were once unique to humans. When most
people refer to the term artificial intelligence (AI) today, they are
referring to a collection of technologies that include [natural language
processing (NLP)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm8}, [machine
learning (ML)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm9}, and [deep learning
(DL)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm10}. AI is an umbrella term used
to define the technologies which enhance the capabilities of computers
to perform tasks which typically require human cognition.

The field of AI focuses on developing algorithms and computational
models which enable the computer systems to perform
[activities]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm11} such as learning from
experience, problem-solving, recognizing patterns and trends in data,
analyzing and interpreting visual information, understanding and
responding in natural language, and making informed decisions. These AI
systems are designed to process and interpret data, learn from it,
derive insights, and apply the knowledge to adapt to new inputs and
perform tasks.

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par21 .Para}
AI is developed on the assumption that the process of human thoughts and
actions can be encoded and automated, leading to development of multiple
programs, methodologies, and algorithms to simulate human or rational
intelligence in machines. Figure
[[1-2](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig2)]{.InternalRef} provides a
structured overview of [AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm12} by
dividing it into four quadrants, each describing different aspects of
AI.[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB6 .pagebreak aria-label="6"
role="doc-pagebreak"}

<figure id="Fig2" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO3" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig2_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle4"
alt="Flow chart illustrating four approaches to artificial intelligence. Top left: &quot;Thinking Humanly&quot; involves cognitive science and model development to mimic human thought. Top right: &quot;Thinking Rationally&quot; refers to logic and formal knowledge representation. Bottom left: &quot;Acting Humanly&quot; covers areas like natural language processing. Bottom right: &quot;Acting Rationally&quot; involves autonomous agents adapting to their environment. Arrows point outward from the center, dividing the sections." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-2</span>
<p>Overview of <span
id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm13">AI</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par22 .Para}
Twentieth-century mathematicians, researchers, and scientists envisioned
a future where machines perform functions faster than humans. These
machines evolved from performing simple calculations to be able to adapt
to a wide range of inputs, being trained to detect patterns and provide
recommendations or make predictions. Figure
[[1-3](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig3)]{.InternalRef} shows the
evolution of major [techniques]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm14} in
the field. In the subsequent sections, we will delve into these
developments in detail.

<figure id="Fig3" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO4" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig3_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle5"
alt="Flow chart illustrating the relationship between Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), Deep Learning, and Generative AI. AI is described as a broad field for developing algorithms that replicate human-like behaviors. ML evolves from explicit programming to pattern detection in large datasets. Deep Learning, an advanced ML branch, uses neural networks for complex data features and predictions. Generative AI, a Deep Learning branch, employs large neural networks to generate new content from trained datasets. Arrows indicate progression from AI to Generative AI." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-3</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15">Evolution</span> of
AI</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::
:::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec2Heading"}
## Journey of AI {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB7 .pagebreak aria-label="7"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The journey began with the study of mathematical
logic, which provided the theoretical foundation for reasoning systems.
This study provided the relevant
[insights]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16} and breakthroughs that
made the concept of artificial intelligence seem possible.

In the 1920s, David Hilbert, a German mathematician, challenged
mathematicians of his time to answer a fundamental question which
stated, "*Can all of mathematical reasoning be formalized?*"

The ***Turing machine,*** among other
[developments]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17}, emerged as an
answer to Hilbert's challenge. This work suggested that, within the
limits of mathematical logic's capabilities, it was possible to program
and automate any form of mathematical reasoning.

::::::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec3Heading"}
### Inception of AI {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par26 .Para}
> *The 1940s and 1950s witnessed the formalization of AI.*
:::

The beginning of AI is often dated back to the 1940s and 1950s, a period
that saw the [formalization]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18} of
the AI field. During the 1940s, AI's usage was primarily confined to
government, academic, and scientific research. One of the significant
developments of the era was the [Turing
machine]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19}, a machine capable of
executing multiple different tasks that could be programmed without
altering its machinery. The realization of the concept and promise of AI
began with the advent of the first programmable digital computers.
Though primitive by today's standards, these computers enabled the
implementation of the first generation of AI methods through their
ability to perform complex calculations with speed and accuracy. In the
subsequent sections, we will explore few of the key advancements of the
1940s and 1950s which transformed the theoretical concept of AI into a
dynamic field of research and practical applications.

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec4Heading"}
#### Turing Machine {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par29 .Para}
> *A* *machine that can execute multiple tasks, each of which can be
> programmed with a set of instructions without making any changes in
> the physical machinery.*
:::

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB8 .pagebreak aria-label="8"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The [Turing
machine]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21}
is a conceptual model that played a pivotal role in the history of
computing. Alan Turing, a distinguished mathematician and computer
scientist, laid the foundation work for the age of computers. In 1936,
while doing his PhD, he published a paper which formed the foundation
for the development of computers.

He introduced the concept of a universal machine which could solve any
problem that could be described by simple and basic instructions. This
led to the invention of the computer, a machine which can solve any
problem and execute any task for which we can write a program.

Before this invention, humans were acting as "computers," performing
complex engineering calculations manually. However, during World War II,
the allied forces faced a critical shortage of these human computers.
[Mathematicians]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22} were urgently
needed to decipher the German navy's Enigma code. This code had an
overwhelming number of 10^114^ possible permutations.

To solve this, Turing designed the
[**Bombe**]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24},
an electromechanical cryptanalysis machine designed specifically to
search through the permutations efficiently and help decipher the
encrypted messages. Figure
[[1-4](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig4)]{.InternalRef} shows the
Turing Bombe.

<figure id="Fig4" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO5" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig4_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle6"
alt="A person stands in front of a large, complex machine with multiple dials and components, appearing to operate or adjust it. The machine has a series of circular dials and switches, suggesting it is an early computing or code-breaking device. The scene is in black and white, indicating a historical context." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-4</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25">Turing</span>
Bombe</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB9 .pagebreak aria-label="9"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The entire logic was based on the
[observation]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm26} that each encrypted
message contained a known piece of German plaintext. This machine
significantly accelerated the decoding process and decoded the messages
in a few hours which otherwise used to take days/weeks. Figure
[[1-5](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig5)]{.InternalRef} shows a diagram
of the front panel of an idealized Bombe,^[^2]^
[showcasing]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm27} the creativity and
complexity of Turing's design.

<figure id="Fig5" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO6" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig5_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle7"
alt="Diagram of a machine with three rows of circular elements labeled as &quot;fast drums,&quot; &quot;middle drums,&quot; and &quot;slow drums.&quot; Each row contains a series of circles, some shaded differently. Labels indicate &quot;batteries of 12 double ended scramblers&quot; and &quot;drums removed to expose template.&quot; The top right corner notes &quot;double ended scramblers.&quot; The circles are arranged in a grid pattern, illustrating a mechanical or computational process." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-5</span>
<p>Front panel of an “idealized Bombe”<span
class="EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional">—</span>simplified and
theoretical representation of the <span
id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm28">Bombe</span> machines</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

This was one of the initial references of an algorithm being used to
expedite and improve human activity, enabling effective and faster
processing.

Through the 1950s, businesses were quick to see the benefits of
computers, and this led to the emergence of a new industry.
[**ENIAC**]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm29}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm30}
(shown in Figure
[[1-6](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig6)]{.InternalRef}), the first
programmable electronic general-purpose digital computer based on the
theoretical foundation laid by Alan Turing, was developed during World
War II.

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB10 .pagebreak aria-label="10"
role="doc-pagebreak"}It was able to solve a large class of numerical
problems through reprogramming, demonstrating functionalities similar to
that of a Turing complete machine. Although not considered to be
strictly Turing complete by modern definitions, its
design[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}developed by **John von
Neumann**[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}proved to be highly
influential in the evolution of computing.

<figure id="Fig6" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO7" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig6_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle8"
alt="A historical black and white photograph of a large room filled with early computer equipment. The room contains numerous panels with wires and switches. Two people are present; one is interacting with the equipment, while the other is reading a document. The scene captures the scale and complexity of early computing technology." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-6</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31">ENIAC</span><span
id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32"></span>, the first computer</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec5Heading"}
#### Test for Machine Intelligence {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par40 .Para}
> *"Computers as intelligent as humans." The Turing test opened doors
> for AI.*
:::

Alan Turing's [vision]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm33} extended
beyond the creation of computers to the field of artificial
intelligence. In 1950, Alan Turing went ahead and published a paper
called "[*Computing Machinery and
Intelligence*]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm34}," in which he
envisaged a future where artificial intelligence (AI) would become a
reality. He proposed the idea that computers would become so powerful
that they would be capable of thinking.

To prove that a machine is intelligent, he devised the ***Turing
test***. The [core]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm35} idea was
simple. A human judge engages in a text-based conversation with both a
human and a machine without knowing which is which. The output generated
is presented to a human judge. The judge's task is to determine which
output is from a human and which one is from a machine, and if the judge
is not able to distinguish one from the other, the
[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB11 .pagebreak aria-label="11"
role="doc-pagebreak"}machine is said to have passed the [Turing
test]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm36} of being able to think and
make decisions rationally and intelligently like humans. Figure
[[1-7](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig7)]{.InternalRef} provides a
visual representation of the Turing test.

<figure id="Fig7" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO8" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig7_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle9"
alt="Diagram illustrating a Turing Test setup. On the left, labeled &quot;Machine,&quot; is a computer with the letter &quot;A.&quot; On the right, labeled &quot;Human,&quot; is a person with the letter &quot;B.&quot; Both the machine and human communicate with a &quot;Tester,&quot; labeled &quot;C,&quot; at the bottom, via text on paper. The tester must determine which is the machine and which is the human based on the responses." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-7</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37">Turing test</span>,
visual representation</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

While the Turing test laid the conceptual foundation, it was the
Dartmouth [workshop]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38} that marked a
major leap forward in the formal development of the field.
::::

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec6Heading"}
#### Birth of the Discipline {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6Heading .Heading}

The birth of artificial
[intelligence]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39} as an academic
discipline happened in the **Dartmouth workshop,**
[which]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40} was organized by Marvin
Minsky and John McCarthy in 1956. This event marked the formal inception
of AI and brought together the brightest minds and key figures in
mathematics and computer science to explore the potential and
possibilities.

The proposal set forward in the conference was the testing of the
assertion that every aspect of learning or any feature of intelligence
can be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it.
It was in this conference that John McCarthy coined the term artificial
intelligence. This workshop set the stage for the next several decades
of research in the field of [AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41}.
It was believed that, with enough time, effort, and resources, a machine
could be created which will not only compute faster but will also learn
and identify patterns. In the following years, AI research flourished.
LISP, a new programming language, was created by McCarthy which became
the standard tool for AI research for decades.
:::
:::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
### Early Programs {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB12 .pagebreak aria-label="12"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Following the [Dartmouth
workshop]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42}, in the late 1950s and
1960s, continued innovations and explorations happened in the field and
several programs were developed. AI research branched out into various
subfields, each focusing on different aspects of learning and
intelligence simulation. In the next few sections, we will highlight a
few of these notable developments that happened during this era.

::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
#### Problem-Solving {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

This era saw the development of many early programs that used basic
[algorithms]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm43} to achieve a goal,
like in a game or while solving a theorem. It followed a step-by-step
approach by making a move as if searching through a maze and
backtracking, i.e., moving back whenever a dead end is reached.

One of the notable developments of this time was the ***General Problem
Solver (GPS)*** developed by Allen Newell and Herbert A. Simon.
[GPS]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44} represented a general
version of this step-by-step algorithm.

The key idea behind GPS was that many problems can be formulated as a
search through a problem space which is represented as a directed graph
where nodes represent states or conditions and edges represents the
possible actions that can be taken to transition from one state to
another. So, any problem that can be expressed as a set of well-formed
formulas and constitutes a directed graph as shown in Figure
[[1-8](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig8)]{.InternalRef} with one or
more sources, i.e., hypothesis, and sinks, i.e., desired conclusion, can
be solved in principle by GPS.

<figure id="Fig8" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO9" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig8_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle10"
alt="Flow chart titled &quot;Directed acyclic graph&quot; showing decision paths. The top node is &quot;1 vs. 4,&quot; leading to two branches labeled &quot;reject 1&quot; and &quot;reject 4.&quot; The left branch leads to &quot;2 vs. 4,&quot; which further branches into &quot;reject 2&quot; leading to &quot;3 vs. 4&quot; and &quot;reject 4&quot; leading to &quot;SAC3.&quot; The right branch from &quot;1 vs. 4&quot; leads to &quot;1 vs. 3,&quot; branching into &quot;reject 1&quot; leading to &quot;2 vs. 3&quot; and &quot;reject 3&quot; leading to &quot;1 vs. 2.&quot; The final nodes are labeled &quot;SAC4,&quot; &quot;SAC3,&quot; &quot;SAC2,&quot; and &quot;SAC1.&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-8</span>
<p>Schematic <span
id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm45">diagram</span> of a General
Problem Solver</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB13 .pagebreak aria-label="13"
role="doc-pagebreak"}[GPS]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm46} was the
first program that separated its domain knowledge of the problem, i.e.,
rules of the problem, represented as input data from its general
problem-solving strategy, i.e., the solver engine. This means that the
same solver engine could be used to solve different problems.

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_FPar1 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

GPS was a precursor to later developments in AI such as expert systems.
::::

Many other problem-solving programs were also developed which
accomplished tasks such as solving problems in algebra. This included
SAINT (Symbolic Automatic
[Integrator]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47}) created by Marvin
Minsky's student James Sagle in 1961. This program specialized in
symbolic manipulation to solve algebra problems. It manipulated symbols
and expressions according to algebra rules. This program demonstrated
that computers could engage in more abstract reasoning and marked a step
forward to create machines that could mimic human cognition.

These programs proved that alongside routine calculations, computers can
perform empirical problem-solving and further inspired research on
algorithms that could handle increasingly complex tasks.

The 1960s also saw the development of the first natural language
processing (NLP) programs, which aimed to enable computers to understand
human language.
:::::

:::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
#### Natural Language Processing {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB14 .pagebreak aria-label="14"
role="doc-pagebreak"}A significant objective of
[AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48} researchers was to develop a
program that enhances a computer's capability to understand and
communicate in a natural language such as English. One of the early
[successes]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49} in this area was the
creation of **STUDENT**, a program which could be considered as a
precursor to modern-day question-and-answer software.

This program was developed by Daniel Bobrow for his PhD thesis in 1964.
STUDENT's primary function was to read, interpret, and solve high school
algebra word problems. To do this, it used preprogrammed rules which
allowed it to parse the natural language, extract relevant information,
and apply algebraic methods to find the solution.

Subsequent to STUDENT, new programs were developed for natural language
[processing]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50} that were based on
the concept of a knowledge representation known as ***semantic net*** or
***semantic network***.

Semantic [net]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51} is a graphical
representation of knowledge where concepts formed the vertices or nodes
and relations between these concepts were represented as links or edges.
This structure helped computers understand the meaning or semantics
behind words and sentences. Figure
[[1-9](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig9)]{.InternalRef} shows a simple
[example]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52} of how knowledge is
represented using semantic net.

<figure id="Fig9" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO10" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig9_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle11"
alt="Flow chart illustrating relationships among animals. &quot;Mammal&quot; is central, connected to &quot;Animal&quot; with &quot;is an&quot; and to &quot;Vertebra&quot; with &quot;has.&quot; &quot;Mammal&quot; links to &quot;Cat&quot; and &quot;Bear&quot; with &quot;is a,&quot; both having &quot;Fur.&quot; &quot;Whale&quot; is a &quot;Mammal&quot; and &quot;lives in&quot; &quot;Water.&quot; &quot;Fish&quot; is an &quot;Animal&quot; and &quot;lives in&quot; &quot;Water.&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-9</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53">Example</span> of a
semantic network</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB15 .pagebreak aria-label="15"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Using this representation, a computer program could
deduce that if someone is talking about *Cat,* they are also referring
to a *Mammal* which is an *Animal* that has *fur*. This was an important
development for AI as it enabled computer programs to have deeper
understanding of the meaning of words and relationships between them
beyond simple keyword matching.

Another significant achievement from this era was the creation of
**ELIZA** program by *Joseph Weizenbaum* at Massachusetts Institute of
Technology (MIT) in the mid-1960s.
[ELIZA]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55}
demonstrated the potential of simple AI techniques for creating engaging
and human-like interactions.

The program employed ***pattern matching and substitution***
[methodology]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58}
giving an illusion of understanding on the program side to the users.
The program scans the input for recognizable patterns or keywords e.g.,
if a user types "*I am feeling sad*," it identifies the keyword "*sad*."
Once it identifies the pattern, it then uses the substitution rule to
construct a response, such as rephrasing the user's query or asking a
relevant question. In the above example,
[ELIZA]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59} might respond "*I am sorry
to hear you are sad. Can you tell me more about it*."

This methodology allowed the program to recognize certain patterns in
the input and respond with predefined
[templates]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60}. **DOCTOR** is the
most famous implementation of ELIZA which simulates a *psychotherapy
session*, engaging users in a conversation that mimicked a therapist
response.

::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par64 .Para}
Sample [pseudocode]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61}:

:::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PC1 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
function ELIZA GENERATOR (user sentence) returns response
:::

::: FixedLine
   Let w be the word in sentence that has the highest keyword rank
:::

::: FixedLine
   if w exists
:::

::: FixedLine
       Let r be the highest ranked rule for w that matches sentence
:::

::: FixedLine
       response ← Apply the transform in r to sentence
:::

::: FixedLine
       if w = \'my\'
:::

::: FixedLine
           future ← Apply a transformation from the \'memory\' rule list
to sentence
:::

::: FixedLine
           Push future onto the memory queue
:::

::: FixedLine
       else (no keyword applies)
:::

::: FixedLine
           Either
:::

::: FixedLine
               response ← Apply the transform for the NONE keyword to
sentence
:::

::: FixedLine
           Or
:::

::: FixedLine
               response ← Pop the oldest response from the memory queue
:::

::: FixedLine
               Return response
:::
:::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB16 .pagebreak aria-label="16"
role="doc-pagebreak"}This ability to process natural language was a
significant leap forward for AI, laying the groundwork for the
development of more advanced and sophisticated natural language
processing systems and intelligent assistants that we see today.
::::::::::::::::::::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
#### Early Neural Networks {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

In the previous sections, we discussed few implementations of [symbolic
AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm62}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm63},
which was the prime focus of AI research during the 1960s. This is also
referred to as [classical AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm64} or
GOFAI (good old-fashioned [AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm65}),
and, as we saw, it was primarily based on the explicit programming of
logic and knowledge to solve problems, requiring all the knowledge to be
manually entered into the system. This is effective for rule-based,
well-defined tasks but less adept in handling complex real-world
problems as the system was not programmed to observe and learn.

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par67 .Para}
Amidst this, a handful of researchers began to explore the potential of
neural networks. [Neural networks]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm66}
are computational models inspired by the structure and function of
biological neural networks, such as those found in the human brain.
Frank Rosenblatt's introduction of the ***Perceptron***
[marked]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm67}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm68}
an early milestone in this area. The ***Perceptron***, a single-layer
neural network, was capable of learning patterns that are linearly
[separable]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm69}. This means it could
classify data that could be separated by a single straight line in the
feature space as shown in Figure
[[1-10](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig10)]{.InternalRef}.

<figure id="Fig10" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO11" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig10_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle12"
alt="Scatter plot showing two groups of data points separated by a diagonal decision boundary. The left region is shaded blue with blue data points, and the right region is shaded red with red data points." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-10</span>
<p>Linearly <span
id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm70">separable</span> data</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

In modern terms, a
[perceptron]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm71}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm72}
is essentially a binary classifier function that determines whether an
input (represented by a vector of numbers) belongs to a particular
class. This exploration of [neural
networks]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm73} in 1960s paved the way
[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB17 .pagebreak aria-label="17"
role="doc-pagebreak"}for what would become a significant area of AI
research later. It was a simple yet groundbreaking step toward creating
systems that could learn from data.

However, it was soon discovered that the [single-layer
perceptron]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm74} had significant
limitations, and it could not recognize many classes of patterns. This
led to a period of stagnation in the neural
[network]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm75} field for many years. It
wasn't until the late 1980s that neural networks regained prominence
when it was recognized that a [multilayer
perceptron]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm76} with two or more
layers, also known as [feedforward neural
network]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm77}, has greater processing
power than their single-layer counterpart. These additional layers
enabled the network to learn more complex and nonlinear patterns. We
will cover this discovery in a while.

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_FPar2 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

The initial perceptron was designed to be a machine and was subsequently
implemented in custom built hardware as the Mark I Perceptron designed
for image recognition.
::::

As research [progressed]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm78}, it
became evident that programs require significant computing power for
running complex calculations. In the early 1970s, the capabilities of AI
were limited due to the compute power available at the time. The
hardware was not ready to meet the demanding requirements of the
[programs]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm79} being researched, which
required vast number of computational steps to prove theorems and
process data.
::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
### AI Revolutionary Leap {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par72 .Para}
> *Resurrection* *in the 1980s marked a revolutionary leap in AI
> research, paving the way for today's intelligent systems. This era led
> to creation of expert systems, knowledge databases, parallel
> processing and neural network reinvention.*
:::

From the 1980s till the year 2000, the AI field saw
[significant]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm80} advancements. It
began with [rule-based expert
systems]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm81} that encapsulated the
knowledge of a human expert in a specific domain to mimic their
decision-making abilities. Parallel processing emerged as a solution to
the computational limits that previously limited AI experimentation.
Knowledge databases grew in importance, enabling AI to learn from vast
amounts of information. The resurgence of [neural
networks]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm82} was a pivotal
development that led to the emergence of algorithms that could not only
learn patterns from data but also improve over time. Probabilistic model
also came to the forefront, enabling the AI
[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB18 .pagebreak aria-label="18"
role="doc-pagebreak"}systems to deal with uncertainties and make
predictions based on probabilities. This was a transformative period,
laying the groundwork for the sophisticated AI technologies of today.
The following section will delve into the key developments of this
period.

:::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}
#### Knowledge-Based Systems {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12Heading .Heading}

[Expert systems]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm83} were
[created]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm84} in the 1970s but truly
flourished in the 1980s, becoming one of the first widely implemented AI
applications. These systems emulated human decision-making abilities
within a specific domain, addressing and answering complex problems by
reasoning through extensive bodies of knowledge, represented primarily
as if-then rules.

The key components of an expert system include a domain-specific
knowledge base that represents facts and rules which guide the system
toward a solution and an inference engine which applies rules to the
knowledge base to either deduce new information or make decisions. It
can handle forward chaining from data to conclusion and backward
chaining from conclusion to data.

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_FPar3 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Expert system is an early example of a knowledge-based program that
references a knowledge base to solve problems.
::::

Notable early implementations of [expert
systems]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm85} include MYCIN, developed
at Stanford University to diagnose blood infections and recommend
antibiotics with its dosage adjusted to the patient's body weight, and
R1 (also known as [XCON]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm86}),
developed by Carnegie Mellon University for a major American computer
company[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}[Digital Equipment
Corporation
(DEC)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm87}[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}to
configure orders for a new computer ensuring all parts are included and
are compatible with each other.

The strength of expert systems came from the domain knowledge they
contained. In the 1980s, knowledge-based systems were a major focus of
AI research with the aim to enrich computers with the vast amount of
knowledge acquired by humans through experience and education. Also
known as [commonsense knowledge]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm88},
it includes basic facts about our world, logical reasoning, and the
ability to make inferences about everyday situations. Researchers
devised and discussed approaches to solve the "commonsense knowledge
problem"[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}the challenge of
enabling machines with the implicit knowledge that a human acquires over
their lifetime.

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB19 .pagebreak aria-label="19"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The hypothesis was that by encoding all the
knowledge into databases, the AI systems could better understand the
problems at hand and perform more human-like reasoning. It was hoped
that vast databases would solve the commonsense knowledge problem.

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par81 .Para}
Researchers started creating massive databases filled with mundane facts
that an average person knows which is crucial for understanding, making
inferences, reasoning, and acting.

> *The only way for machines to know the meaning of human concepts is to
> teach them, one concept at a time in granular and systematic way,
> similar to how humans learn over time.*

> *---Douglas Lenat*
:::

One of the most significant efforts in this direction was the initiation
of the **Cyc** [project]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm89} by
**Douglas Lenat,** where he and his team manually entered facts and
rules into the Cyc database. The Cyc project captured not only facts but
also relationships and contexts that govern their application, for
example, where the system needed to know that birds typically fly, but
penguins are an exception to that fact.

Other notable projects include
[WordNet]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm90}, which groups words into
synonym sets (synsets) and documents the different semantic
relationships between them, ConceptNet and OpenCyc, among others. These
efforts contributed to the development of ontologies, semantic networks,
and other forms of knowledge representation that continue to influence
AI research and its applications today.

Despite their successes, these systems had limitations, including
expensive and time-consuming efforts required for build and maintenance.
Consequently, other approaches were explored and gained significant
momentum during this period, which we will discuss in the upcoming
sections.
::::::

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec13Heading"}
#### Neural Network Revival---The Connectionist Approach {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13Heading .Heading}

The 1980s saw a pivotal revival in neural
[network]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm91}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm92}
research. This was driven by two key developments: the publication of
the "Parallel Distributed Processing" paper by David Rumelhart, James
McClelland, and their colleagues and the influential work of John
Hopfield. Together, they addressed the limitations of the earlier neural
network model, the [single-layer
perceptron]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm93}, and reignited
interest in the field.

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB20 .pagebreak aria-label="20"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The "[Parallel distributed
processing]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm94}" research introduced
the key concepts of intermediate processing layers, known as [hidden
layers]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm95}, and the [backpropagation
algorithm]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm96}, which were crucial
advancements in neural network architecture and its training algorithm.

[Hidden layers]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm97} are one or more
layers situated between the input and output layers as depicted in a
Neural network example in Figure
[[1-11](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig11)]{.InternalRef}. Each layer
consists of neurons/nodes which are connected though weighted
connections to every neuron in the next layer and performs complex
computations. These layers transform the input data and are capable of
detecting and amplifying features that are critical for predictions or
classifications.

<figure id="Fig11" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO12" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig11_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle13"
alt="Diagram of a neural network with labeled layers. The input layer on the left, labeled &quot;X,&quot; has 784 nodes. There are three hidden layers with 512, 256, and 128 nodes respectively. The output layer on the right, labeled &quot;Y,&quot; has 10 nodes. Arrows indicate connections between nodes across layers, representing the flow of data through the network." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-11</span>
<p>Neural <span id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm98">network</span>
example</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[Backpropagation]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm99} is a training
algorithm that enables neural networks to learn from data. It trains by
forwarding the input data through the network to predict an output,
calculating the error using a loss function by comparing the predicted
output with the expected actual output, and then propagating the error
backwards through the network. This process adjusts the connection
weights to minimize the error.

This combination of [hidden
layers]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm100} and
[backpropagation]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm101} enabled neural
networks to learn from data without explicit programming. Instead, the
network self-optimizes through exposure to multiple examples and
iterative adjustments. This led to development of multilayer perceptrons
and formed the foundation of modern neural networks.

In parallel, John Hopfield introduced the [Hopfield
network]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm102} as shown in Figure
[[1-12](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig12)]{.InternalRef}, an
associative memory system capable of storing and retrieving patterns,
even when the input is incomplete or noisy. For example, a Hopfield
network trained to remember a set []{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB21
.pagebreak aria-label="21" role="doc-pagebreak"}of facial images can
recall the most similar complete face from its stored patterns even if
we present a partial or noisy image of one of the faces (e.g., a face
with sunglasses or a face partially obscured).

<figure id="Fig12" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO13" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig12_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Diagram illustrating a Hopfield network model for associative memory. The network consists of interconnected nodes with arrows indicating connections. A red arrow labeled &quot;Learning&quot; points to a section of the network. On the right, three pixelated images show the letter &quot;T&quot; in different states: original, half corrupted by noise, and 20% corrupted by noise. The bottom includes logos and contact information for the Institut Supérieur de l&#39;Automobile et des Transports." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-12</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm103">Hopfield
network</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

This new field, often referred to as the connectionist approach,
emphasized on the importance of learning from data, setting the stage
for the subsequent developments in deep learning, which has since
revolutionized the field of AI by enabling creation of systems which can
perform different tasks by learning complex representations.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec14Heading"}
#### Probabilistic Reasoning {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14Heading .Heading}

In the later part of the last century, research in the AI field began to
embrace soft computing
[methodologies]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm104}, marking a shift
from deterministic models which rely on exact logic to probabilistic
models capable of dealing with ambiguity and uncertainty.
[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB22 .pagebreak aria-label="22"
role="doc-pagebreak"}This shift was driven by acknowledgment of the fact
that real-world problems often involve imprecise incomplete information
requiring a technique that could handle these uncertainties.

Probabilistic reasoning, which uses principles of probability theory to
handle uncertainty in problem-solving, became a keystone in this new
direction. It enabled AI programs to generate the most probable or
approximately correct response when faced with incomplete, uncertain,
and imprecise information, enabling them to solve problems that
traditional AI algorithms could not handle.

In the 1990s and early 2000s, many soft computing models were developed
and put in use, including Bayesian networks, hidden Markov models (HMM),
information theory, and stochastic modelling. [Bayesian
networks]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm105} provided a graphical
model that could represent and reason about uncertain relationships
between variables. [HMMs]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm106} were
particularly useful in temporal pattern recognition, such as speech,
handwriting, or gesture recognition. [Information
theory]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm107} contributed to
understanding and quantifying data entropy and mutual information, while
[stochastic modeling]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm108} offered
ways to predict and analyze systems that exhibit random behavior.

The main objective of these developments was to develop AI systems which
are robust and adaptable and are capable of learning from experience and
make probabilistic inferences. This foundation laid the groundwork for
modern-day AI where probabilistic reasoning is integral in many AI
applications, from natural language processing systems that interpret
human language with a degree of
[uncertainty]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm109} to that of machine
learning algorithms which classify and predict based on likelihoods.
:::

::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec15Heading"}
#### Compute Advancements {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15Heading .Heading}

The expanding AI field led to the growth of both hardware and software
companies to support these AI systems. The period saw significant
initiatives launched to develop computer systems based on [massively
parallel computing
architectures]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm110}. This approach
refers to the use of large number of processors or separate computers
simultaneously to perform computation in parallel.

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_FPar4 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Architecture is similar to modern GPUs which contain hundreds to
thousands of cores for handling concurrent threads.
::::

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB23 .pagebreak aria-label="23"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The evolution of [computing
hardware]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm111} can be categorized into
generations, each marking a significant breakthrough. The first
generation utilized vacuum tubes and was large power-consuming machines
capable of performing basic computations. In the second generation,
transistors and diodes led to smaller more efficient machines. The third
generation saw integrated circuits where hundreds of transistors were
packed on a single chip reducing the size and cost further and improved
performance. The fourth generation saw the rise of microprocessors where
all CPU components were integrated on a single chip. While earlier
generations focused on increasing the number of logic elements within a
single CPU, the fifth generation of computing which was the primary
focus of this period focused on achieving enhanced performance by using
massive numbers of CPUs to work on a problem in a coordinated manner.

The shift toward massively parallel
[computing]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm112} laid the foundation
for multicore processors and specialized hardware such as GPUs (graphic
processing [unit]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm113}) and TPUs
(tensor processing [unit]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm114}) which
are prevalent today. These developments collectively contributed to the
development of the AI we see today by enabling the efficient handling of
complex algorithms and large datasets, which are fundamental for
modern-day AI applications.
:::::
:::::::::::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec16Heading"}
### Big Data {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par102 .Para}
> *Big data fuels the engine of modern AI.*
:::

As we entered the new millennium (2000s), the AI landscape underwent
significant transformation, driven by the explosion of [big
data]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm115}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm116},
continued advancement in computing power, and other key developments.
This section will explore few of these developments that happened during
this period.

The digital revolution and the advent of the internet led to an
exponential increase in the volume of data being generated and stored.
The explosive growth of the internet gave AI programs access to billions
of pages of text and images that could be scraped for analysis. This
proliferation of digital data provided AI systems with a rich source of
training data which is curated and labeled by the users themselves. A
significant development was the ImageNet database in 2009 by Fei-Fei-Li,
which contained millions of labeled images. ImageNet served not only as
a training dataset but also as a benchmark for testing and evaluating
image processing systems leading to significant developments in [image
recognition systems]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm117}.

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB24 .pagebreak aria-label="24"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Another notable development was [Google's
Word2Vec]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm118}. Released in 2013, it
used large amounts of text data scraped from the internet to create
numeric vectors to represent each word, capturing their meanings and
relationships. This laid the foundation for the development of large
language [models]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm119} (LLMs) in the
late 2010s.

Alongside, the shift and advancements in compute which we previously
mentioned provided the relevant computational performance required for
handling the demands of training large models from the large datasets
that were now available, enabling researchers to explore more complex
architectures, including multiple hidden layers in neural networks,
leading to the rise of large deep learning models. This deep
[learning]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm120} was applied to a wide
array of problems, from speech recognition and medical diagnosis to
visual recognition and machine translation over the subsequent years.

The research focus shifted from [Narrow
AI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm121}, which is designed for
specific tasks, to [artificial general intelligence
(AGI)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm122}, which aims to create
systems with the ability to learn, understand, and apply the knowledge
in a variety of contexts. Several labs and companies were founded to
develop AGI including notable entities like DeepMind (later acquired by
Google), OpenAI, and Anthropic.

The initial development of the [Transformer
architecture]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm123} in 2017 laid the
groundwork for subsequent research, leading to the development of large
language [models]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm124} (LLMs). This
period marked a pivotal time in AI history, characterized by models that
are trained on vast amounts of data and set the stage for the current AI
era.

In addition to these developments, we also saw the rise of data storage
and processing frameworks such as
[Hadoop]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm125}, which enabled handling
of big data across distributed networks enhancing the AI systems ability
to learn from larger and complex datasets. Cloud computing platforms,
such as AWS and Microsoft Azure, democratized the access to compute
resources, enabling development of scalable AI applications. The open
source movement gained momentum, enabling a global community of
developers to innovate and contribute to advancements of the AI field by
democratizing access to powerful AI tools and removing the barrier of
expensive proprietary software.

As we proceed, in the final section, we will delve into the architecture
and important milestones of large language models.
::::

:::::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec17Heading"}
### Large Language Models {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB25 .pagebreak aria-label="25"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The Transformer
[architecture]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm126} and the
development of large language models (LLMs) represent a significant
advancement in the AI field specifically in [natural language processing
(NLP)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm127}. These are built upon the
earlier progress in computing power, big data, and neural network
research which we have covered in the previous sections.

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec18Heading"}
#### Transformer Architecture {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18Heading .Heading}

A team of data
[scientists]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm128}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm129}
at Google in 2017 authored a research paper \"Attention is All You
Need,\" introducing the Transformer architecture, which revolutionized
the field of [natural language processing (NLP)
and]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm130} became a cornerstone of
modern AI. Focus of the research at that time was to address the
limitations of [sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq)
models]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm131} used for tasks like
machine translation.

Seq2Seq is an approach for natural language processing which uses two
[recurrent neural networks
(RNNs)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm132}: an encoder and a
decoder. The encoder processes an input sentence of tokens (words or
characters) (x0, x1...) and maps it into a fixed size numerical vector
representing the entire sequence's meaning. The decoder then takes this
vector and maps it back to produce an output sequence of tokens in the
target language.

A major limitation of this approach is the fixed size vector. If the
input sentence is long, the fixed size vector would not be able to
encode all relevant information, leading to information bottleneck. The
longer the input, the more difficult it is for the encoder to capture
all the relevant information in a single vector, leading to degradation
of the output quality. In addition, earlier RNNs processed sequences
sequentially, operating on one token at a time from first to last, which
can be slow and inefficient. So, the focus of the research was to
process all tokens in parallel.

The Google paper introduced a new deep learning architecture known as
Transformer which is a multihead attention model. The attention
mechanism is a method to weigh the importance of different tokens in the
sequence relative to each other regardless of the position and
determines the relative importance. The process is done through multiple
independent heads enabling processing of all tokens in parallel and
allowing the model to capture relationships between tokens
simultaneously.

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB26 .pagebreak aria-label="26"
role="doc-pagebreak"}This transformer architecture has found many
applications since. It led to the development of pretrained models like
[Generative Pretrained Transformers
(GPTs)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm133} and [Bidirectional
Encoder Representation from Transformers
(BERT)]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm134} by Google. These were
foundational
[models]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm135}[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm136}
trained on massive corpus of text data. Next, we will look at the
timeline of the model releases.
:::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec19Heading"}
#### Timeline of LLM Models {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19Heading .Heading}

[OpenAI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm137}
[published]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm138} a research paper
"Improving Language understanding by Generative Pretraining" in 2016
which introduced the [concept]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm139} of
a Generative Pretrained Transformer (GPT). This laid the architectural
foundation for its early language models GPT-1 and GPT-2.

GPT-1, the initial Generative Pretrained Transformer language model
released in 2018, was trained on 117 million parameters and had the
capacity to generate a logical context-appropriate response for a
specific prompt.

:::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_FPar5 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

Parameters are components of models that are adjusted during the
training process to minimize the difference between the predicted and
the actual output. Parameters in this context are the weights and biases
of the neural network.
::::

GPT-2, released in February 2019 with 1.5 billion parameters, had a
strong language generation capability.

In June 2020, OpenAI launched GPT-3 large language model with 175
billion parameters. This marked a significant milestone in the natural
language processing field with its ability to generate human-like text.

In 2021, [OpenAI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm140} introduced
Dall-E, a text-to-image model leading to emergence of multimodal AI
systems. Dall-E enabled users to prompt in natural language text, and it
responds by generating images. The first iteration of DALL-E was trained
on 12 billion parameters.

In 2022, OpenAI published research on
[InstructGPT]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm141}, which showed
improved instruction following ability with decrease in toxicity and
hallucination in the output generated.

In November 2022, AI chatbot
[ChatGPT]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm142} based on GPT 3.5 was
released by OpenAI. This release democratized AI by providing an
intuitive interface for interacting with large language models,
generating contextually relevant responses in numerous
[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB27 .pagebreak aria-label="27"
role="doc-pagebreak"}languages, leading to rapid adoption. Within 2
months of its launch, ChatGPT reported 100 million monthly active users
and became the fastest growing application in history.

In March 2023, OpenAI launched the APIs for ChatGPT allowing users to
incorporate the ChatGPT functionality into their applications. In the
same month, GPT-4 was launched with the capability of producing safer
and more relevant responses.

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Par127 .Para}
In April 2023, data controls were introduced by
[OpenAI]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm143}, providing users with
privacy option to choose which conversation to use as training data for
subsequent GPT releases. In September 2023, OpenAI integrated DALL-E 3
into ChatGPT. All these advancements further expanded the applicability
and accessibility of these models. Microsoft integrated ChatGPT into its
search engine Bing and Google released its chatbot Bard. Major
development in large language models followed. Figure
[[1-13](#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Fig13)]{.InternalRef} shows a
timeline of the major developments post launch of ChatGPT.

<figure id="Fig13" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_MO14" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_1_Chapter/641294_1_En_1_Fig13_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Timeline of major large language model (LLM) developments from November 2022 to April 2023. Key events include: 1. Nov 30, 2022: OpenAI&#39;s ChatGPT-3.5 becomes widely used, gaining 100 million users in 2 months. 2. Dec 12: Cohere releases a multilingual LLM. 3. Dec 26: Google&#39;s Med-PaLM targets specific use cases. 4. Feb 2, 2023: Amazon&#39;s model uses &quot;chain-of-thought prompting.&quot; 5. Feb 24: Meta&#39;s LLaMA is efficient for certain tasks. 6. Feb 27: Microsoft introduces Kosmos-1, a multimodal LLM. 7. Mar 7: Salesforce announces Einstein GPT for customer management. 8. Mar 13: OpenAI releases GPT-4 with improved accuracy. 9. Mar 14: Anthropic introduces Claude with &quot;constitutional AI.&quot; 10. Mar 16: Microsoft integrates GPT-4 into Office 365. 11. Mar 21: Google releases Bard, an AI chatbot. 12. Mar 30: Bloomberg announces a financial data-trained LLM. 13. Apr 13: Amazon announces Bedrock, a managed service for LLMs." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 1-13</span>
<p>Major <span
id="641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm144">developments</span>
timeline</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

[]{#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_PB28 .pagebreak aria-label="28"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The ongoing evolution of these technologies
continues to shape the future of AI, with improved architectures and
intuitive interfaces driving widespread adoption across industries and
applications.
::::::
::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::: {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec20Heading"}
## Conclusion {#641294_1_En_1_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20Heading .Heading}

In this chapter, we explored the journey of artificial intelligence (AI)
from its early days of rule-based systems to the present-day large
language models (LLMs). The journey reflects not only the technological
advancements but also the limitless possibility that lie ahead in the
realm of AI. The LLMs are not only changing our understanding of AI's
potential but are also set to revolutionize the way we interact with
AI-enabled systems. Its application extends across sectors and
industries. From assisting in disease diagnosis and parsing medical
records in healthcare to that of personalizing learning in education
sector, LLMs hold significant promise. As the capability of AI
technology continues to evolve and advance, so will its applications. In
the next chapter, we will cover applications of the technology.
:::

::: MainTitleSection
# 2. Generative AI in Business Unlocking Value {#641294_1_En_2_Chapter.xhtml_chaptertitle .ChapterTitle lang="en"}
:::
