:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: Fulltext
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB55 .pagebreak aria-label="55"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Building enterprise-wide GenAI applications
requires a balanced
approach[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}AI systems must be
scalable, modular, and performant while also being ethical, secure, and
compliant. In the previous chapter, we covered the applications of
generative AI across industries and sectors. This chapter delves into
the design patterns required for building enterprise-grade generative AI
solutions.

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par3 .Para}
Building an enterprise-wide application requires a balanced approach
between scalability, performance and security, and ethics. Both are
interdependent as an AI system that scales but lacks security can expose
sensitive data while a secure AI system that cannot scale will fail
under real-life demands. By the end of this chapter, readers will

::: UnorderedList
- Grasp the fundamental concept of design patterns and understand their
  importance in creating efficient, maintainable, reusable, and scalable
  Generative AI applications.

- Identify and categorize various design patterns used throughout the
  generative AI life cycle, from data handling to model deployment and
  user interaction.

- Adopt best practices for implementing design patterns in generative AI
  solutions, ensuring high performance, scalability, and long-term
  sustainability.

- []{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB56 .pagebreak aria-label="56"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}Understand how design patterns address
  scalability and performance challenges, providing strategies to manage
  growing AI workloads efficiently.

- Navigate the complexities of security, compliance, and ethical AI
  considerations, and learn how design patterns can help mitigate risks
  related to privacy, bias, and regulatory adherence in generative AI
  systems.
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec1Heading"}
## Introduction {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par9 .Para}
> *Design patterns are reusable solutions to common problems in software
> design, providing a structured approach to solving recurring issues,
> offering reusable templates that can be applied across various
> scenarios.*
:::

In the ever-evolving world of [software
development]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm1}, maintaining a
high-quality code is crucial. To achieve this, developers rely on best
practices that are defined and continue to be refined over time; these
practices are built into design patterns, which serve as a foundational
framework for writing software that is reusable, configurable, readable,
scalable, and maintainable for a longer period.

[Design
patterns]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm2}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm3}
create a common standard, enabling developers to understand and
implement the code easily. Also, it helps them communicate effectively
among themselves symbolically, thus improving collaboration and
consistency.

[Software development]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm4} remains no
longer a "local" team development confined to a single team. Design
patterns help standardize the way it develops across the developer
community worldwide. Design patterns should be seen as a guiding
framework to help develop robust, scalable, and efficient software
solutions effectively. Design patterns help eliminate "local" or
idiosyncratic coding [practices]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm5} by
offering a standardized approach to solving common software problems.
They help develop shared vocabulary for developers, which surpasses
local coding practices.

When terms like "Singleton," "Factory," or "Observer" are used,
developers worldwide easily understand the approach taken to solve the
problem, reducing the need to understand individual coding styles and
approach, enabling better understanding and collaboration.
::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec2Heading"}
## Design Patterns in Enterprise Generative AI Applications {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB57 .pagebreak aria-label="57"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Like traditional software applications, developing
"enterprise-wide generative AI applications" also requires a guiding
framework which ensures high-quality, reusable, scalable, maintainable,
and configurable generative AI solutions.

Applications built using well-chosen design patterns (as shown in Figure
[[3-1](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Fig1)]{.InternalRef} below) are more
adaptable to future requirements and technological changes.
[Debugging]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm6} also becomes easier
because patterns impose a logical structure on the software, making
potential problem areas more predictable.

<figure id="Fig1" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_MO1" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_3_Chapter/641294_1_En_3_Fig1_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Flow chart illustrating the generative AI lifecycle phases and their corresponding design patterns. Central green circle labeled &quot;Generative AI Lifecycle Phase &amp; Mapping to Relevant Design Patterns&quot; connects to six blue circles: &quot;Data Processing,&quot; &quot;Model Training,&quot; &quot;Model Testing,&quot; &quot;Model Deployment,&quot; &quot;Model Monitoring,&quot; and &quot;User Interaction.&quot; Each phase links to a box listing relevant design patterns. &quot;Data Processing&quot; includes Pipeline, Batch, Streaming. &quot;Model Training&quot; includes Model Factory, Microservice, Federated Learning. &quot;Model Testing&quot; includes A/B, Shadow, Canary. &quot;Model Deployment&quot; includes Blue-Green, Canary, Microservices. &quot;Model Monitoring&quot; includes Feedback Loop, Monitoring, Drift Detection. &quot;User Interaction&quot; includes Prompt Template, Chain of Thought, -Shot Prompting. Arrows indicate the flow between phases." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 3-1</span>
<p>GenAI life cycle phase mapped with relevant design <span
id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm7">patterns</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec3Heading"}
### Key Building Blocks {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par17 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB58 .pagebreak aria-label="58"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Enterprise generative AI
[solutions]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm8} comprise of multiple
key components (as shown in Figure
[[3-2](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Fig2)]{.InternalRef} below),
including

::: UnorderedList
- Data processing

- Model training

- Model testing

- Model deployment

- Model monitoring
:::
::::

<figure id="Fig2" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_MO2" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_3_Chapter/641294_1_En_3_Fig2_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle14"
alt="Flow chart in the shape of a gear illustrating the process of building blocks in a machine learning workflow. The steps are numbered sequentially: 1) Data Processing, 2) Model Training, 3) Model Testing, 4) Model Deployment, and 5) Model Monitoring. Each step is positioned around the gear, with &quot;Building Blocks&quot; labeled at the center." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 3-2</span>
<p>Building blocks of AI/GenAI <span
id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm9">solutions</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB59 .pagebreak aria-label="59"
role="doc-pagebreak"}If the right design patterns are not applied at
each of these stages, then systems may encounter more instances of
failures or "gaps." Design patterns address these issues that may not be
immediately evident, in particular to less experienced developers. They
help prevent [subtle]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm10} issues that
can lead to major failures in AI application development.
:::::

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec4Heading"}
### Optimizing Pipeline with Design Patterns {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4Heading .Heading}

AI-driven solutions often require
[extensive]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm11} data pipelines, which
include processes like data collection, cleaning, harmonization,
transformation, feature extraction, and many others. Developers must
also decide whether data should be streamed or processed in batches and
handle different modalities in the data such as audio, video, and text.
Failing to implement best practices to handle each of these leads to
higher chances of the system failing at the data ingestion layer itself,
making it difficult to adapt/scale to future needs.

The "**Pipeline Pattern**"
[addresses]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm12} these challenges by
organizing the data ingestion
[pipeline]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm13} into a structured,
sequential workflow, ensuring efficient, reliable, and scalable data
processing.
:::

::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec5Heading"}
### Model Scalability and Maintainability {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5Heading .Heading}

Another common problem in AI-driven
[applications]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm14} is model
scalability and maintainability in the production environment. AI models
in production need to handle increasing workloads, frequent updates, and
seamless integration with other systems.

The "**Model Factory Pattern**"
[helps]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15} automate the creation and
deployment of multiple model versions, ensuring consistency and reducing
maintenance efforts.

For flexibility and scalability, the "**Microservice Architecture**"
[pattern]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16} is particularly useful.
It decomposes the AI systems into smaller, independently deployable
units, enabling the system to be scaled dynamically up or down in real
time.

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par29 .Para}
For example, consider a customer service chatbot used by a
telecommunications company. The pipeline might be organized using these
patterns as follows:

::: UnorderedList
- **Model Factory Pattern**: Trains
  [multiple]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17} intent recognition
  models (for different languages or regions), packages them into
  containers, and deploys them automatically through a CI/CD workflow.

- :::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par31 .Para}
  **Microservice Architecture**:
  [Breaks]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18} down the chatbot
  pipeline into modular services:[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB60
  .pagebreak aria-label="60" role="doc-pagebreak"}

  ::: UnorderedList
  - A data ingestion microservice that collects conversation logs.

  - A preprocessing microservice that cleans and tokenizes input.

  - A model inference microservice that uses the latest deployed intent
    classification model.

  - A feedback loop microservice that captures customer ratings to feed
    into retraining.
  :::
  ::::
:::
::::

Each of these services can be
[scaled]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19} and updated
independently[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}for instance,
deploying a newer version of the inference model without touching the
ingestion service.
:::::

::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec6Heading"}
### AI Testing and Reliability {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par37 .Para}
Testing of AI applications can be
[challenging]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20} due to the
probabilistic nature of predictions. Design patterns, such as "**A/B
Testing Pattern**" and "**Shadow Testing Pattern**," ensure reliability
by providing structured testing methodologies.

::: UnorderedList
- **A/B testing pattern** [helps]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21}
  compare performance of two model versions under real-world conditions,
  helping the team choose the most effective model.

- **Shadow testing pattern**
  [allows]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22} a new model to run
  alongside the production model without impacting users, enabling safe
  validation before full deployment.
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par40 .Para}
Table [[3-1](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab1)]{.InternalRef} shows
concise comparison table between A/B testing and shadow testing.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab1 .Table}
  Feature                     A/B testing                                                     Shadow testing
  --------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------
  Purpose                     Compare two or more model versions in live environments         Test new model silently alongside the current model
  Exposure to user            Users are split across different model versions (e.g., 50/50)   Only the current model's output is shown to users
  Deployment considerations   Requires load balancing and routing for real-time testing       Requires dual inference pipelines
  Common usage                UI improvements, content ranking models                         Upgrading language models, compliance-sensitive systems

  : [Table 3-1]{.CaptionNumber}\
  [A/B
  testing]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24}
  vs. shadow testing {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::
:::::::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
### Security and Privacy in AI Solutions {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB61 .pagebreak aria-label="61"
role="doc-pagebreak"}AI solutions often deal with sensitive
[data]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25} that must be protected from
any adversarial attacks or privacy breaches. The "**Federated Learning
Pattern**" [enables]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm26} training
models without transferring raw data outside predefined boundaries,
ensuring data privacy and security.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par42 .Para}
In Figure [[3-3](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Fig3)]{.InternalRef},
there is an anchor/central region and many other peripheral regions.
Each of these regions has their own model trained on their regional
data. Only the updated/deployed version is shared across the regions via
anchor/central solution. There will be a great role played by code
branching and merging policy in this kind of ecosystem. Suppose the
anchor region creates a base model which is shared with all other
peripheral regions. These regions train this model copy on their
respective regional data and make some important updates. They push the
updated code or could even push the revised model directly, which will
be accepted/rejected by the central region. After precise validation,
the updated code/model by peripheral regions is accepted by the central
region which then notifies other regions of an updated version of the
model available which they may accept and further train on their
respective regional data. In this whole process, regional data is never
shared. Only model updates are shared.

<figure id="Fig3" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_MO3" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_3_Chapter/641294_1_En_3_Fig3_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Diagram illustrating a federated learning system with multiple data pipelines. Region A, B, and C each have their own data pipelines, consisting of stages like data validation, processing, training, evaluation, and deployment. These regions share deployed models with a central region data pipeline. The central region acts as a hub for model updates, which are shared back to the regions. Key points of federated learning are listed, emphasizing local training, central model updates, no inter-region data sharing, and a notification-based ecosystem." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 3-3</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm27">Federated
learning</span> across regions anchored by central region</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

There is another pattern known as "**Differential Privacy**"
[which]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm28} is

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par44 .Para}
::: UnorderedList
- []{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB62 .pagebreak aria-label="62"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}A method of sharing data that describes patterns
  in a dataset while hiding personally identifiable information.

- It mathematically guarantees that anyone seeing the result of a
  differentially private analysis will essentially make the same
  inference about any individual's private information, whether that
  individual's private information is included in the input to the
  analysis.

- With differential privacy, it is important to identify which
  information is general and which is private so as to benefit from this
  mechanism and reduce any harm. Differential privacy guarantees to
  protect only private information. So, if one's secret is general
  information, it will not be protected!

- An algorithm is said to be differentially private if by looking at the
  outcome statistics, one cannot tell which individual was included in
  the dataset. This is depicted in Figure
  [[3-4](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Fig4)]{.InternalRef}.
:::

<figure id="Fig4" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_MO4" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_3_Chapter/641294_1_En_3_Fig4_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle15"
alt="Flow chart illustrating a data analysis process. The top section shows a database labeled &quot;D&quot; leading to &quot;Output (A)&quot; through an &quot;Algorithm/Analysis&quot; step. The bottom section adds &quot;PII Data&quot; to &quot;D&quot; resulting in &quot;D&#39;&quot; and &quot;Output (B)&quot; through the same analysis step. A note indicates that outputs A and B are similar." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 3-4</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm29">Differential
privacy</span> as a pattern</p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
::::
::::::

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
### Handling Model and Data Drifts {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB63 .pagebreak aria-label="63"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Yet another common
[challenge]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm30} faced with AI
applications is handling models and data drifts which leads to a
decrease in effectiveness of the model over a period.

The "**Monitoring & Feedback Loop Pattern**"
[continuously]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31} tracks model
performance and triggers retraining pipelines whenever data or concept
drifts are detected.
:::

::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
### Model Deployments {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par51 .Para}
Deploying models into [production]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32}
and managing multiple versions is another critical challenge in AI
application development. The two most common model deployment and
version control patterns are

::: UnorderedList
- ::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par52 .Para}
  "**Blue-Green Deployment Pattern**": A
  [new]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm33} model (blue) is deployed
  alongside an existing one (green) to ensure zero downtime and smooth
  rollbacks in case of issues, depicted in Figure
  [[3-5](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Fig5)]{.InternalRef}.
  <figure id="Fig5" class="Figure">
  <div id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_MO5" class="MediaObject">
  <img src="images/641294_1_En_3_Chapter/641294_1_En_3_Fig5_HTML.jpg"
  class="ImageStyle16"
  alt="Diagram illustrating a flow chart for deploying a model or app. A user icon connects to a load balancer, which distributes requests to two instances of a model or app. The top instance is labeled &quot;App/Model in Production&quot; and connects to a database. The bottom instance is labeled &quot;New Version of Model/App&quot; and also connects to the database." />
  </div>
  <figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
  <span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 3-5</span>
  <p><span id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm34">Blue-Green Deployment
  strategy</span></p>
  </div></figcaption>
  </figure>
  :::

- "**Canary Deployment Pattern**": The
  [new]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm35} model is gradually
  released to a subset of users, allowing real-world
  [testing]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm36} before full scale
  deployment, minimizing risks, depicted in Figure
  [[3-6](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Fig6)]{.InternalRef}.
:::

<figure id="Fig6" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_MO6" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_3_Chapter/641294_1_En_3_Fig6_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle17"
alt="Diagram illustrating a flow chart for user groups interacting with a system. Two user groups are shown, each connecting to a load balancer. The load balancer directs traffic to two models or applications: one in production and a new version. Both models connect to a database. The production model is labeled &quot;App/Model in Production,&quot; and the new version is labeled &quot;New Version of the Model/App.&quot;" />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 3-6</span>
<p>Canary Deployment <span
id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37">strategy</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
::::
:::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
### Orchestration {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par54 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB64 .pagebreak aria-label="64"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Complex AI applications often
[involve]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38} more than one model.
Right orchestration and composition are crucial for efficiency and
accuracy.

::: UnorderedList
- The "**Orchestrator Pattern**" manages interaction between multiple
  models working together, ensuring smooth operation of "multiagent
  systems," "ensemble systems," or "hybrid systems."

- The "**Composite Pattern**" enables combining predictions from
  multiple models into a single cohesive output, often used in ensemble
  modeling techniques.

- For these systems, it's also important that models are optimized in
  size in order to reduce computational cost without impacting
  performance. The "**Model Compression Pattern**"
  [applies]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39} techniques such as
  [pruning]{.EmphasisTypeSmallCaps},
  [quantization]{.EmphasisTypeSmallCaps}, [and knowledge
  distillation]{.EmphasisTypeSmallCaps} to reduce model size and
  computation [requirements]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40}
  without significant loss in accuracy.
:::
::::
:::::

:::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
### Prompt Engineering {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par58 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB65 .pagebreak aria-label="65"
role="doc-pagebreak"}There are no formalized design
[patterns]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42}
specifically for "*Prompt Engineering*" in generative AI or "agentic AI"
as a structured pattern. However, existing design patterns can be
adapted to the unique challenges of generative AI. Few examples are
listed below. Deciding which prompt structure to use could be guided
through the decision tree shown below in Figure
[[3-7](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Fig7)]{.InternalRef} below. Also
more such guided questions are mentioned in Table
[[3-2](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab2)]{.InternalRef}.

::: UnorderedList
- The "**Strategy Pattern**" can be applied to prompt engineering by
  defining a family of prompt strategies, making them interchangeable
  within the context of generating outputs from a model. This allows for
  dynamic selection of prompt strategies based on the desired outcomes.
  This pattern could easily be extended to algorithms or models as well
  to switch between them dynamically based on requirements.

- The "**Template Pattern**" could define the skeleton of the prompt
  generation process with predefined steps and customizable elements.

- The "**Builder Pattern**" might be used to construct complex prompts
  by specifying and assembling various parts like context, instructions,
  and examples step by step.
:::
::::

Since generative AI models, such as GPT, depend heavily on prompts to
produce results, clearly specifying tasks and instructions while
designing
[prompts]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm43}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44}
is essential to generate relevant and accurate results. Poorly designed
prompts can lead to vague, irrelevant, or inaccurate outcomes.

<figure id="Fig7" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_MO7" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_3_Chapter/641294_1_En_3_Fig7_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle18"
alt="Flow chart illustrating the decision-making process for selecting a prompt structure. It begins with the question, &quot;What type of prompt structure needed?&quot; leading to three options: &quot;One of several prompting strategies?&quot; directing to &quot;Use Strategy Pattern,&quot; &quot;Reusable prompt structure template?&quot; leading to &quot;Use Template Pattern,&quot; and &quot;Step-by-step custom prompt build?&quot; pointing to &quot;Use Builder Pattern.&quot; Each option is connected by arrows, showing the flow of decision-making." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 3-7</span>
<p>Decision tree: choosing a prompt engineering <span
id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm45">pattern</span><span
id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm46"></span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par63 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB66 .pagebreak aria-label="66"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Here is an example of simple vs. dynamic prompting.

:::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PC1 .ProgramCode}
::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
**Simple prompt example:**
:::

::: FixedLine
**(python)**
:::
:::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
prompt = \"You are an assistant helping with customer sentiment
analysis. Analyze the following review: Delivery was quick, but the
product quality is poor. Not satisfied at all.\"
:::

::: FixedLine
**Dynamic prompt example:**
:::

::: FixedLine
**(python)**
:::
::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
user_input = \"Review: Great service but the food was cold.\"
dynamic_prompt = f\"\"\" You are a restaurant feedback analyser. Analyze
the following feedback: {user_input}. Provide sentiment
(Positive/Negative) and key actionable insights. \"\"\"
:::
::::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par64 .Para}
In our subsequent chapters, we will cover prompt
[designing]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48}
and engineering in detail.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab2 .Table}
  Question                                                                  Preferable pattern
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------
  Do you have multiple prompt strategies that should be interchangeable?    Strategy Pattern
  Do you want to switch prompt behavior based on user intent or context?    Strategy Pattern
  Are you repeatedly using the same prompt structure with minor tweaks?     Template Pattern
  Do you need a standard format with placeholders for dynamic content?      Template Pattern
  Is your prompt composed of multiple sections (context, examples, etc.)?   Builder Pattern
  Do you want to construct prompts step by step for modularity?             Builder Pattern

  : [Table 3-2]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Checklist for choosing different [prompt
  engineering]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50}
  patterns {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}
### Design Patterns for Agentic AI {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12Heading .Heading}

Agentic AI
[refers]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52}
to AI systems that can act autonomously and make decisions without human
intervention. While "**agentic AI**" itself is not a design pattern, the
development of such systems could benefit from several well-established
design patterns, particularly []{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB67
.pagebreak aria-label="67" role="doc-pagebreak"}those that support
modularity, decision-making, and autonomous behavior. Key design
patterns applicable to Agentic AI include

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par66 .Para}
::: UnorderedList
- "**Observer Pattern"**: The "Observer Pattern" enables an AI agent to
  monitor changes in its environment and react to it accordingly. This
  pattern is crucial for real-time decision-making in dynamic
  environments.

- "**State Pattern**": The "State Pattern" enables an object to alter
  its behavior when its internal state changes. State management is a
  critical component in any multiagent system ensuring smooth
  transitions between different operational modes.

- "**Chain of Responsibility Pattern**": The "Chain of Responsibility
  Pattern" patterns allow to pass requests along a chain of handlers
  where each handler processes information or makes decisions based on
  predefined criteria. In agentic AI, this pattern can be used to build
  a structured decision-making pipeline ensuring adaptability and
  modularity.
:::
::::

In the subsequent chapters, we will explore ways in which these design
patterns
[work]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54}
in combination in practical use cases.
:::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec13Heading"}
## Categorized View {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par71 .Para}
In the previous section, we explained different design patterns. In this
section, we will organize everything we discussed into structured
categories. Organizing design patterns into categories based on their
purpose helps provide clarity and structure, making it easier to apply
them in specific phases of developing generative AI solutions. Table
[[3-3](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab3)]{.InternalRef} showcases the
[categorized]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55} view.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab3 .Table}
  Category             Key patterns
  -------------------- ----------------------------------------------------
  Data handling        Pipeline, Batch, and Streaming Pattern
  Model training       Model Factory Pattern, Microservices Pattern
  Model deployment     Blue-Green Deployment, Canary Deployment Pattern
  Model monitoring     Monitoring & Feedback Loop Pattern
  Multiagent systems   Orchestrator, Composite, Strategy, Builder Pattern
  Optimization         Model Compression Pattern
  Safety/security      Federated learning
  Testing              A/B testing, shadow testing pattern

  : [Table 3-3]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Selective patterns across generative AI life
  [cycle]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56} {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par72 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB68 .pagebreak aria-label="68"
role="doc-pagebreak"}It's important to note that not all the concepts we
discuss fall strictly under the definition of design patterns in the
traditional sense. Instead, they represent a broader framework
comprising of

::: UnorderedList
- Design patterns, e.g., Observer and Strategy

- Best practices and strategies, e.g., ensuring modularity in AI
  applications and prompt creation strategies

- Architectural principles, e.g., multiagent system orchestration
:::
::::

By integrating these, we lay the foundation for developing enterprise
GenAI solutions which will be scalable, reusable, and adaptable to
future advancements.

::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec14Heading"}
### Beyond Design Patterns: Best Practices for Generative AI {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14Heading .Heading}

To create impactful, reliable, and scalable generative AI systems,
following [proven]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57} strategies/best
practices is as important as selecting the right design patterns. While
design patterns help structure the AI system, these best practices
unlock value across every stage of your AI journey, ensuring
performance, fairness, user satisfaction, security, and long-term
adaptation. In the previous sections, we discussed design patterns. In
this section, we will list a set of best practices that work alongside
the design patterns to ensure the best designed GenAI systems.

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par78 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB69 .pagebreak aria-label="69"
role="doc-pagebreak"}To set the stage, Table
[[3-4](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab4)]{.InternalRef} presents
mapping between common challenges in generative AI to the best practices
that directly address them---highlighting the practical value of each
recommendation.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab4 .Table}
  Best practice                     Key GenAI challenges addressed
  --------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------
  Prioritize data quality           Poor output quality, hallucinations, model underperformance
  Handling bias                     Ethical concerns, fairness, discrimination, trust
  Preserving privacy by design      Data misuse, compliance violations, user distrust
  Build modular, flexible systems   Scalability, maintenance complexity, integration issues
  Effective prompt engineering      Misinterpretation of user inputs, poor context resolution
  Monitor, iterate, and improve     Model drift, latency, lack of continuous learning
  Ethical considerations            Responsible use, lack of transparency, user skepticism
  Design for scale and speed        Performance bottlenecks, traffic spikes, latency issues

  : [Table 3-4]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Best practices vs. key GenAI challenge it
  [addresses]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58} {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

Let us delve into brief details of each of them below.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec15Heading"}
#### Prioritize Data Quality {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15Heading .Heading}

Generative AI thrives on data quality. The higher the data quality, the
better the results are. Clean, diverse, and representative datasets are
the source of powerful models. Implement version control for datasets
and verify inputs rigorously to eliminate noise and inconsistencies.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec16Heading"}
#### Handling Bias {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16Heading .Heading}

Conduct bias audits, balance datasets to reflect diversity, and enforce
fairness constraints during training. Leverage tools like SHAP or LIME
are used for transparency and to uncover hidden biases.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec17Heading"}
#### Preserving Privacy by Design {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec17Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB70 .pagebreak aria-label="70"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Incorporate privacy-preserving methods such as data
anonymization, federated learning, and differential privacy. Keep
sensitive data local and secure with robust encryption and minimal
exposure.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec18Heading"}
#### Build Modular, Flexible Systems {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec18Heading .Heading}

Design AI systems as modular pipelines, separating data preprocessing,
model training, and implementation. Modular architectures simplify
scaling, maintenance, and adaptation to evolving requirements.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec19Heading"}
#### Effective Prompt Engineering {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec19Heading .Heading}

The development of effective prompts is as crucial as designing
algorithms. Establish standards for [prompt
engineering]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59} to handle ambiguous
inputs and edge cases with precision.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec20Heading"}
#### Monitor, Iterate, and Improve {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec20Heading .Heading}

Establish real-time monitoring for output relevance and latency. Collect
user feedback to improve models and build pipelines for continuous
learning and performance upgrades.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec21Heading"}
#### Ethical Considerations {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec21Heading .Heading}

Embed responsible AI principles at every stage. Evaluate misuse
scenarios, disclose AI usage to users, and ensure transparent decisions
with interpretable models.
:::

::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec22Heading"}
#### Design for Scale and Speed {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec22Heading .Heading}

Use containerized deployments and load testing to handle spikes in
demand. Employ caching and efficient serving frameworks to deliver
lightning-fast responses.

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par88 .Para}
Each of these practices transforms complexity into clarity, ensuring the
generative AI systems are not just functional but truly impactful.
Embedding these strategies to work alongside design patterns ensures the
AI applications are ethical, impactful, and ready for the future. Table
[[3-5](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab5)]{.InternalRef} summarizes the
best practices for each stage of the generative AI development life
cycle.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab5 .Table}
  Category                                                             Best practices
  -------------------------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------
  Ensuring data quality                                                Data validation
                                                                       Data cleaning
                                                                       Data augmentation
                                                                       Feature engineering
  Handling bias                                                        Diverse datasets
                                                                       Bias detection and mitigation
                                                                       Continuous monitoring
  Preserving privacy                                                   Data anonymization
                                                                       Differential privacy
                                                                       RBAC
                                                                       Data governance
  Model training and deployment                                        Version control
                                                                       Containerization
                                                                       CI/CD pipeline
                                                                       Scalability
  Monitoring and [maintenance]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61}   Performance metrics
                                                                       Logging and auditing
                                                                       Model updating
                                                                       Feedback loops
  Ethical considerations                                               Transparency
                                                                       Consent and regulatory adherence

  : [Table 3-5]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Best practices across generative AI life
  [cycle]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60} {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::
:::::
:::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec23 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec23Heading"}
## Scalability and Performance {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec23Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB72 .pagebreak aria-label="72"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In the previous sections, we covered design
[patterns]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm62} and best practices that
help develop modular, reusable, and well-structured AI systems. However,
in addition to being well structured, enterprise-grade generative AI
applications must be able to perform reliably and efficiently under
heavy load and scale seamlessly with increasing demands.

Scalability is not an afterthought; it's a fundamental requirement. A
system that cannot scale fails under high demands, suffers latency
issues, or becomes too costly to maintain. Each design pattern addresses
scalability and performance in their unique way. Let's review a few of
them.

:::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec24 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec24Heading"}
### Observer Pattern {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec24Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par91 .Para}
> *Implements event driven updates, reducing unnecessary computation and
> improving real time responsiveness.*
:::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par93 .Para}
The [Observer Pattern]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm63} is built
upon the ***publisher-subscriber***
[principle]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm64}.

::: UnorderedList
- The subscribers are also known as
  "subjects"[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}the object that
  is being observed. The subject maintains a list of observers that are
  interested in its state and notifies all of them whenever its state
  changes.

- The subject must provide methods to add or remove observers from the
  list.

- On the other hand, the "observer" implements an update method that is
  called by the subject when its state changes and takes appropriate
  actions based on the received state update.
:::
::::

A single subject can have multiple observers listening to its state and
is loosely coupled, i.e., a subject doesn't need to know the specific
implementation details of its observer or what action it takes upon
receiving the subjects' state change notification. Decoupling the
subject and observer ensures the system handles dynamic changes in
components without requiring a full redesign.

Further, instead of constant
[polling]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm65}, notifications are sent
only when necessary. Event occurs, reducing computational usage and
overhead.
::::::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec25 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec25Heading"}
### Strategy Pattern {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec25Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par99 .Para}
> *Dynamically switches between lightweight and heavyweight models,
> optimizing resource usage and performance based on varying load.*
:::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par101 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB73 .pagebreak aria-label="73"
role="doc-pagebreak"}It defines *multiple interchangeable strategies
between algorithms/models* that can be swapped based on context.

::: UnorderedList
- AI [systems]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm66} can switch
  depending on the need and analysis of the infrastructure requirements
  for the environment between advanced heavy-weight models and
  lightweight models and vice versa ensuring performance is optimized as
  per the available resources.

- For example, a student model (compressed version of a full-fledged
  model) can be used for quick predictions while the more advanced full
  model is reserved for high-accuracy scenarios.
:::
::::
::::::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec26 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec26Heading"}
### Federated Learning Pattern {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec26Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par104 .Para}
> *Distributes training across decentralized devices, reducing
> infrastructure strain and improving data privacy.*
:::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par106 .Para}
[Federated learning]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm67} is a
*decentralized approach to training machine learning models*.

::: UnorderedList
- It involves multiple entities working together to train a shared model
  while keeping their data local.

- Although a lot of synchronization and collaboration is required to
  implement such systems, it helps immensely in maintaining data
  privacy.

- Training across decentralized devices reduces central infrastructure
  strain and enables horizontal scaling by distributing workloads to
  edge devices.

- Performance is also improved as local training minimized data
  transfer, reducing latency and improving real-time performance in
  distributed systems.
:::
::::
::::::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec27 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec27Heading"}
### Ensemble Pattern {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec27Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par111 .Para}
> *Runs multiple models in parallel, combining their strengths for
> faster and more accurate decision-making.*
:::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par113 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB74 .pagebreak aria-label="74"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Many AI/ML implementations may require using
*ensemble of models to arrive at a final solution*.

::: UnorderedList
- In this [pattern]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm68}, multiple
  model outputs are combined to produce a more accurate result.

- For example, data deduplication process using AI/ML must include exact
  matches, distance-based matches, embedding matches, etc.

- Depending solely on one of these might not help with accurate final
  similarity score. Hence, scores from each of these are ensembled
  together by their relative importance, and then a final score is
  determined.

- All these intermediate modeling approaches must run in parallel as far
  as possible to improve fault tolerance and manage increased demand.
  [Aggregating]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm69} results from
  specialized models ensures robust and faster decision-making compared
  to a single monolithic model.
:::
::::
::::::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec28 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec28Heading"}
### Microservice Pattern {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec28Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par118 .Para}
> *Breaks down monolithic AI systems into modular services, allowing
> independent scaling of critical components.*
:::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par120 .Para}
A [monolithic AI
system]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm70}[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm71}
could be broken down into *smaller, independently deployable
services/modules.*

::: UnorderedList
- This pattern allows horizontal scaling for specific components,
  matching workload demands.

- Each component communicates via APIs.

- This distributed deployment minimizes latency for the overall AI
  system.

- Each service can scale independently, improving resource allocation
  and helping in reducing latency.

- []{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB75 .pagebreak aria-label="75"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}Example, AI chatbot leveraging separate
  microservices for language processing, response
  [generation]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm72}, sentiment
  analysis, and intent recognition.
:::
::::
::::::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec29 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec29Heading"}
### Agentic AI Pattern {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec29Heading .Heading}

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par126 .Para}
> *Uses multi-agent collaboration, enabling efficient task distribution
> and improved adaptability in complex AI workflows.*
:::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par128 .Para}
A multiagent [system]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm73} architecture
employs *multiple independent agents collaborating to solve complex
tasks*.

::: UnorderedList
- Unlike single-agent systems, where a single entity handles everything,
  each agent in a multiagent system specializes in specific roles,
  personas, or tools, bringing unique perspectives and expertise.

- This specialization enhances efficiency and decision-making by
  allowing agents to work together seamlessly.

- A key benefit of this architecture is its scalability---new agents can
  be added as demands grow, or tasks evolve, requiring minimal system
  redesign.

- By fostering specialization, collaboration, and adaptability,
  multiagent systems offer a robust and flexible approach to tackling
  dynamic and complex challenges.

- Multiagent systems distribute tasks among agents, scaling effectively
  for complex workflows.

- Task-specific agents reduce overhead by working independently or in
  parallel, optimizing execution times for workflows.
:::
::::

Scalability and performance are
[fundamental]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm74} for the success of
generative AI applications to last longer.
::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec30 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec30Heading"}
## Security, Compliance, and Ethical AI Challenges {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec30Heading .Heading}

In previous sections, we explored design patterns to optimize AI models
for speed, [efficiency]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm75},
scalability, and modularity to address challenges such as performance
bottlenecks, model selections to help develop AI systems that scale
effectively and []{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB76 .pagebreak
aria-label="76" role="doc-pagebreak"}operate efficiently in dynamic
environments. However, for AI to be trustable and sustainable, it's
important that it's secure, compliant, and ethically sound. In this
section, we will cover design patterns focusing primarily on privacy,
fairness, security, and regulatory compliance ensuring that the final AI
systems are safe, ethical, and trustworthy.

[Ethical AI]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm76} is no longer just a
choice but is a requirement for sustainable and impactful AI adoption
and encompasses many risks/challenges such as data security, safety,
anonymity, biasness, regularity compliances, third-party integrations,
etc. Each of these needs to be handled/mitigated for enterprise-wide
adoption of AI/generative AI solution. Design patterns for ethical AI
are as follows:

"**Bias Detection & Mitigation**" identifies and reduces bias in
training datasets and model output.

"**Explainability and Auditability**" makes AI decisions more
interpretable for end users and stakeholders.

"**Content Moderation Pattern**" helps filters generate outputs for
harmful or inappropriate contents. It helps prevent the dissemination of
offensive materials.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par141 .Para}
"**Security and compliance--focused**" design patterns help enhance the
safety, security, and compliance of software systems, particularly when
handling sensitive data. They provide a structured approach to implement
security best practices and other
[compliance]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm77} requirements such as
encryption, decryption, authentication, authorization, audition,
governance, etc. Figure
[[3-8](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Fig8)]{.InternalRef} represents some
of the privacy-preserving [best
practices]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm78} to ensure
enterprise-wide adoption of generative AI.

<figure id="Fig8" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_MO8" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_3_Chapter/641294_1_En_3_Fig8_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle19"
alt="Flow chart illustrating a &quot;Generative AI Solution&quot; at the center, surrounded by interconnected nodes representing key components: Data Minimization, Security Audits, Secured Data Transmission, Secured Deployment, Access Control, API Security, PII Masking, Democratization, Loggings &amp; Monitoring, Model Security, Regulatory Compliance, and Third-Party Security. Each component is depicted in a circular node, indicating its role in the overall solution." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 3-8</span>
<p>Privacy-preserving best practices for enterprise-wide adoption of
generative <span id="641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm79">AI</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>
:::

[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB77 .pagebreak aria-label="77"
role="doc-pagebreak"}A few design patterns which we discussed earlier by
default enable security and safety of data in software systems. For
example:

In "**federated learning pattern**,"
[data]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm80} remains unshared and stays
local. Data is encrypted during the state of rest or during
transmissions (if at all) between central servers and edge devices. It
also helps comply with laws like GDPR by processing data locally.

Patterns such as "**Differential Privacy Pattern**" add mathematical
[noise]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm81} to datasets or even the
model output to protect individual data points while also preserving the
aggregated insights. This again aligns with privacy laws by anonymizing
data used in the training and inference process.

"**Zero Trust Pattern**" implements stricter control over data. By
default, it considers [zero trust]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm82}
in any system/anyone, and permissions are given on a need basis. This
helps meet [regulatory compliance]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm83}
like HIPAA and PCI-DSS by ensuring secure access to sensitive data.

By integrating these patterns, developers can secure sensitive data
while minimizing risk exposure, which helps build trust with end users
by demonstrating robust privacy and security practices. Also, all these
helps adhere to various regulatory and compliance requirements. All
these enhance wider adoption and future proof of the AI system.

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par147 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB78 .pagebreak aria-label="78"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The following matrix (Table
[[3-6](#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab6)]{.InternalRef}) maps each
ethical and security-focused design pattern to the key privacy
objectives it supports, along with corresponding global regulations or
standards it helps address.

::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Tab6 .Table}
  Design patterns                   Privacy/security objective                                                                    Relevant standard/compliance
  --------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------
  Federated learning                Keeps data local, avoids central aggregation                                                  GDPR, CCPA
  Differential privacy              Adds statistical noise to anonymize data                                                      GDPR, FERPA, OECD Privacy Guidelines
  Zero trust architecture           Ensures least-privilege access and zero default trust                                         HIPAA, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS
  Encryption at rest/transit        Protects sensitive data during storage and transmission                                       HIPAA, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS
  Content moderation                Prevents generation of harmful or inappropriate outputs                                       Platform policies, Digital Services Act
  Explainability and auditability   Increases transparency and supports traceability                                              AI Act (EU), GDPR (Right to Explanation)
  Bias detection and mitigation     Promotes fairness and nondiscrimination in [outcomes]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm85}   EEOC, GDPR (Fairness), FTC AI Guidelines

  : [Table 3-6]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Mapping security and privacy design patterns to compliance
  [standards]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm84} {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_FPar1 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

It's important to note that design patterns alone are not sufficient to
ensure ethical AI. They must be combined with **ethical guidelines,
stakeholder engagement, diverse team composition, and ongoing
monitoring** to address the dynamic and context-specific nature of
ethical challenges in AI.
::::
::::::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec31 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec31Heading"}
## Conclusion {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Sec31Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_Par149 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_3_Chapter.xhtml_PB79 .pagebreak aria-label="79"
role="doc-pagebreak"}To create enterprise AI solutions, we must adopt a
holistic strategy:

::: UnorderedList
- Start with scalable AI architecture leveraging patterns like
  microservices, federated learning, observer, and multiagent AI.

- Ensure fairness, security, and compliance using patterns like
  explainability, differential privacy, and zero trust.

- Continuously monitor and improve AI performance using feedback loops
  and bias mitigation.
:::
::::

By incorporating all these patterns into the AI design life cycle, we
can build [high-performing,
responsible]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional} generative AI
applications.

In conclusion, this chapter has provided a comprehensive introduction to
design patterns and their crucial role in building robust, scalable, and
ethical generative AI applications. Understanding these foundational
concepts and exploring the best practices enables us to make informed
decisions in designing and implementation of AI solutions. As we move
forward in this book, we will uncover the patterns and discover the ways
in which these can be applied to complex, real-world scenarios in the
ever-changing field of generative AI.
:::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml}

`<!--Begin Abstract-->`{=html}

::: MainTitleSection
# 4. Introduction to Agentic AI {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_chaptertitle .ChapterTitle lang="en"}
:::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: Fulltext
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB81 .pagebreak aria-label="81"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In the previous chapter, we explored design
patterns essential for building an enterprise-grade AI application that
ensures stability, scalability, modularity, performance, and security
throughout the development life cycle---from data preprocessing to model
deployment to inference.

While these patterns provide a strong foundation for building robust
generative AI applications, today's applications increasingly demand
adaptability, real-time decision-making, dynamic context integration,
and continuous learning. Agentic systems are proving to be a powerful
evolution in AI architecture.

These systems leverage memory architectures, reinforcement learning,
autonomous workflow and multiagents that interact with data, tools, and
environments, extending traditional design patterns to enable
intelligent systems capable of responding to changing conditions,
refining themselves iteratively, and making complex decisions
autonomously.

In this chapter, we will cover the emerging trend in generative
AI[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}agentic systems. While
traditional AI systems are rule-based and narrowly programmed for
specific tasks, and generative AI focuses on producing content such as
text, images, or code, agentic AI goes a step further---acting
autonomously, making decisions, setting goals, and dynamically
interacting with its environment to accomplish complex objectives.

We will introduce you to the core principles of agentic systems, explore
key design patterns for building autonomous AI agents, and discuss how
these systems integrate into enterprise workflows.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par7 .Para}
By the end of this chapter, you will

::: UnorderedList
- Develop a better understanding of agentic and their role in dynamic AI
  systems.

- Learn about agent registry and discovery process essential for
  orchestrating agent ecosystems.

- []{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB82 .pagebreak aria-label="82"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}Gain insights into the fundamental building
  blocks and key characteristics of agentic workflows.

- Explore a case study illustrating the application of agentic AI in
  manufacturing processes.

- Understand the architectural considerations crucial for implementing
  effective agentic workflows.

- Identify common technical, organizational, and ethical challenges, and
  discover strategies for overcoming them.

- Get introduced to the concept of self-reflection in AI and appreciate
  its importance for continuous learning and improvement.

- Understand the evolving concept of the Model Context Protocol and its
  role in enabling context-aware agents.
:::
::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1 .section .Section1 .RenderAsSection1 aria-labelledby="Sec1Heading"}
## Agentic Workflows and Its Inception {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec1Heading .Heading}

The automation process has come a long way from manual labor to today's
increasingly autonomous and intelligent systems. To understand the
significance of agentic workflow, it's important to understand the
evolution that led us here.

Agentic
[workflows]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm1}[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm2}
are a modern automation paradigm driven by AI agents capable of
perceiving, reasoning, acting, and adapting, and this represents a
significant shift to dynamic, autonomous AI systems.

***From Manual to Robotic Process Automation***

In the early days, business processes were carried out manually,
requiring significant human efforts and collaboration for repetitive
tasks such as data entry, status tracking, invoice processing, etc. The
emergence of rule-based systems such as [Robotic Process Automation
(RPA)]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm3}[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm4}
marked the first major wave of automation. This development led to
automation of many of these well-structured repetitive rule-based tasks
by mimicking human interactions with digital systems. These systems
worked well and brought efficiency gains in environments where the
inputs and outcomes were predictable.

However, RPA systems came with limitations. They lacked flexibility,
couldn't handle unstructured data, and were not very adaptable when
underlying systems or formats changed.

***Integration of AI with RPA***

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB83 .pagebreak aria-label="83"
role="doc-pagebreak"}To overcome these limitations, businesses began
integrating artificial intelligence (AI) into these RPA
[systems]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm5}[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm6}
which brought significant advancements enabling them to address these
shortcomings. This hybrid solution is often referred to as [intelligent
automation]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm7}. AI techniques such as
[optical character recognition
(OCR)]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm8}, [natural language
processing (NLP)]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm9}, and machine
[learning]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm10} (ML) enabled these
systems to process unstructured data and take basic decisions through
predictive models providing a degree of adaptability.

Despite these advancements, systems still had several limitations, and
it remained largely static. Though they were able to perform additional
tasks and respond to variations with limitations, they often lacked
deeper contextual or domain-specific understanding and reasoning
capabilities. Most importantly, they still lacked adaptability in
evolving or changing situations.

***Emergence of AI Agents***

The next major [leap]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm11} came with
the emergence of "AI agents" which began to transform automation. Unlike
traditional systems that follow fixed logic, AI agents are autonomous.
In its simplest form, an AI agent is an "intelligent entity" powered by
large language models (LLMs).
[LLMs]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm12} act as the reasoning,
planning, and execution engine to perform complex tasks. This enables
the AI agents to understand its goals, reason through complex
situations, and take actions. These agents brought a higher level of
intelligence enabling systems to perceive their environments and adapt
to changing needs.

***Rise of Multiagent Systems***

Initially, single AI agent systems were deployed by enterprises to
[perform]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm13} focused, narrowly scoped
tasks. However, to cater to complex enterprise needs where multitude of
tasks must be performed simultaneously, multiagent systems emerged where
multiple specialized agents collaborate, communicate, share context, and
dynamically coordinate to achieve shared goals. The advancement in
generative AI models enables these agents to not only analyze data but
also generate content, define strategies, and derive and deliver
contextual and meaningful insights.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_FPar1 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

It would not be wrong to say that the real breakthrough in agentic
workflows is due to large language models which bring reasoning, context
understanding, and NLG (natural language generation) capabilities to the
agents.
::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB84 .pagebreak aria-label="84"
role="doc-pagebreak"}As the capabilities of individual agents matured
and it became clear that to solve complex real business problems, we
need multiple agents working in collaboration, researchers and
practitioners started to explore and develop theoretical and practical
foundations for these multiagent systems designing protocols for these
groups of specialized agents to collaborate, communicate, and perform
distributed decision-making to solve complex problems.

These [protocols]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm14} enabled the
agents to divide the work among themselves, exchange intermediate
results with each other, and optimize the workflows through dynamic
adjustments.

***Orchestration Frameworks***

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par32 .Para}
To implement multiagent systems effectively, researchers and AI
[practitioners]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15} began focusing on
how to orchestrate, structure, and operationalize these systems which
laid foundation for frameworks that could manage the communication, task
dependencies, role allocations, and decision hierarchies between the
agents. This led to the creation of many frameworks such as Lang Graph,
LangChain, and Autogen - as shown in Table
[[4-1](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Tab1)]{.InternalRef} below. These
frameworks enabled the developers to design and implement modular
agentic workflows where agents interact seamlessly and autonomously.

::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Tab1 .Table}
  Framework    Primary use case                                                   Complexity                                                     Ease of deployment
  ------------ ------------------------------------------------------------------ -------------------------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------
  LangChain    Building LLM-powered apps with modular chains, memory, and tools   Medium---depends on custom chains and integrations             High---well-documented, broad community support
  AutoGen      Multiagent conversations and autonomous task execution             High---advanced agent behaviors, needs careful orchestration   Moderate---setup is script-heavy, less plug-and-play
  Lang Graph   Stateful, multiagent workflows with branching and coordination     Medium to high---requires graph-based thinking and structure   Moderate---easier with LangChain knowledge, still maturing

  : [Table 4-1]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Agentic AI [framework]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16}
  comparison {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

***What makes the agent workflow different?***

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par34 .Para}
Agentic Workflows embodies the convergence of AI agents, language
models, and orchestration frameworks, all working in harmony to manage
complex processes with high level of efficiency and autonomy. The true
strength of an agentic AI [system]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17}
lies in its[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB85 .pagebreak
aria-label="85" role="doc-pagebreak"}

::: UnorderedList
- Autonomy

- Reasoning and planning

- Managing multifaceted tasks
:::
::::

Figure [[4-1](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Fig1)]{.InternalRef} shows
these key capabilities of agentic AI system. With dynamic reasoning and
decision-making, these agents analyze, deliberate, and apply their
learning to solve problems with a high level of efficiency. These
autonomous intelligent agents make decisions, execute actions, and
coordinate tasks with minimal human intervention and use tools to
execute complex tasks efficiently.

***Sample Agentic Workflow***

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par40 .Para}
An agentic [workflow]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18} might have
the following agents working together:

::: UnorderedList
- A **data collection agent** which gathers unstructured data from
  different sources

- A **text analysis agent** which processes the text content and derives
  meaningful insights

- A **recommendation agent** which evaluates the results and generates
  tailored personalized strategies

- A **campaign execution agent** which automates the execution and
  monitors feedback loop
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_FPar2 .FormalPara .FormalParaRenderingStyle1 .ParaTypeImportant}
::: Heading
Note
:::

It is not necessary for AI agents to use generative AI (LLMs) for their
tasks. AI agents can very well be based on traditional algorithms,
rule-based logic, or any other simple logic. The need for an agent to be
generative AI based depends entirely on the complexity of the task and
the level of reasoning or creativity needed. An ideal agentic AI
solution combines traditional AI/ML techniques with selective generative
AI agents. Traditional AI agents could be used for structured
well-defined tasks while generative AI agents could be used for tasks
requiring interpretation, reasoning or dynamic rule adaptation. The
strength of the agentic AI system lies in its autonomy, interagent
collaboration, and ability to manage large complex tasks (often standing
for a business process) effectively.
::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB86 .pagebreak aria-label="86"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Each agent plays a specific role. It's designed to
perform a specific task or a set of tasks autonomously, often by
receiving input, processing them, and producing outputs. Together, they
operate with an orchestration layer which ensures communication,
coordination, and a proper process flow. This orchestration layer can be
developed using standard frameworks such as Autogen or LangChain or
custom-built based on organizational requirements.

<figure id="Fig1" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO1" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Fig1_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle20"
alt="Flow chart illustrating the key capabilities of agentic AI. Central box labeled &quot;Key Capabilities of Agentic AI&quot; connects to four surrounding circles: &quot;Planning &amp; Decision Making&quot; at the top, &quot;Networking with other Agents&quot; on the right, &quot;Act Autonomously&quot; at the bottom, and &quot;High Performance&quot; on the left. Arrows indicate the flow from the central box to each capability." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 4-1</span>
<p>Key capabilities of agentic AI <span
id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19">system</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

In summary, agentic workflow represents a significant leap forward; it
combines the flexibility of AI with the modularity of the agents and the
coordination of orchestration frameworks to solve business problems
effectively and efficiently.

They sometimes are also referred to as "[agentic
processes]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20}" where emphasis is not
only on task execution but on autonomous collaboration and real-time
decision-making.

As the adoption of agentic workflows and businesses develop hundreds and
thousands of agents across domains, managing them at scale brings in a
new challenge. This brings us to agent registry and discovery, the next
foundational layer of agentic systems which forms the backbone of
scalable, modular, interoperable, and reusable agentic systems. We will
discuss the layer in the upcoming section.

::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec2Heading"}
### Agent Registry and Discovery {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec2Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB87 .pagebreak aria-label="87"
role="doc-pagebreak"}As agentic systems evolve into enterprise scale
systems and continue to [grow]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21} in
scale, complexity, and distribution, it becomes essential to have
efficient ways to catalog, register, search, select, and orchestrate
these multiple agents developed for specific purposes. Without a
centralized system to register and discover agents, it becomes difficult
to know what agents already exist, limiting reusability and leading to
duplication of development efforts. In addition, it limits the
adaptability of orchestrators to search for agents based on
capabilities/input/output and plug agents into the workflow dynamically.
This is where the concept of agent registry and agent discovery comes
into play, which is our topic of discussion in this section.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par51 .Para}
To understand the way this works, let\'s first look at a few design
patterns (illustrated in Figure
[[4-2](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Fig2)]{.InternalRef} below) that an
agentic workflow commonly follows to manage complex tasks.

::: UnorderedList
- **Hierarchical pattern** or multilevel pattern has agents arranged in
  tiers where the top layer delegates the task to lower-level agents and
  then aggregates the results upwards or back up the chain.

- **Multiple pattern or parallel pattern**: Orchestrator agent contacts
  multiple downstream agents for a task and then consolidates the
  results or selects the best output.

- **Sequential pattern**: These have all the agents in series, and they
  all work sequentially to achieve a goal.
:::
::::

<figure id="Fig2" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO2" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Fig2_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Diagram illustrating three design patterns: 1. Multilevel Design Pattern: A hierarchical structure with a &quot;Coordinator Agent&quot; at the top, branching into two &quot;Node Agents,&quot; each further branching into another &quot;Node Agent.&quot; 2. Multiple Design Pattern: A &quot;Coordinator Agent&quot; at the top, branching into three &quot;Node Agents.&quot; 3. Sequential Design Pattern: A linear sequence of three &quot;Node Agents&quot; connected in a row." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 4-2</span>
<p>Various design patterns of <span
id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22">agents</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB88 .pagebreak aria-label="88"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Each of these node agents can encapsulate an AI
model or program to perform a specific task. They may come from the same
team or provider, or they may be developed independently by different
vendors. They could be custom-built or built using the same or different
frameworks, and their deployment could be either colocated or
geographically distributed. Regardless of these differences, all node
