agents should be able to function together.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par56 .Para}
In all these cases, all these node agents must act as "black box" for
the coordinator agent or any other node agent. They should be known only
by their interface. Their internal logic remains abstracted, enabling
interoperability based solely on their capabilities exposed. Agent
registry enables management of this diverse ecosystem. It's a structured
catalog and acts as a discovery hub where each agent is registered with
its **essential metadata fields** such as

::: UnorderedList
- Agent name and description

- Task or domain specialization

- Acceptable input and output schema

- Underlying engine, i.e., LLM, ML, rule-based, etc.

- Capabilities, e.g., reasoning, tool use, planning, etc.

- **Endpoint location**: Endpoint where the agent is deployed as a
  microservice
:::
::::

As agents are registered, they become discoverable through the agent
discovery process where any coordinator/consumer or orchestrator agent
searches for the best fit agent for the given task and invokes it to
make use of it to solve complex problems at hand. This entire process
makes the agentic workflow highly modular, dynamic, and scalable.

Let's further understand this with a diagrammatic representation, shown
below in Figure
[[4-3](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Fig3)]{.InternalRef}.

<figure id="Fig3" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO3" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Fig3_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Flow chart illustrating the interaction between different types of agents across three zones: Zone A, Agents Pool, and Zone B. Blue circles represent Coordinator Agents, orange circles represent Manufacturing Agents, and green circles represent Operations Management Agents. Arrows labeled &quot;EP&quot; indicate endpoints connecting the agents. The chart shows the flow and communication paths among the agents within and between the zones." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 4-3</span>
<p>Agent pool and their <span
id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23">coordination</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB89 .pagebreak aria-label="89"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In the center, we have a pool of agents known as
the [agent pool]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24} or agent registry
where agents with different problem-solving capabilities are registered.
Few are domain focused and perform activities of specific domains, e.g.,
manufacturing and healthcare, and few are aligned with specific business
pillars such as operations management or customer service. In addition,
the pool also includes task-specific agents developed to perform
specialized functions.

The agent pool is just a register where all agent details are available
in a go. These agents are developed, deployed, and maintained across
various geographical regions (countries/metros). Each agent is hosted at
its own endpoint and is made available for external consumption. The
left- and right-hand side represents two different parts of the world
(or distinct business units). There are various coordinator agents
looking for task-specific agents to solve the problem at hand. They go
through the process of agent discovery and identify the most suitable
and relevant agent(s) from the pool of agents and consume it to solve
the problem at hand.

Each of these agents in the pool is unknown to the orchestrator agent or
any other agent in the pool, exposing only its interface and
capabilities, communicating with each other via their respective end
points (EP). Coordination between them can be orchestrated using agentic
AI frameworks such as Autogen, LangChain, or customized code. We saw
above how agent registry and discovery enable support for building
distributed and scalable agentic systems. However, these distributed and
scalable deployments face critical challenges which need to be
addressed, few cases mentioned below.

In globally distributed systems, ensuring privacy and compliance
presents significant challenges. One key consideration is complying with
Data residency regulations where certain jurisdictions maintain that
sensitive data must remain within their geographical boundaries. Agents
interacting with such data must adhere to these regulations. Any
violation of this policy could lead to severe penalties or even
exclusion from the operational network or consortium. Therefore, in such
scenarios, the agents must be replicated and deployed within the same
jurisdiction as the data they process.

Another key challenge is maintaining low latency. To achieve this,
enterprises must strategically deploy agents near the data sources and
ensure they are connected via high-speed networks.

Adding to the complexity, agents might need to pull data from multiple
different data sources, each possibly governed by different policies or
are on different platforms and using different access protocols. This is
where a well-defined and maintained agent registry plays a key role.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par71 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB90 .pagebreak aria-label="90"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Registry may store detailed metadata about each
agent including the type of data sources it is authorized to interact
with, the location where it operates, etc. This metadata enables the
coordinator agent to perform intelligent agent discovery selecting only
the right agents that can efficiently and legally operate within the
context of the required data source. As a result, agent registry serves
as a filtering mechanism ensuring that only the right
fit[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}technically, legally, and
geographically[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}is engaged for
a given purpose. Table
[[4-2](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Tab2)]{.InternalRef} below shows key
metadata fields that must be captured for each agent and registered in
the registry.

::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Tab2 .Table}
  Metadata fields     Definition
  ------------------- -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Agent name          Stores the name of the agent
  Agent description   Stores the detailed description of the agent functionality
  Task                Holds the details of the task which this agent specializes in
  Input schema        Details of the input fields of this agent
  Output schema       Details of the output fields of this agent
  Agent engine        Details of the underlying language model which the agent is using or other details such as any auxiliary tool which this agent accesses
  Capabilities        The capabilities of the agents
  Location            The endpoint where the agent is deployed as a microservice

  : [Table 4-2]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Metadata fields [details]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25}
  {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par72 .Para}
In summary, as the agentic system scales, the agent registry and
discovery system becomes the connective tissue of the agentic
ecosystems. It

::: UnorderedList
- Enables reuse and modularity

- Ensures compliance and performance in distributed environments

- Enables dynamic orchestration across agent frameworks and providers
:::
::::

Having explored how agents are deployed, they cataloged and discovered
across large-scale systems. Let's next look at the major building blocks
that power an agentic workflow.
:::::::::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec3Heading"}
### Key Components of Agentic Workflows {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec3Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB91 .pagebreak aria-label="91"
role="doc-pagebreak"}To understand the way the agentic system operates,
it becomes crucial to look at their key
[building]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm26} blocks, as shown in
Figure [[4-4](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Fig4)]{.InternalRef}. These
components enable the intelligence and end-to-end functionality of agent
workflows.

**Natural language processing (NLP)**:
[NLP]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm27} comprising both natural
language generation and natural language understanding is a crucial
building block for agentic systems as it forms the foundation for
human-AI interactions. It enables the agents to understand, interpret,
and respond to human queries that are both meaningful and contextually
relevant. This is important for building conversational agents that
interact with humans in real time.

**Agents and tools**: Agents are intelligent autonomous entities capable
of decision-making and task execution and learn from interactions. These
may or may not employ large language models (LLMs). Tools are functions
or utilities agents use, such as Python functions, vector databases, API
endpoints, or traditional AI/ML services. These tools act as agents\'
peripherals.

**Orchestrator**: [Orchestrator]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm28}
is the brain of an agentic AI system; it's the central planner and
executor. It helps determine which tools or agents to use, in which
sequence, and under what conditions. Orchestrators often use LLM for
planning, reasoning, and adapting the workflows. They rely on structured
system prompts which they use to achieve the desired results.

**Memory management** is [another]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm29}
core capability of the orchestrator. They maintain short-term and
long-term memory to contain any information that the agent might need to
retain and help with the reason for the actions it needs to take.

**System integration**: Agentic
[workflow]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm30} must integrate
seamlessly with various systems and data sources including input/output
channels, agents, third-party APIs, databases, and internal tools.
Effective integration ensures agents can access and act on data and
exchange details across the distributed environment.

<figure id="Fig4" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO4" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Fig4_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Flow chart depicting a process involving four components. The &quot;Core Orchestrator (LLM Based)&quot; is central, with arrows pointing to and from it. To the left, an arrow connects to &quot;Planning Step.&quot; To the right, an arrow leads to &quot;Set of Tools.&quot; Below, an arrow points to &quot;Memory.&quot; The chart illustrates the interaction between these components." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 4-4</span>
<p>Building blocks of agentic AI <span
id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31">system</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

**Memory**

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB92 .pagebreak aria-label="92"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Memory plays a critical role in ensuring agents
behave intelligently over time. Like humans, agents also need memory
structures to retain and recall and use relevant information during
reasoning, interactions, or task executions.

Let us delve a bit further into the "memory"
[management]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32} within an agentic AI
system. The controller might want to revisit some historic interactions
or retain minute details about any interactions which it does so via
memory component. There are primary two types of memory in an agentic
system.

**Working memory**: Also known as [short-term
memory]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm33}. [Working
memory]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm34} is used by the
controller/orchestrator during the immediate reasoning loop to decide on
the next best action to solve the human intent. It enables the system to
retain temporary additional context such as agents output, user input,
or recent interactions often as appended messages or variables in a
system prompt. For example, the LLM response based on a prior query
could be retained in a variable which could be passed as context in the
subsequent call to LLM. This enables the orchestrator to chain reasoning
steps together effectively. However, working memory has limitations:

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB93 .pagebreak aria-label="93"
role="doc-pagebreak"}LLMs operate within a fixed context window or token
limit. As we continue to append the interactions, the prompt size grows
and may eventually exceed the token limit size of the LLM model in use.
This leaves us with limited space to store the entire interaction
history with the LLM.

Also, larger context reduces the space available for new information
that needs to be provided to the model in the subsequent calls and may
dilute [model]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm35} performance if
overloaded with irrelevant history.

To address these limitations, agentic systems often complement working
memory with explicit long-term memory.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par90 .Para}
**Long-term memory**: [Long-term
memory]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm36} stores information or
context that resides outside the working memory of an agent and is not
provided in the system prompt. It can be retrieved on demand at any
point in time of the flow by the orchestrator or the agent. Examples
include

::: UnorderedList
- Vector databases (such as Pinecone, FAISS, Chroma, Elastic, Redis
  Vector)

- Any private knowledge database

- Other ground context landing location
:::
::::

Long-term memory enables agents to access historical or broader
domain-specific knowledge without overwhelming the context window
ensuring continuity across broader workflows. Together, these memory
structures enable agents to adapt intelligently, maintain relevance, and
handle multistep complex problems while keeping track of context or
prior knowledge.

Having understood the key components, it\'s now important to understand
the key [capabilities]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37} that make
these agentic workflows effective, scalable, intelligent, and powerful.
:::::

:::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec4Heading"}
### Core Capabilities of Agentic Workflows {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec4Heading .Heading}

::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par96 .Para}
Agentic workflows comprise powerful operational capabilities that
differentiate them from traditional automation systems (highlighted in
Table [[4-3](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Tab3)]{.InternalRef}) and even
basic AI [systems]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38} and enable them
to handle complex tasks with minimal to no human intervention and
operate with intelligence. These capabilities define how agentic AI
systems behave in real-world settings and what makes them uniquely
suited for these complex real-world
scenarios.[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB94 .pagebreak
aria-label="94" role="doc-pagebreak"}

::: UnorderedList
- ***Autonomy:*** Agentic AI workflows operate with a high degree of
  autonomy. Agents can plan, make decisions, and execute tasks with
  minimal or no human intervention. Each agent can run independently to
  complete its assigned tasks. Decisions within tasks are made based on
  predefined rules or context or AI-driven insights. It even does not
  require explicit external triggers as it can monitor and act at the
  right time, freeing humans from constant "wait-and-watch"/oversight
  tasks.

- ***Multiagent collaboration:*** Agents work in tandem, passing outputs
  to one other either directly or via the orchestration layer. All the
  participating agents collaborate to solve complex problems as a team,
  leveraging each other's strengths. The tasks can either be worked in
  parallel or sequential or follow any other design patterns discussed
  previously in the chapter, such as multilevel, multiple, or sequential
  series of agents.

- ***Orchestration:*** There could be centralized or
  decentralized/distributed orchestrators to coordinate between the
  agents to ensure the workflow process correctly.

- ***Generative reasoning:*** Agents with LLM or similar generative AI
  capabilities can analyze, infer, and generate creative and contextual
  solutions.

- ***Scalability:*** The modular design of agentic workflows enables
  easy extension of new agents for added tasks. Through the agent
  registry and discovery process, orchestrators can dynamically identify
  and invoke the most suitable agent from the growing pool, enabling
  dynamic scaling of the workflows.

- ***Real-time optimization:*** Agents continuously optimize their
  behavior in real time based on built-in feedback loops or
  self-reflective mechanisms. Workflows can dynamically reorganize
  themselves to minimize delays and maximize resource utilization.

- []{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB95 .pagebreak aria-label="95"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}***Efficiency:*** Since each AI agent focuses on
  specific tasks, they can be fine-tuned and optimized independently to
  achieve maximum efficiency. This modularity ensures that the overall
  system performs more efficiently than monolithic AI models, especially
  when fine-tuning is needed.

- ***Governance and compliance:*** Agents can maintain detailed logs of
  all their actions, interactions, and decisions taken. This
  transparency enables easy traceability, regulatory compliance, and
  debugging. Accountability can be clearly attributed to a specific
  agent by analyzing their activity logs.

- ***Adaptability:*** Agentic AI systems can quickly readjust their plan
  and strategy as per the need and information at hand, making it more
  robust, resilient, and effective in dynamic situations.

- ***Self-reflection:*** Agentic systems can evaluate their own
  performance and refine their future behavior accordingly. This
  self-reflective capability enables continuous iterative improvements
  in the overall system.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Tab3 .Table}
  Feature               Traditional automation         Agentic workflows
  --------------------- ------------------------------ -------------------------------------
  **Task management**   Rule-based scripts             Autonomous and intelligent agents
  **Adaptability**      Limited                        High with AI-driven decision-making
  **Collaboration**     Sequential and rigid           Dynamic and contextual
  **Scalability**       Requires significant efforts   Scales organically with agents

  : [Table 4-3]{.CaptionNumber}\
  [Traditional automation vs. agentic
  workflow]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39}[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40}
  {.TableStyle5}
:::
:::::

Having covered the core capabilities, let's now understand how these
translate into real-world value. Let's next focus on a practical
scenario. In the next section, let us explore a miniature version of the
manufacturing plant process flow and identify areas of automation. We
will also look at how implementation of such systems could be made more
efficient, intelligent, and robust through the agentic AI approach.
::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec5Heading"}
### Agentic AI Uses in Industry {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec5Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par108 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB96 .pagebreak aria-label="96"
role="doc-pagebreak"}There are many enterprise-wide problem statements
that could be effectively addressed using an agentic AI framework. Few
examples use cases that can benefit from an agentic AI
[approach]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41} including

::: UnorderedList
- Data duplication and validation

- Automated regulatory compliance monitoring

- Manufacturing process optimization

- Dynamic supply chain optimization

- IT incident management

- Personalized customer engagement

- Employee skill development and career planning

- Energy management in enterprises

- Fraud detection in financial systems

- Software development and testing

- Real-time incident response for cybersecurity

- Cross departmental collaboration in large organizations

- Predictive maintenance of industrial equipment

- Intelligent proposal and bid management

- Dynamic workforce scheduling

- Multichannel marketing optimization

- Real-time content moderation

- [Enterprise]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42} knowledge
  management
:::
::::

These applications highlight the need for autonomous agents capable of
handling tasks with little to no human oversight precisely of what
agentic AI offers. In this section, we will delve into a use case from
the manufacturing domain. We will see how to implement a miniature
version of end-to-end process flow in a manufacturing plan using agentic
AI.

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB97 .pagebreak aria-label="97"
role="doc-pagebreak"}For this example, we will not use any of the
available agentic frameworks, rather we will create our own custom
solution grounds up. This will enable us to understand the concept by
implementing it from scratch as the available framework abstracts a lot
of implementation concepts.

**Example: Manufacturing Plant End-to-End Process (Mini Version)**

In a typical manufacturing
[plant]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm43}, the overall production
life cycle is broken into a series of well-defined stages, each
comprising different activities which are performed one after the other.

**Stage I:** Raw material procurement

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par132 .Para}
*Activities:*

::: UnorderedList
- Sourcing suppliers

- Negotiating contracts

- Managing inventory and purchase orders
:::
::::

**Stage II:** Inbound logistics coordination

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par137 .Para}
*Activities:*

::: UnorderedList
- Transportation and tracking of raw materials

- Warehouse receipt and storage
:::
::::

**Stage III:** Production planning

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par141 .Para}
*Activities:*

::: UnorderedList
- Creating schedules for manufacturing

- Allocating resources

- Estimating costs and timelines
:::
::::

**Stage IV:** Manufacturing

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par146 .Para}
*Activities:*

::: UnorderedList
- Operating [machinery]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44} and
  assembling parts

- Monitoring quality in real time
:::
::::

**Stage V:** Quality control

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par150 .Para}
*Activities:*

::: UnorderedList
- Inspecting products for defects

- Conducting compliance test
:::
::::

**Stage VI:** Outbound logistics coordination

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par154 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB98 .pagebreak aria-label="98"
role="doc-pagebreak"}*Activities:*

::: UnorderedList
- Packaging and shipping of finished goods

- Tracking shipments to customers
:::
::::

**Stage VII:** Customer feedback and support

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par158 .Para}
*Activities:*

::: UnorderedList
- Collecting feedback post delivery

- Resolving issues and returns
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par161 .Para}
Trying to execute all these stages and activities with a single AI agent
would be inefficient, impractical, and nonscalable. Instead, we
decompose the entire flow into multiple separate, individual,
modularized task-specific agents, each responsible for a specific stage
or activity. This modular [design]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm45}
will build a more robust and efficient AI system. Table
[[4-4](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Tab4)]{.InternalRef} shows mapping
of individual AI agents to specific stages and activities.

::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Tab4 .Table}
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
| Process stage     | AI-based agents                               | Functionality                                     | LLM utility |
+===================+===============================================+===================================================+=============+
| **Raw material    | Supplier matching agent                       | Uses NLP and similarity matching to analyze       | **ΔΔΔΔΔ**   |
| procurement**     |                                               | supplier contracts, historical data, and          |             |
|                   |                                               | recommend best suppliers                          | **High**    |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
|                   | Price prediction agent                        | Predicts pricing trends for raw materials using   | **ΔΔΔ**     |
|                   |                                               | historical data and market conditions             |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
| **Inbound         | Logistics optimization agent                  | Plans best routes and schedules deliveries based  | **ΔΔΔ**     |
| logistics**       |                                               | on traffic, distance, and urgency                 |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
|                   | Inventory forecasting agent                   | Predicts inventory levels and flags shortages or  | **ΔΔΔ**     |
|                   |                                               | overstock situations using demand forecasts       |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
| **Production      | Resource allocation agent                     | Allocates resources dynamically based on          | **ΔΔΔ**     |
| planning**        |                                               | priorities, ability, and cost-effectiveness       |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
|                   | Demand forecasting agent                      | Predicts production demand using historical sales | **High**    |
|                   |                                               | data, market trends, and seasonality              |             |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
| **Manufacturing** | Predictive maintenance                        | Analyzes sensor data to predict machine failures  | **ΔΔΔ**     |
|                   | [agent]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47} | and schedules maintenance to reduce downtime      |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
|                   | Process optimization agent                    | Monitors production parameters (e.g., speed,      | **ΔΔΔ**     |
|                   |                                               | temperature) and optimizes them for maximum       |             |
|                   |                                               | efficiency                                        | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
| **Quality         | Defect detection agent                        | Uses computer vision to inspect products for      | **ΔΔΔ**     |
| control**         |                                               | defects in real time                              |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
|                   | Compliance monitoring agent                   | Analyzes test data for compliance with standards  | **ΔΔΔΔΔ**   |
|                   |                                               | and regulations                                   |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **High**    |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
| **Outbound        | Shipment tracking agent                       | Monitors shipments in real time and provides      | **ΔΔΔ**     |
| logistics**       |                                               | status updates to customers and plant managers    |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
|                   | Route Planning Agent                          | Calculates the most efficient delivery routes     | **ΔΔΔ**     |
|                   |                                               | based on real-time traffic and other conditions   |             |
|                   |                                               |                                                   | **Medium**  |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
| **Customer        | Sentiment analysis agent                      | Analyzes customer feedback to find issues and     | **ΔΔΔΔΔ**   |
| feedback and      |                                               | recommend improvements                            |             |
| support**         |                                               |                                                   | **High**    |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+
|                   | Issue resolution agent                        | [Automates]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48} | **ΔΔΔΔΔ**   |
|                   |                                               | responses to customer complaints or routes them   |             |
|                   |                                               | to the right team for resolution                  | **High**    |
+-------------------+-----------------------------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+-------------+

: [Table 4-4]{.CaptionNumber}\
Mapping AI-based agents to manufacturing [process
stages]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm46} {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

<figure id="Fig5" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO5" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Fig5_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Flow chart illustrating a supply chain process. It begins with &quot;Raw Material Procurement,&quot; followed by &quot;Inbound Logistics,&quot; &quot;Production Planning,&quot; and &quot;Manufacturing.&quot; Each stage is connected by arrows and utilizes various agents: Supplier Matching, Price Prediction, Logistics Optimization, Inventory Forecasting, Resource Allocation, Demand Forecasting, Predictive Maintenance, and Process Optimization. The agents are linked to the stages, indicating their roles in the process." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 4-5a</span>
<p>Manufacturing stage “Raw Material Procurement” until “Manufacturing”
mapping with their corresponding AI-based <span
id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49">agents</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

<figure id="Fig6" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO6" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Fig6_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Flow chart illustrating a process involving several agents and functions. &quot;Quality Control&quot; leads to &quot;Outbound Logistics,&quot; which connects to &quot;Customer Feedback &amp; Support.&quot; Below, agents include &quot;Defect Detection,&quot; &quot;Compliance Monitoring,&quot; &quot;Shipment Tracking,&quot; &quot;Route Planning,&quot; &quot;Sentiment Analysis,&quot; and &quot;Issue Resolution.&quot; Arrows labeled &quot;Uses&quot; indicate the flow and relationships between these components." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 4-5b</span>
<p><span id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB100" class="pagebreak"
aria-label="100" role="doc-pagebreak"></span>Manufacturing stage
“Quality Control” until “Customer Feedback &amp; Support” mapping with
their corresponding AI-based <span
id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50">agents</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

The "**LLM utility**" [column]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51} in
the table indicates the applicability of LLM in the design of each
agent. "High" suggests that LLM plays a central role. "Medium" indicates
that LLMs can be useful, but the design is not fully dependent and
traditional statistical model or rule-based models may suffice.

Please note that not all agents require LLMs to be used. In fact,
force-fitting LLMs into tasks which can be achieved using simpler
solutions can lead to overengineering resulting in complex,
unnecessarily slow, and less accurate solutions. Choosing the right
solution for the right task---be it an LLM, a traditional ML model, a
rule engine, or a utility function---is essential to building efficient
and maintainable agentic systems.

::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec6Heading"}
#### The Orchestrator Layer {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec6Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB101 .pagebreak aria-label="101"
role="doc-pagebreak"}As we see in the above figures - Figure
[[4-5a](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Fig5)]{.InternalRef} and Figure
[[4-5b](#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Fig6)]{.InternalRef}, we have
identified the need for 14
[agents]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52} that will be required in
executing the complete workflow of the manufacturing process. It will be
challenging to coordinate without an orchestration layer. As we saw in
the capabilities section, the orchestrator layer serves as the "brain"
of the agentic AI system.

All the agents are controlled by this layer. The orchestrating layer in
a workflow is responsible for coordinating and controlling the execution
of tasks performed by agents by managing dependencies and ensuring they
run in the right sequence. The orchestration layer also ensures a smooth
flow of data and instructions between the agents.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par166 .Para}
There are primarily two orchestration approaches:

::: UnorderedList
- Centralized orchestration

- Decentralized orchestration
:::
::::

In **centralized orchestration**, a
[central]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53} orchestrator agent
manages all agent interactions. Individual agents report their status
back to the central orchestrator, which ensures that dependencies are
followed, and the right sequence is maintained.

In the **decentralized orchestrator**
[setting]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54}, the agents are more
autonomous and interact directly with one another. They often use
communication protocols such as message queues to ensure smooth
interaction. The system relies on event-driven design patterns to
trigger the next tasks. Though this approach provides more flexibility
and resilience as it does not depend on a central controller, such
systems are more complex to design.

For our manufacturing process use case, we will use the centralized
orchestration approach due to its predictability, ease of monitoring,
and control, which are critical for the structured industrial workflow.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par172 .Para}
Several frameworks are available to assist with orchestration, and the
choice depends on the implementation\'s needs. Few are

::: UnorderedList
- ***Apache Airflow:*** Ideal for task scheduling and dependency
  management.

- ***Kubernetes:*** Useful for containerized agent orchestration.

- ***Lang Graph and AutoGen:*** These are designed for orchestrating
  complex multiagentic systems.
:::
::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB102 .pagebreak aria-label="102"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Finally, **prompt engineering**
[plays]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55} a key role when building
an agentic application. A well-defined system prompt containing the
execution context, available tool definitions, expected output
[formats]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56} and/or example enhances
the orchestrator's capability, enabling it to plan and coordinate
effectively.

Let\'s now move from theory to practice.
:::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
### The Implementation {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

Now that we understand key capabilities and components, looked, and
[understood]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57} how memory is managed
and how orchestration is done, let's now try to implement part of the
manufacturing process we discussed above using an agentic AI system.

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
#### Agents {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par179 .Para}
We will focus on a few selected ones out of the 14 agents we listed
above to show the design and execution of an agentic system. The agents
we will implement are

::: UnorderedList
- Supplier matching agent

- Price prediction agent

- Predictive maintenance agent

- Sentiment analysis agent

- We have one centralized orchestrator which acts as the brain of the
  system and designs and plans the sequence in which the agents must be
  called.
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par185 .Para}
For each of these agents, we created the following set of files:

::: UnorderedList
- **\_\_init\_\_.py**: This file is always kept empty, but it helps
  convert code into module.

- **app.py**: In this file, we create FAST API application for each of
  the agent so that it could be exposed as real-time endpoint which
  could be consumed by the orchestrator (in the centralized setting) or
  directly by other agents (in a decentralized setting).

- **Dockerfile**: To create the image to be deployed later.

- **requirements.txt**: To hold all required dependencies.

- []{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB103 .pagebreak aria-label="103"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}**\<agent\>\_logic.py**: This file holds the
  actual logic/algorithm of the agent.

- **tool.py**: Holds the agentic tool function and its definition to be
  infused later into LLM.
:::
::::

The figure below shows how the folder structure looks like for each of
the agent.

<figure id="Figa" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO7" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Figa_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle21"
alt="Screenshot of a file directory in a code editor. The directory includes the following files and folders: `__pycache__`, `__init__.py`, `app.py`, `Dockerfile`, `requirements.txt`, `supplier_logic.py`, and `tool.py`." />
</div>
</figure>

Let's start building the code for each of the selected agents.

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section4 .RenderAsSection4 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
##### Supplier Matching Agent {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

**Purpose**: Matches and [finds]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58}
the best supplier against the user-provided score threshold.

::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par195 .Para}
*supplier_logic.py* file code block:

:::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC1 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Single Responsibility: Supplier Matching Logic
:::

::: FixedLine
class SupplierMatcher:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        # define some fictional suppliers, their locations along with
their scores
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.suppliers = \[
:::

::: FixedLine
                            {\"name\": \"Supplier A\", \"score\": 95,
\"location\": \"India\"},
:::

::: FixedLine
                            {\"name\": \"Supplier B\", \"score\": 90,
\"location\": \"China\"},
:::

::: FixedLine
                            {\"name\": \"Supplier C\", \"score\": 88,
\"location\": \"USA\"},
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \]
:::
::::::::::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def find_best_supplier(self, threshold):
:::

::: FixedLine
        # Logic to match the best supplier based on threshold
:::

::: FixedLine
        best_supplier = max(\[s for s in self.suppliers if
s\[\"score\"\] \>= threshold\], key=lambda x: x\[\"score\"\],
default=None)
:::

::: FixedLine
        return best_supplier
:::
:::::::
::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB104 .pagebreak aria-label="104"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this code block, we have defined a small custom
dataset to represent a few dummy suppliers with attributes such as
locations and a threshold. This score can be derived from factors such
as their audit performance, customer reviews, or popularity.

The function ***find_best_supplier*** returns the best matched supplier
whose threshold value exceeds user specified threshold.

We have kept the code simple for demonstration purposes. You may extend
this logic to integrate with your own custom function, adding more
advanced criteria based on your use case requirements of the best
matching supplier.

:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par199 .Para}
*app.py* file code block:

::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC2 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from fastapi import FastAPI
:::

::: FixedLine
import uvicorn
:::

::: FixedLine
from supplier_logic import SupplierMatcher
:::
:::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
app = FastAPI(title=\"Supplier Matching Agent\", version=\"1.0\",
contact={\"author\": \"Vikas Sinha\"})
:::
::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# We are exposing this agent as FAST API endpoint to be consumed
directly in orchestrator (i.e. centralized orchestration) or from any
other agent (i.e. decentralized orchestration)
:::

::: FixedLine
\@app.post(\"/match-supplier\")
:::
:::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
def match_supplier(criteria: dict):
:::

::: FixedLine
    agent = SupplierMatcher()
:::

::: FixedLine
    threshold = criteria.get(\"score_threshold\", 75)
:::

::: FixedLine
    if threshold is None:
:::

::: FixedLine
        return {\'error\': \'score_threshold parameter is required.
Example, {\"score_threshold\" : 75}\'}
:::
::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    best_supplier = agent.find_best_supplier(int(threshold)) \# type:
ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
    return {\"best_supplier\": best_supplier}
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
if \_\_name\_\_ == \"\_\_main\_\_\":
:::

::: FixedLine
    uvicorn.run(app, host=\"localhost\", port=int(os.getenv(\"PORT\",
8000)))
:::
:::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB105 .pagebreak aria-label="105"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this file, we expose our supplier matching agent
as FAST API endpoint to be consumed directly by orchestrator (i.e.,
[centralized orchestration]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59}) or
from any other agent (i.e., [decentralized
orchestration]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60}).

The ***match_supplier*** method accepts threshold value from the user
and invokes ***find_best_supplier*** method, defined in the
supplier_logic.py file to get the best matching supplier in return. The
name of this FAST API endpoint is "Supplier Matching Agent."

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par202 .Para}
*tool.py* file code block:

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC3 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import requests
:::

::: FixedLine
import json
:::

::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from dotenv import load_dotenv
:::
:::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
def supplier_matching_tool_func(score_threshold):
:::

::: FixedLine
    load_dotenv()
:::

::: FixedLine
    url = os.getenv(\'predict_supplier_uri\')  # Replace with your AKS
service endpoint for production in the environment file
:::

::: FixedLine
    response = requests.post(url, json={\"score_threshold\":
score_threshold}) \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
    if response.status_code == 200:
:::

::: FixedLine
        output = response.json()\[\'best_supplier\'\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        return json.dumps({\"best_supplier\": output, \"Error\": None})
:::

::: FixedLine
    else:
:::

::: FixedLine
        return json.dumps({\"best_supplier\": None, \"Error\":
response.text})
:::
::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Interface Segregation
:::

::: FixedLine
supplier_matching_tool = {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"type\": \"function\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"function\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"name\": \"supplier_matching_tool_func\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"Matches and finds the best supplier
against the user provided score threshold.\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"parameters\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"type\": \"object\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"properties\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \"score_threshold\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"type\": \"string\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"description\": \'\'\'The score threshold
provided by the user, e.g. 75\'\'\',
:::

::: FixedLine
                        },
:::

::: FixedLine
                    },
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"required\": \[\"score_threshold\"\],
:::

::: FixedLine
                },
:::

::: FixedLine
            }
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB106 .pagebreak aria-label="106"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this file, we define the agentic AI tool
function which will be invoked by the orchestrator (basis the planning
schedule) as required. The ***supplier_matching_tool_func*** is the
method that calls the deployed endpoint of the supplier matching agent.
The variable \'predict_supplier_uri' is the actual URI of the deployed
supplier matching agent. This URI can be stored in an environment
variable, Azure Key Vault or any other secure location and fetched from
there. Since this code is written for demo purposes only, we choose to
hard-code it in this file itself, but it's not recommended as it will
lead to frequent updates in the code whenever the endpoint or its
versions change.

An important variable which is defined in this file is
supplier_matching_tool. Since we want to use this agent as one of the
agentic tools, this file is where we register it by defining it as a
global variable which will be passed as an input to the underlying LLM
defined in the orchestrator layer.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par205 .Para}
The variable is a JSON object with following key fields:

::: UnorderedList
- Type

- Function
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par208 .Para}
The "*function"* field contains the following key-value pair:

::: UnorderedList
- **Name**: Name of the function to be executed whenever this tool is
  selected by the LLM.

- **Description**: A brief description of what this function does.

- **Parameters**: Definition of the function's input parameters
  including their description and data type.
:::
::::

::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par212 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB107 .pagebreak aria-label="107"
role="doc-pagebreak"}*requirements.txt* code block:

:::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC4 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
fastapi
:::

::: FixedLine
uvicorn
:::

::: FixedLine
pandas
:::

::: FixedLine
numpy
:::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par213 .Para}
This file contains all the dependencies required for this agent to
execute.

:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC5 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Dockerfile code block
:::
::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Use Python slim image
:::

::: FixedLine
FROM python:3.10-slim
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Set the working directory
:::

::: FixedLine
WORKDIR /app
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Copy the requirements file
:::

::: FixedLine
COPY requirements.txt .
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Install dependencies
:::

::: FixedLine
RUN pip install \--no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Copy the agent code
:::

::: FixedLine
COPY . .
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Expose the port where the agent will run
:::

::: FixedLine
EXPOSE 8000
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Run the app
:::

::: FixedLine
CMD \[\"python\", \"app.py\"\]
:::
:::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::

This file contains the code to generate the docker image of the agent so
that it could be deployed in isolation using deployment platforms such
as Kubernetes.
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section4 .RenderAsSection4 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
##### Price Prediction Agent {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

**Purpose**: Predicts the [price]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61}
of raw materials based on the input day which must be one of the valid
values, i.e., 1, 2, or 3.

:::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par216 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB108 .pagebreak aria-label="108"
role="doc-pagebreak"}*price_logic.py* file code block:

::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC6 .ProgramCode}
::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
:::

::: FixedLine
import numpy as np
:::
:::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class PricePredictionAgent:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, historical_data):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.model = LinearRegression()
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.historical_data = historical_data
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.\_train_model()
:::
::::::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_train_model(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        days = np.array(\[data\[0\] for data in
self.historical_data\]).reshape(-1, 1)
:::

::: FixedLine
        prices = np.array(\[data\[1\] for data in
self.historical_data\])
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.model.fit(days, prices)
:::
:::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def predict_price(self, day):
:::

::: FixedLine
        predicted_price = self.model.predict(\[\[day\]\])\[0\] \# type:
ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
        return round(predicted_price, 2)
:::
::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::

In this code, we have written a simple custom logic using scikit-learn
[linear regression model]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm62} to
predict the price based on the day input. In the \_\_init\_\_ method, we
would be training the model on a fictional historical dataset. The
predict_price method is then used to perform real-time price prediction
based on the input day.

You can extend this logic to implement your own custom price prediction
depending on your use case and data.

:::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par219 .Para}
*app.py* file code block:

::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC7 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from fastapi import FastAPI
:::

::: FixedLine
import uvicorn
:::

::: FixedLine
from price_logic import PricePredictionAgent
:::
:::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
app = FastAPI(title=\"Price Prediction Agent\", version=\"1.0\",
contact={\"author\": \"Vikas Sinha\"})
:::
::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\@app.post(\"/predict-price\")
:::

::: FixedLine
def predict_price(data: dict):
:::

::: FixedLine
    historical_price_day_wise = \[(1, 100), (2, 105), (3, 110)\]
:::

::: FixedLine
    agent = PricePredictionAgent(historical_price_day_wise)
:::

::: FixedLine
    day = data.get(\'day\')
:::

::: FixedLine
    if day is None:
:::

::: FixedLine
        return {\'error\': \'Day parameter is required. Example,
{\"day\" : 3}\'}
:::

::: FixedLine
    predicted_price = agent.predict_price(int(day))
:::

::: FixedLine
    return {\'predicted_price\': predicted_price}
:::
::::::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
if \_\_name\_\_ == \"\_\_main\_\_\":
:::

::: FixedLine
    uvicorn.run(app, host=\"localhost\", port=int(os.getenv(\"PORT\",
8001)))
:::
:::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB109 .pagebreak aria-label="109"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this file, we expose our price prediction agent
as a FAST API endpoint, which can be consumed directly by orchestrators
(i.e., [centralized
orchestration]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm63}) or by any other
agents (i.e., [decentralized
orchestration]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm64}).

The historical_price_day_wise variable is a JSON object comprising of
day-price pair values which is used to train the underlying [linear
regression model]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm65} at the time of
initialization of the agent.

Please note that we are doing the model initialization only for demo
purposes; it is not recommended in actual use case implementation. In
actual implementation, you should create a separate "train.py" file
where you can write the complete price prediction model training and
evaluation code which can then be saved/registered as .pkl file. Then,
within the "***predict_price***" method, you can simply download the
model.pkl file and use it for prediction/inference. There are many other
custom approaches that could be followed to train the model and for
prediction.

The predict_price method accepts user input as a dictionary and passes
it to the logic defined in the price_logic.py file to return the
predicted price. The FAST API endpoint here is named as "Price
Prediction Agent."

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par224 .Para}
*tool.py* file code block:

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC8 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import requests
:::

::: FixedLine
import json
:::

::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from dotenv import load_dotenv
:::
:::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
def price_prediction_tool_func(day):
:::

::: FixedLine
    load_dotenv()
:::

::: FixedLine
    url = os.getenv(\'predict_price_uri\')  # Replace with your AKS
service endpoint for production
:::

::: FixedLine
    response = requests.post(url, json={\"day\": day}) \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
    if response.status_code == 200:
:::

::: FixedLine
        output = response.json()\[\'predicted_price\'\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        return json.dumps({\"predicted_price\": output, \"Error\":
None})
:::

::: FixedLine
    else:
:::

::: FixedLine
        return json.dumps({\"predicted_price\": None, \"Error\":
response.text})
:::
::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
price_prediction_tool = {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"type\": \"function\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"function\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"name\": \"price_prediction_tool_func\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"Predicts the price of raw materials
based on the input day: either 1 or 2 or 3 as valid values\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"parameters\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"type\": \"object\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"properties\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \"day\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"type\": \"string\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"description\": \'\'\'The day number for
which price needs to be predicted, e.g. 1\'\'\',
:::

::: FixedLine
                        },
:::

::: FixedLine
                    },
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"required\": \[\"day\"\],
:::

::: FixedLine
                },
:::

::: FixedLine
            }
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::
::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB110 .pagebreak aria-label="110"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this file, we define the agentic AI tool
function which will be invoked whenever required by the orchestrator,
based on the planning schedule. The price_prediction_tool_func is the
method which invokes the deployed endpoint of the price prediction
agent. The \'predict_price_uri' variable holds the URI of the deployed
price prediction agent. As we mentioned above though we have hardcoded
the variable in the file for demo purposes, it\'s always recommended to
store this in an environment variable or any secure location and fetch
it from there.

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB111 .pagebreak aria-label="111"
role="doc-pagebreak"}An important variable which is defined in this file
is price_prediction_tool. As we did earlier since we want to utilize
this agent as one of the "agentic tools," it is in this file we define
it as a global variable which will then be passed as an input to the
underlying LLM defined in the orchestrator layer.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par227 .Para}
This is a JSON variable comprising of the following two fields:

::: UnorderedList
- Type

- Function
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par230 .Para}
where "*function*" contains the following key-value pair:

::: UnorderedList
- **Name**: This contains the name of the function to be executed
  whenever this tool is invoked by the LLM.

- **Description**: This stores the description of what this function can
  do.

- **Parameters**: Defines all the input variables to the function along
  with its description and type.
:::
::::

:::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par234 .Para}
*requirements.txt* code block:

::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC9 .ProgramCode}

fastapi
:::

::: FixedLine
uvicorn
:::

::: FixedLine
scikit-learn
:::

::: FixedLine
pandas
:::

::: FixedLine
numpy
:::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par235 .Para}
As we seen before, this file holds all the dependencies which are
required by this agent to execute.

:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC10 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup

\# Dockerfile code block
:::
::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Use Python slim image
:::

::: FixedLine
FROM python:3.10-slim
:::
:::::

:::

WORKDIR /app
:::

:::

:::

RUN pip install \--no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
:::
:::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB112 .pagebreak aria-label="112"
role="doc-pagebreak"}This file has the code to generate the docker image
of an agent which can be used for deployment using platforms such as
Kubernetes.
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section4 .RenderAsSection4 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
##### Predictive Maintenance Agent {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

**Purpose**: Predicts whether
[maintenance]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm66} is required for a
machine based on its sensor data.

::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par238 .Para}
*maintenance_logic.py* file code block:

:::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC11 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
:::
::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class PredictiveMaintenanceAgent:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, training_data, labels):
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        Initializes the agent with training data and labels.
:::

::: FixedLine
        :param training_data: 2D array of sensor readings.
:::

::: FixedLine
        :param labels: 1D array of labels (0: No Failure, 1: Failure).
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.model = RandomForestClassifier()
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.models.fit(training_data, labels)
:::
::::::::::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def predict_failure(self, sensor_data):
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        Predicts whether a machine might fail based on sensor readings.
:::

::: FixedLine
        :param sensor_data: 1D array of sensor readings.
:::

::: FixedLine
        :return: 1 (Failure) or 0 (No Failure).
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        prediction = self.model.predict(\[sensor_data\])\[0\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        return prediction
:::
:::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB113 .pagebreak aria-label="113"
role="doc-pagebreak"}As we mentioned above, this logic is a simple
custom code for demo purposes only. You can extend or customize the
logic based on your requirements and data. In this logic, we used
scikit-learn's random forest classifier model to predict whether a
machine requires maintenance or not. In the \_\_init\_\_ method, the
model is trained on a fictional 2D array representing historical sensor
data. The predict_failure method is used to predict in real time whether
the machine needs maintenance or not.

::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par240 .Para}
*app.py* file code block:

:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC12 .ProgramCode}
:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from fastapi import FastAPI
:::

::: FixedLine
import uvicorn
:::

::: FixedLine
import json
:::

::: FixedLine
from maintenance_logic import PredictiveMaintenanceAgent
:::
::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
app = FastAPI(title=\"Predictive Maintenance Agent\", version=\"1.0\",
contact={\"author\": \"Vikas Sinha\"})
:::
::::

:::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\@app.post(\"/predict-maintenance\")
:::

::: FixedLine
def predict_maintenance(machine_data: dict):
:::

::: FixedLine
    training_data = \[\[70, 30, 50\], \[60, 25, 45\], \[80, 35,
55\]\]  # Example sensor readings
:::

::: FixedLine
    labels = \[0, 0, 1\]  # Corresponding labels
:::

::: FixedLine
    agent = PredictiveMaintenanceAgent(training_data, labels)
:::

::: FixedLine
    input_data = machine_data.get(\'sensor_data\')
:::

::: FixedLine
    if input_data is None:
:::

::: FixedLine
        return {\'error\': \'sensor_data parameter is required. Example,
{\"sensor_data\" : \"\[75, 28, 52\]\"}\'}
:::

::: FixedLine
    parsed_data = json.loads(input_data)
:::

::: FixedLine
    failure = agent.predict_failure(parsed_data)
:::

::: FixedLine
    return {\"needs_maintenance\": int(failure)}
:::
::::::::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
if \_\_name\_\_ == \"\_\_main\_\_\":
:::

::: FixedLine
    uvicorn.run(app, host=\"localhost\", port=int(os.getenv(\"PORT\",
8002)))
:::
:::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::

In this file, we expose our predictive maintenance agent as FAST API
endpoint, which can be consumed either by orchestrator (i.e.,
[centralized orchestration]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm67}) or by
any other agent (i.e., [decentralized
orchestration]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm68}).

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB114 .pagebreak aria-label="114"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The training_data is a 2D array which is used to
train the underlying [random forest classifier
model]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm69} at the time of
initialization of the agent. As previously mentioned, it is not
recommended to train the model during initialization. Instead, the model
should be trained separately in a dedicated "train.py" file where you
can write your complete prediction model training and evaluation code
and register/save it as a .pkl file, and in the "***predict_failure***"
method, you may simply download the model.pkl file and use it for
prediction.

The ***predict_maintenance*** method accepts sensor data as user input
as showcased in the above code and invokes the predict_failure, defined
in the maintenance_logic.py file to generate the prediction. The name of
this FAST API endpoint is "Predictive Maintenance Agent."

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par244 .Para}
*tool.py* file code block:

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC13 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import requests
:::

::: FixedLine
import json
:::

::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from dotenv import load_dotenv
:::
:::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
def predict_maintenance_tool_func(sensor_data):
:::

::: FixedLine
    load_dotenv()
:::

::: FixedLine
    url = os.getenv(\'predict_maintenance_uri\')  # Replace with your
AKS service endpoint for production
:::

::: FixedLine
    response = requests.post(url, json={\"sensor_data\": sensor_data})
\# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
    if response.status_code == 200:
:::

::: FixedLine
        output = response.json()\[\'needs_maintenance\'\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        return json.dumps({\"needs_maintenance\": output, \"Error\":
None})
:::

::: FixedLine
    else:
:::

::: FixedLine
        return json.dumps({\"needs_maintenance\": None, \"Error\":
response.text})
:::
::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
predict_maintenance_tool = {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"type\": \"function\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"function\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"name\": \"predict_maintenance_tool_func\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"Predicts whether maintenance is
required on the machine or not by reading it\'s sensor data\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"parameters\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"type\": \"object\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"properties\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \"sensor_data\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"type\": \"string\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"description\": \'\'\'The sensor data e.g.
\[75, 28, 52\]\'\'\',
:::

::: FixedLine
                        },
:::

::: FixedLine
                    },
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"required\": \[\"sensor_data\"\],
:::

::: FixedLine
                },
:::

::: FixedLine
            }
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::
::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB115 .pagebreak aria-label="115"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this file, we define the agentic AI tool
function which will be invoked by the orchestrator as required based on
its planning schedule. The ***predict_maintenance_tool_func*** is the
method which invokes the deployed endpoint of the agent. The
\'predict_maintenance_uri\' variable stores the URI of the deployed
maintenance prediction agent. As we have mentioned above, it\'s always
recommended to store this in an environment variable of similar secure
location instead of hard coding it.

Like in all the above codes we covered, in this code as well, we have
defined the global variable predict_maintenance_tool which will be
passed as an input to the underlying LLM defined within the orchestrator
layer. The structure remains the same as described in previous files
such as price_prediction_tool or supplier_matching_tool, a JSON object
with key fields: type and function. As covered earlier, the function
field follows the same format and contains the function name, a
description of what it\'s capable of, and the input parameters along
with their types and descriptions.

::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par248 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB116 .pagebreak aria-label="116"
role="doc-pagebreak"}As we already described in previous agent
implementations, this file contains all the dependencies required for
this agent to execute.
