As we described for the other agents, this file contains code to
generate the docker image for the agent enabling it to be deployed in
isolation using any deployment services such as Kubernetes.
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section4 .RenderAsSection4 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}
##### Sentiment Analysis Agent {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12Heading .Heading}

**Purpose**: Determines if the sentiments in the user-provided comments
[related]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm70} to the machine is
positive, negative, or neutral.

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par251 .Para}
*sentiment_logic.py* file code block:

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC16 .ProgramCode}
:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import os, sys
:::

::: FixedLine
from dotenv import load_dotenv
:::

::: FixedLine
from azure.identity import ClientSecretCredential
:::

::: FixedLine
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
:::

::: FixedLine
from openai import AzureOpenAI
:::
::::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class SentimentAnalysisAgent:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        load_dotenv()
:::

::: FixedLine
        tenant_id = os.getenv(\'tenant_id\')
:::

::: FixedLine
        client_id = os.getenv(\'client_id\')
:::

::: FixedLine
        client_secret = os.getenv(\'client_secret\')
:::

::: FixedLine
        key_vault_uri = os.getenv(\'key_vault_uri\')
:::
::::::::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        # Initialize a ClientSecretCredential object
:::

::: FixedLine
        try:
:::

::: FixedLine
            credential = ClientSecretCredential(tenant_id, client_id,
client_secret) \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
        except Exception as ex:
:::

::: FixedLine
            print(f\'Ensure proper service principal details are
provided. Current except details: {ex}\')
:::

::: FixedLine
            sys.exit()
:::
:::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        # Initialize a SecretClient object
:::

::: FixedLine
        secret_client = SecretClient(vault_url=key_vault_uri,
credential=credential) \# type: ignore
:::
:::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        azure_endpoint =
secret_client.get_secret(\"AzureOpenAIEndpoint\").value
:::

::: FixedLine
        api_key = secret_client.get_secret(\"AzureOpenAIKey\").value
:::

::: FixedLine
        api_version =
secret_client.get_secret(\"AzureOpenAIVersion\").value
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.deployment_id =
secret_client.get_secret(\"AzureOpenAIDeploymentId\").value
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.openai_client = AzureOpenAI(azure_endpoint= azure_endpoint,
api_key= api_key, api_version= api_version) \# type: ignore
:::
::::::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def analyze_sentiment(self, comments):
:::

::: FixedLine
        messages = \[
:::

::: FixedLine
        {\"role\": \"system\", \"content\": \'\'\'You\'re an AI
assistant designed to identify the sentiments from the given text.
:::

::: FixedLine
         You will be provided the user input comments and your task to
best determine the sentiment out of it.\'\'\'},
:::

::: FixedLine
        {\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": comments}
:::

::: FixedLine
        \]
:::
:::::::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        response = self.openai_client.chat.completions.create(
:::

::: FixedLine
            model=self.deployment_id, \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
            messages=messages, \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
            temperature= 0.2
:::

::: FixedLine
        )
:::
::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        # Process the model\'s response
:::

::: FixedLine
        sentiment = response.choices\[0\].message
:::

::: FixedLine
        return sentiment.content
:::
::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB118 .pagebreak aria-label="118"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this logic, we will be using Azure OpenAI's
large language model to determine the sentiment of the input comments.
In the \_\_init\_\_ method, we initialized the Azure OpenAI client,
which is then used to make a chat completion call within the
analyze_sentiment method. The system and user prompts are defined as
shown in the code. The user-provided machine-related comments are passed
to the "user" in the prompt. The Azure OpenAI model's response is
captured and passed back to the caller function. The Azure OpenAI model
we are using here is GPT-4o. As mentioned above, we have kept the code
simple for demo purposes, and you can extend or customize this logic to
implement alternative sentiment analysis strategy based on your use
case.

::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par253 .Para}
*app.py* file code block:

:::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC17 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from fastapi import FastAPI
:::

::: FixedLine
import uvicorn
:::

::: FixedLine
from sentiment_logic import SentimentAnalysisAgent
:::
:::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
app = FastAPI(title=\"Sentiment Analysis Agent\", version=\"1.0\",
contact={\"author\": \"Vikas Sinha\"})
:::
::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\@app.post(\"/analyze-sentiment\")
:::

::: FixedLine
def analyze_sentiment(feedback: dict):
:::

::: FixedLine
    agent = SentimentAnalysisAgent()
:::

::: FixedLine
    comments = feedback.get(\"comments\")
:::

::: FixedLine
    if comments is None:
:::

::: FixedLine
        return {\'error\': \'comments parameter is required. Example,
{\"comments\" : \"This is a good tea.\"}\'}
:::

::: FixedLine
    sentiment = agent.analyze_sentiment(comments)
:::

::: FixedLine
    return {\"sentiment\": sentiment}
:::
:::::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
if \_\_name\_\_ == \"\_\_main\_\_\":
:::

::: FixedLine
    uvicorn.run(app, host=\"localhost\", port=int(os.getenv(\"PORT\",
8003)))
:::
:::::
::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB119 .pagebreak aria-label="119"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this file, we expose our sentiment analysis
agent as FAST API endpoint which can be consumed either by an
orchestrator (i.e., [centralized
orchestration]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm71}) or by other agents
(i.e., [decentralized
orchestration]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm72}) based on the
orchestration setup.

The feedback parameter is a dictionary object which holds the users
comments about the machine which is passed to the logic for evaluation.
The name of this FAST API endpoint is "Sentiment Analysis Agent."

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par256 .Para}
*tool.py* file code block:

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC18 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import requests
:::

::: FixedLine
import json
:::

::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from dotenv import load_dotenv
:::
:::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
def sentiment_analysis_tool_func(comments):
:::

::: FixedLine
    load_dotenv()
:::

::: FixedLine
    url = os.getenv(\'predict_sentiments_uri\')  # Replace with your AKS
service endpoint for production
:::

::: FixedLine
    response = requests.post(url, json={\"comments\": comments}) \#
type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
    if response.status_code == 200:
:::

::: FixedLine
        output = response.json()\[\'sentiment\'\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        return json.dumps({\"sentiment\": output, \"Error\": None})
:::

::: FixedLine
    else:
:::

::: FixedLine
        return json.dumps({\"sentiment\": None, \"Error\":
response.text})
:::
::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
sentiment_tool = {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"type\": \"function\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"function\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"name\": \"sentiment_analysis_tool_func\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"Determines if the sentiments are
positive or negative or neutral around the machine from the user
provided comments\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"parameters\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"type\": \"object\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"properties\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \"comments\": {
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"type\": \"string\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"description\": \'\'\'The comments provided
by the user\'\'\',
:::

::: FixedLine
                        },
:::

::: FixedLine
                    },
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"required\": \[\"comments\"\],
:::

::: FixedLine
                },
:::

::: FixedLine
            }
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::
::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB120 .pagebreak aria-label="120"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In this file, we define the agentic AI tool
function which will be invoked by the orchestrator as required based on
the planning schedule. The sentiment_analysis_tool_func is the method
which invokes the deployed endpoint of the agent. The
\'predict_sentiments_uri\' holds the actual URI of the deployed agent.
As mentioned earlier, the hard coding here is done for demo purposes; in
actual development, you must always store it in an environment variable,
Azure Vault, or similar secure places and invoke as needed. The key
variable which is defined here in this file is sentiment_too which
registers this agent as one of the "agentic tools." This global variable
will be then passed as an input to the underlying LLM in the
orchestrator layer.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par258 .Para}
The structure of the variable follows the same format; it is a JSON
variable with following fields:

::: UnorderedList
- Type

- Function
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par261 .Para}
where "*function*" in turn contains the following key-value pair:

::: UnorderedList
- **Name**: The function's name as used by the LLM

- **Description**: Brief description explaining the functionality

- **Parameters**: Lists all the input fields to the function including
  their description and type
:::
::::

:::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par265 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB121 .pagebreak aria-label="121"
role="doc-pagebreak"}*requirements.txt* code block:

::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC19 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
fastapi
:::

::: FixedLine
uvicorn
:::

::: FixedLine
pandas
:::

::: FixedLine
numpy
:::

::: FixedLine
openai
:::

::: FixedLine
azure-identity
:::

::: FixedLine
azure-keyvault-secrets
:::
::::::::::
:::::::::::
::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par266 .Para}
As seen above for other agents, this file contains all the dependencies
required by this agent to execute.

::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC20 .ProgramCode}
:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Dockerfile code block
:::

::: FixedLine
\# Use Python slim image
:::

::: FixedLine
FROM python:3.10-slim
:::
::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Set the working directory
:::

::: FixedLine
WORKDIR /app
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Copy the requirements file
:::

::: FixedLine
COPY requirements.txt .
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Install dependencies
:::

::: FixedLine
RUN pip install \--no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Copy the agent code
:::

::: FixedLine
COPY . .
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Expose the port where the agent will run
:::

::: FixedLine
EXPOSE 8003
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Run the app
:::

::: FixedLine
CMD \[\"python\", \"app.py\"\]
:::
:::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::

In line with the approach outlined for the other agents, this file
contains the code to build the docker image for the sentiment analysis
agent so that it could be deployed in isolation through any deployment
platform such as Kubernetes.
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec13Heading"}
#### Orchestrator {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB122 .pagebreak aria-label="122"
role="doc-pagebreak"}With the individual agents defined and exposed via
its own API lets now focus on the
[orchestrator]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm73}[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm74}
layer. It is the orchestrator which will manage the execution of the
tools. The orchestrator is LLM-based powered by Azure OpenAI GPT-4o
model.

::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par269 .Para}
In the ***orchestrator.py*** file, we begin with importing all essential
libraries and tools shown as below:

:::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC21 .ProgramCode}
::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import os, sys
:::

::: FixedLine
import json
:::

::: FixedLine
from dotenv import load_dotenv
:::

::: FixedLine
from azure.identity import ClientSecretCredential
:::

::: FixedLine
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
:::

::: FixedLine
from openai import AzureOpenAI
:::
:::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
sys.path.append(os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(\_\_file\_\_),
\"../agents\")))
:::
::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
from supplier_matching_agent.tool import supplier_matching_tool,
supplier_matching_tool_func
:::

::: FixedLine
from price_prediction_agent.tool import price_prediction_tool,
price_prediction_tool_func
:::

::: FixedLine
from predictive_maintenance_agent.tool import predict_maintenance_tool,
predict_maintenance_tool_func
:::

::: FixedLine
from sentiment_analysis_agent.tool import
sentiment_tool,sentiment_analysis_tool_func
:::
:::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
load_dotenv()
:::
::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
tenant_id = os.getenv(\'tenant_id\')
:::

::: FixedLine
client_id = os.getenv(\'client_id\')
:::

::: FixedLine
client_secret = os.getenv(\'client_secret\')
:::

::: FixedLine
key_vault_uri = os.getenv(\'key_vault_uri\')
:::
:::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par270 .Para}
As you may notice in this code, all the tools which we defined above in
their respective modules have been imported into this file. The
***load_dotenv()*** method reads the environment file variables which
are tenant id, client id, client secret, and key vault
uri.[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB123 .pagebreak aria-label="123"
role="doc-pagebreak"}

::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC22 .ProgramCode}
::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Initialize a ClientSecretCredential object
:::

::: FixedLine
try:
:::

::: FixedLine
    credential = ClientSecretCredential(tenant_id, client_id,
client_secret) \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
except Exception as ex:
:::

::: FixedLine
    print(f\'Ensure proper service principal details are provided.
Current except details: {ex}\')
:::

::: FixedLine
    sys.exit()
:::
:::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Initialize a SecretClient object
:::

::: FixedLine
secret_client = SecretClient(vault_url=key_vault_uri,
credential=credential) \# type: ignore
:::
:::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
azure_endpoint = secret_client.get_secret(\"AzureOpenAIEndpoint\").value
:::

::: FixedLine
api_key = secret_client.get_secret(\"AzureOpenAIKey\").value
:::

::: FixedLine
api_version = secret_client.get_secret(\"AzureOpenAIVersion\").value
:::

::: FixedLine
deployment_id =
secret_client.get_secret(\"AzureOpenAIDeploymentId\").value
:::

::: FixedLine
openai_client = AzureOpenAI(azure_endpoint= azure_endpoint, api_key=
api_key, api_version= api_version) \# type: ignore
:::
::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::

In the section above, the client credentials are prepared and Azure
OpenAI object is created which will be used in subsequent method to make
GPT-based calls.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par272 .Para}
The core is defined in the run_workflow method which defines two OpenAI
calls:

::: UnorderedList
- ***Planning call*** to instruct the model on what tasks to be done and
  provide it with details of tools which are available. The purpose of
  this call is to enable the model to develop its internal plan and
  decide which tool to use and when.

- The next ***OpenAI call*** is the execution call which will actually
  invoke the tools based on the plan and consolidate all the outputs to
  produce the final response from the model.
:::
::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par275 .Para}
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC23 .ProgramCode}
:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
def run_workflow(system_prompt, prompt):
:::

::: FixedLine
    messages = \[
:::

::: FixedLine
        {\"role\": \"system\", \"content\": system_prompt},
:::

::: FixedLine
        {\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": prompt}
:::

::: FixedLine
        \]
:::
::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    tools = \[supplier_matching_tool, price_prediction_tool,
predict_maintenance_tool, sentiment_tool\]
:::
::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    # First API call: Ask the model to use the function
:::

::: FixedLine
    response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
:::

::: FixedLine
            model=deployment_id, \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
            messages=messages, \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
            tools=tools,
:::

::: FixedLine
            tool_choice=\"auto\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            temperature= 0
:::

::: FixedLine
        )
:::
:::::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    # Process the model\'s response
:::

::: FixedLine
    response_message = response.choices\[0\].message
:::

::: FixedLine
    messages.append(response_message) \# type: ignore
:::
::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    print(\"Model\'s response:\")
:::

::: FixedLine
    print(response_message)
:::
:::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    # Handle function calls
:::

::: FixedLine
    if response_message.tool_calls:
:::

::: FixedLine
        for tool_call in response_message.tool_calls:
:::

::: FixedLine
            function_name = tool_call.function.name
:::

::: FixedLine
            function_args = json.loads(tool_call.function.arguments)
:::

::: FixedLine
            print(f\"Function call: {function_name}\")
:::

::: FixedLine
            print(f\"Function arguments: {function_args}\")
:::

::: FixedLine
            if function_name == \"supplier_matching_tool_func\":
:::

::: FixedLine
                response = supplier_matching_tool_func(
:::

::: FixedLine
                        score_threshold=function_args.get(\"score_threshold\")
:::

::: FixedLine
                    )
:::

::: FixedLine
            elif function_name == \"price_prediction_tool_func\":
:::

::: FixedLine
                response = price_prediction_tool_func(
:::

::: FixedLine
                        day=function_args.get(\"day\")
:::

::: FixedLine
                    )
:::

::: FixedLine
            elif function_name == \"predict_maintenance_tool_func\":
:::

::: FixedLine
                response = predict_maintenance_tool_func(
:::

::: FixedLine
                        sensor_data=function_args.get(\"sensor_data\")
:::

::: FixedLine
                    )
:::

::: FixedLine
            elif function_name == \"sentiment_analysis_tool_func\":
:::

::: FixedLine
                response = sentiment_analysis_tool_func(
:::

::: FixedLine
                        comments=function_args.get(\"comments\")
:::

::: FixedLine
                    )
:::

::: FixedLine
            else:
:::

::: FixedLine
                response = json.dumps({\"error\": \"Unknown function\"})
:::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
            messages.append({
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"tool_call_id\": tool_call.id,
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"role\": \"tool\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"name\": function_name,
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"content\": response,
:::

::: FixedLine
            })
:::

::: FixedLine
    else:
:::

::: FixedLine
        print(\"No tool calls were made by the model.\")
:::
:::::::::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    # Get the final response from the model for all the messages
appended so far from each of the tool
:::

::: FixedLine
    final_response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
:::

::: FixedLine
            model=deployment_id, \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
            messages=messages, \# type: ignore
:::

::: FixedLine
        )
:::
::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    return final_response.choices\[0\].message.content
:::
::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB125 .pagebreak aria-label="125"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Let us review the above code in detail now. To
begin with, a message body is created which will be sent to the LLM.
This message body consists of a **system_prompt** and a **user_prompt**.
You may notice that later in this method, the messages get appended with
multiple other information. We will discuss in the upcoming section the
importance of a well-crafted and elaborate prompt with examples. Next, a
**tools** variable is defined to hold the list of all available tools.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par277 .Para}
Following this, the ***chat completion API*** call is made via the
"create" method by passing in key parameters which includes

::: UnorderedList
- Deployment ID of the GPT-4o model

- Message

- Tools

- Tool selection method (e.g., \"auto\")

- Model tuning parameters such as temperature
:::
::::

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB126 .pagebreak aria-label="126"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The response includes a ***tools_call*** list which
we read to extract the function's name and its arguments.

For each name, the underlying function is identified and invoked by
passing the required arguments from function_args JSON variable. The
outcome from each of these functions is stored in the "response"
variable. The messages variable is then appended with following
additional details: tool call id, the tool name, the function name, and
its response.

Finally, toward the end, a final Azure OpenAI API call is prepared by
passing in the entire context of the tools, functions to invoke, their
potential outcomes, system prompt, and user prompt. All of these are
consolidated into the messages object.

:::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par286 .Para}
*requirements.txt* file content would be

::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC24 .ProgramCode}
:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
requests
:::

::: FixedLine
python-dotenv
:::

::: FixedLine
azure-identity
:::

::: FixedLine
azure-keyvault
:::

::: FixedLine
Openai
:::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par287 .Para}
Docker file content will be as below:

::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC25 .ProgramCode}
::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Use Python slim image
:::

::: FixedLine
FROM python:3.10-slim
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Set the working directory
:::

::: FixedLine
WORKDIR /app
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Copy the requirements file
:::

::: FixedLine
COPY requirements.txt .
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Install dependencies
:::

::: FixedLine
RUN pip install \--no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Copy the orchestrator code
:::

::: FixedLine
COPY . .
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Run the orchestrator
:::

::: FixedLine
CMD \[\"python\", \"orchestrator.py\"\]
:::
:::::
:::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par288 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB127 .pagebreak aria-label="127"
role="doc-pagebreak"}*The* ***docker-compose.yml*** file would look like
something below:

::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC26 .ProgramCode}
:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
version: \"3.8\"
:::

::: FixedLine
services:
:::

::: FixedLine
  supplier_matching_agent:
:::

::: FixedLine
    build: ./agents/supplier_matching_agent
:::

::: FixedLine
    ports: \[\"8000:8000\"\]
:::
::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  price_prediction_agent:
:::

::: FixedLine
    build: ./agents/price_prediction_agent
:::

::: FixedLine
    ports: \[\"8001:8001\"\]
:::
::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  predictive_maintenance_agent:
:::

::: FixedLine
    build: ./agents/predictive_maintenance_agent
:::

::: FixedLine
    ports: \[\"8002:8002\"\]
:::
::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
  sentiment_analysis_agent:
:::

::: FixedLine
    build: ./agents/sentiment_analysis_agent
:::

::: FixedLine
    ports: \[\"8003:8003\"\]
:::
::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::

Having looked at the design and implementation of individual agents and
the orchestrator, the next important component is prompt design. The
effectiveness of the orchestrator and the entire agentic system heavily
depends on how well the prompt is crafted.
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec14Heading"}
#### Role of Prompt {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14Heading .Heading}

The agent will only [perform]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm75} well
if you are able to provide the system with a well-constructed definition
of available tools, along with clearly articulated expected outputs,
either in the form of planned actions or raw responses. The GPT model
family, especially Azure OpenAI's GPT-4o which we are leveraging in our
examples, performs better when the input prompts are formatted and
structured as JSON. [System
prompt]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm76} is the most important
element in the prompt message to the LLM as it serves as a blueprint
which governs the behavior and understanding of the model. Let us build
the system prompt step by step.

:::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par291 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB128 .pagebreak aria-label="128"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The system prompt is a JSON-formatted string
containing the following key fields:

::: UnorderedList
- Role

- Capabilities

- Instructions

- Tools

- Response format

- Examples (optional but recommended)
:::
::::

The initial three elements, i.e., role, capabilities, and instructions,
help set the behavior of the agent model, explicitly explaining what to
do, what not to do, and how to do it. The tools key provides the set of
tools the LLM can use to respond to user queries. The "response_format"
and "examples" help set expectations for how the model should respond.

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par299 .Para}
Next, we will walk you through a comprehensive system prompt example
that will incorporate all the above elements we discussed.

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC27 .ProgramCode}
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
system_prompt = f\'\'\'
:::

::: FixedLine
{{
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"role\": \"AI Assistant\",
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"capabilities\": \[
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"You are provided with a set of tools which are capable of
performing multiple independent activities\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"Use provided tools to help users whenever necessary\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"You may respond directly without tools assistance for
questions that don\'t require tool usage\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"Plan efficient tool usage sequences\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    \],
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"instructions\": \[
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"You\'re an AI assistant designed to help manufacturing users
decide on:
:::

::: FixedLine
           (i) Finding the right supplier,
:::

::: FixedLine
           (ii) Predicting the price of their machines,
:::

::: FixedLine
           (iii) Checking if maintenance of the machine is due or not,
and
:::

::: FixedLine
           (iv) Determining the general public sentiments towards the
machine of purchasers.\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"Use tools/functions only when they are necessary for the
task.\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"Don\'t make assumptions about what values to use with
functions.\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"If a query can be answered directly, respond with a simple
message instead of using tools.\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"When tools are needed, plan their usage efficiently to
minimize tool calls.\"
:::

::: FixedLine
    \],
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"tools\": \[
:::

::: FixedLine
        {{
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"name\": tool\[\"function\"\]\[\"name\"\],
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"description\": tool\[\"function\"\]\[\"description\"\],
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"parameters\": tool\[\"function\"\]\[\"parameters\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        }}
:::

::: FixedLine
        for tool in {tools}
:::

::: FixedLine
    \],
:::

::: FixedLine
    \"response_format\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"type\": \"json\",
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"schema\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"requires_tools\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"type\": \"boolean\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"It helps explain if tools are
required for this query\"
:::

::: FixedLine
            }},
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"direct_response\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"type\": \"string\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"It is the direct response when none
of the tools are required\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"optional\": true
:::

::: FixedLine
            }},
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"thought\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"type\": \"string\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"The underlying reasoning about how to
solve the task (only when tools are necessary)\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"optional\": true
:::

::: FixedLine
            }},
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"plan\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"type\": \"array\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"items\": {{\"type\": \"string\"}},
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"Sequence of steps to solve the task
(only when tools are necessary)\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"optional\": true
:::

::: FixedLine
            }},
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"tool_calls\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"type\": \"array\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"items\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"type\": \"object\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"properties\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \"tool\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"type\": \"string\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"description\": \"name of the tool\"
:::

::: FixedLine
                        }},
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \"args\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"type\": \"object\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"description\": \"arguments for the tool\"
:::

::: FixedLine
                        }}
:::

::: FixedLine
                    }}
:::

::: FixedLine
                }},
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"description\": \"Tools to call in sequence (only when
tools are necessary)\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"optional\": true
:::

::: FixedLine
            }}
:::

::: FixedLine
        }},
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"examples\": \[
:::

::: FixedLine
            {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"query\": \"Purchased an item from supplier on second
day of their promotion campaign\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"response\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"requires_tools\": true,
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"thought\": \"To solve this part of the problem, I
need to use the price prediction tool to predict the price of the
equipment on the second day.\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"plan\": \[
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \"Use price_prediction_tool to predict the
price.\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                        \"Return the predicted result.\"
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \],
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"tool_calls\": \[
:::

::: FixedLine
                        {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"tool\": \"price_prediction_tool\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                            \"args\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                                \"day\": 2
:::

::: FixedLine
                            }}
:::

::: FixedLine
                        }}
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \]
:::

::: FixedLine
                }}
:::

::: FixedLine
            }},
:::

::: FixedLine
            {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"query\": \"Can you list high level activities
performed in Supply Chain?\",
:::

::: FixedLine
                \"response\": {{
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"requires_tools\": false,
:::

::: FixedLine
                    \"direct_response\": \"1. Demand Planning and
Forecasting. 2. Procurement and Sourcing. 3. Production Planning and
Scheduling. 4. Inventory Management. 5. Order Fulfillment.\"
:::

::: FixedLine
                }}
:::

::: FixedLine
            }}
:::

::: FixedLine
        \]
:::

::: FixedLine
    }}
:::

::: FixedLine
}}
:::

::: FixedLine
\'\'\'
:::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par300 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB132 .pagebreak aria-label="132"
role="doc-pagebreak"}We can keep the user prompt simple as the
following:

::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC28 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
sensor_data = \"\[75, 28, 52\]\"
:::

::: FixedLine
day = 2
:::

::: FixedLine
score = 90
:::

::: FixedLine
comment = \'Machine is functioning not good so far but has expected
lifetime of 10 more years with two paid services.\'
:::
:::::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
user_prompt = f\'\'\'
:::

::: FixedLine
I have purchased a second hand Manufacturing machine from a supplier
whose rating score is more than {score}.
:::

::: FixedLine
He was running a sales promotion and I purchased it from them on their
day {day} of the sales/promotion campaign.
:::

::: FixedLine
The current sensor data of the machine is {sensor_data} and the feedback
provided by the supplier at the time of sale was {comment}.\'\'\'
:::
:::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::

In the planning phase of the tool usage by the LLM, it is decided which
tool to execute and in which order based on the user's prompt. In the
above example, the prompt is designed to use all the available tools.
Let's see how.

"I have purchased a secondhand Manufacturing machine from a supplier
whose rating score is more than *{score}*" will trigger the best
supplier matching agent.

"He was running a sales promotion, and I purchased it from them on their
day *{day}* of the sales/promotion campaign." will trigger the price
prediction agent.

"The current sensor data of the machine is *{sensor_data}*" will trigger
the maintenance agent.

"The feedback provided by the supplier at the time of sale was
*{comment}*." will trigger the sentiment analysis agent.

When we change the user prompt, the LLM automatically adjusts, i.e.,
include or exclude, the set of tools required to complete the response.
This adaptability is the reason why we refer to the orchestrator as the
"brain" of the agentic AI system. All the controls for solution
functioning are applied from this layer, where LLM helps plan and make
real-time decisions on the set of tools it requires during each
execution.

::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Par307 .Para}
Finally, at the end of the orchestrator.py file, we just need to call
the run_workflow method which we defined earlier and pass the system
prompt, user prompt, and the list of tools.

:::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PC29 .ProgramCode}
::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
response = run_workflow(system_prompt, user_prompt, tools)
:::

::: FixedLine
print(response)
:::
:::::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec15Heading"}
#### Code Structure {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB133 .pagebreak aria-label="133"
role="doc-pagebreak"}After defining all the
[agents]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm77} and orchestrator, the
final code structure would look something like below.

<figure id="Figb" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO8" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Figb_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle22"
alt="File explorer screenshot showing a directory structure for a project named &quot;MANUFACTURING.&quot; It includes folders such as &quot;.venv,&quot; &quot;.vscode,&quot; &quot;agents,&quot; and &quot;orchestrator.&quot; The &quot;agents&quot; folder contains subfolders like &quot;predictive_maintenance_agent,&quot; &quot;price_prediction_agent,&quot; and &quot;sentiment_analysis_agent,&quot; each with various Python files and a Dockerfile. The &quot;orchestrator&quot; folder includes files like &quot;docker-compose.yml,&quot; &quot;DockerFile,&quot; &quot;orchestrator.py,&quot; and &quot;requirements.txt.&quot;" />
</div>
</figure>

The "***.venv***" directory contains the local environment for this
solution. All dependencies and libraries are installed in this
[local]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm78} environment. The ".env"
file contains all the required configuration variables.

The content of "***.env***" file is as below:

<figure id="Figc" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_MO9" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Figc_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="A screenshot of a .env file in a code editor. The file contains environment variables for a service principal, tenant ID, client ID, client secret, key vault name, and key vault URI, with placeholders for each value. It also includes URIs for deployed AKS microservices related to supplier matching, price prediction, maintenance prediction, and sentiment analysis, each with a placeholder for the endpoint URL." />
</div>
</figure>
:::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec16Heading"}
#### The Execution and Output {#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_Sec16Heading .Heading}

[]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_PB134 .pagebreak aria-label="134"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Now that we have defined the
[agents]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm79} and orchestrator, it\'s
time to focus on the execution flow and the output produced. The
"[orchestrator]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm80}" expects all the
agents to be already deployed and actively running. Each agent will act
as a black box for the orchestrator which means it will just send input
and expects an output without knowing the internal functioning of the
agents. Make sure all your agents are correctly deployed, executing and
accessible via their own unique endpoint as shown below. In our setup
for the demo perspective, we have hosted all our agents locally, each
running on different ports. Each of them is producing results as shown
below:

**Supplier Matching Agent**

![Screenshot of a web interface titled \"Supplier Matching Agent\"
version 1.0, compliant with OAS 3.1. It includes a link to
\"/openapi.json\" and a contact named Vikas Sinha. Below, there is a
section labeled \"default\" with a green button for a POST request to
\"/match-supplier\" labeled \"Match
Supplier.\"](images/641294_1_En_4_Chapter/641294_1_En_4_Figd_HTML.jpg){.ImageStyle23}

The request [body]{#641294_1_En_4_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm81} for this agent
is empty.

</div>
