  - **Could have**: These are nice to have if time permits.

  - **Won't have**: These are out of scope items for now.
  :::
  ::::
:::
::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par96 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB171 .pagebreak aria-label="171"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In our example use case:

::: UnorderedList
- **Must have**: Full data flow and transformation tracing, RBAC for
  access control, and
  [anonymization]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm15} (PII).

- **Should have**: Real-time updates.

- **Could have**: Impact analysis or AI-driven anomaly detection.

- **Won't have**: Multicloud support.

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par101 .Para}
  Implement prototype and validate with stakeholders.

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Create mockups and UI sketches for early feedback.

  - Implement a small proof of concept (PoC) to check feasibility.

  - In our example, a demo of extraction helps discuss all assumptions
    and constraints very clearly with stakeholders.
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par105 .Para}
  Defining acceptance criteria for each requirement.

  ::: UnorderedList
  - All the requirements are converted into features and stories with
    clearly defined acceptance criteria.

  - Clearly call out dependencies.

  - Tag test cases for each requirement.
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par109 .Para}
  Maintain a requirement traceability
  [matrix]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm16} (RTM).

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Ensures every requirement is mapped to a business goal and test
    cases and helps track changes and impact analysis.

  - Below Table
    [[5-4](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab4)]{.InternalRef}, showcases
    the RTM.
  :::
  ::::
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab4 .Table}
  Requirement ID   Business objective        Technical component      Test case ID   Status
  ---------------- ------------------------- ------------------------ -------------- -------------
  FR-01            Improve data governance   Extraction algorithm     TC-01          Done
  FR-02            Enhance usability         Graph visualization UI   TC-02          Done
  NFR-03           Security compliance       RBAC and encryption      TC-03          In progress

  : [Table 5-4]{.CaptionNumber}\
  [RTM]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm17} {.TableStyle5}
:::
:::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par112 .Para}
::: UnorderedList
- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par113 .Para}
  []{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB172 .pagebreak aria-label="172"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}Capturing changes in requirements.

  ::: UnorderedList
  - As requirements are ever-evolving, there are expected changes which
    must be captured clearly and all downstream impact to business
    outcome, timelines, budgets, etc., must be recalculated and
    showcased to stakeholders. An example of change request matrix is
    shown in the Table
    [[5-5](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab5)]{.InternalRef} below.

  - The usual process would be something like

  - Capturing changes in a structured format.

  - Reevaluating the impact on scope, budget, and timeline.

  - Getting approval from stakeholders before implementation is needed.
  :::
  ::::
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab5 .Table}
  Change ID   Description                                                    Impact analysis                       Approval status
  ----------- -------------------------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------- -----------------
  CR-01       Add support for NoSQL database                                 Medium impact on development effort   Approved
  CR-02       Add a pane to showcase user activities on existing dashboard   Low impact on development effort      In progress

  : [Table 5-5]{.CaptionNumber}\
  [Change request matrix]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm18}
  {.TableStyle5}
:::
:::::

By investing time in this step, we ensure that the solution is built
correctly for the right problem in the right way.
:::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec7Heading"}
### Solution Architecture {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec7Heading .Heading}

Once we have the requirements validated, the next step is defining the
architecture which covers and represents all business and technical
objectives. Architecting an enterprise-grade solution requires a
methodological approach to designing, implementing, and maintaining
scalable, secure, high performing, and robust/resilient systems. All the
functional and nonfunctional requirements must be well represented in
architectural documents. This document must be clearly crafted as it
involves making high-level design choices; selecting tools,
technologies, frameworks, and design patterns; and defining interactions
between system components while ensuring seamless integrations with
existing systems and helps develop scalable, robust, and secure system.

[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB173 .pagebreak aria-label="173"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In any large-scale enterprise implementations,
especially those involving [emerging
technologies]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm19} such as generative
AI, it's essential to have a well-designed architecture document. Few of
the key architecture [artifacts]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm20}
for an enterprise-grade large project include

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par122 .Para}
::: UnorderedList
- **Solution overview diagram**: This artifact represents the key
  components and interactions between them. For example, for an
  LLM-based analytics application, it will represent the interaction
  between ETL processes, databases, APIs, LLM components, and
  visualization layers.

- **Component and interaction diagram**: This artifact showcases the
  breakdown of how different modules communicate with each other. For
  example, data processing module and model development module which
  includes chunking submodule, embedding submodule, vector database
  submodule, visualization module, etc.

- **Data flow diagram**: This artifact showcases the flow of data across
  the system.

- **Deployment diagram**: This artifact showcases the deployment
  landscape of the system and includes all cloud components, data lake
  integrations, Kubernetes, API management layer, networking boundaries,
  etc.

- **Technology stack document**: This document details the reasons
  behind selecting each component involved, tools used, frameworks
  adhered, etc., and justifies selections such as why RAG over graph DB,
  why LLM model 'X' instead of LLM model 'Y', etc. It documents all the
  design decisions.

- **Security architecture**: This document defines the security aspects
  of the system such as authentication, authorization, and
  encryption[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}date at rest and
  in transit, compliance, etc.

- **Mind map diagram**: This helps represent an overall tasks landscape
  using a nonlinear graphical layout linked to and arranged around a
  central concept, as shown below.
:::
::::

<figure id="Fig2" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_MO2" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_5_Chapter/641294_1_En_5_Fig2_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Mind map titled &quot;IDFE Mind Map&quot; with branches illustrating a data processing workflow. The left side includes steps like &quot;DDL/DML Scripts,&quot; &quot;Raw Data Ingestion,&quot; &quot;Data Masking,&quot; &quot;Data Segmentation,&quot; &quot;Data Cleaning,&quot; &quot;Metadata Generation,&quot; &quot;Vectorization,&quot; &quot;Index Generation,&quot; and &quot;Vector Search Enablement.&quot; The right side features steps such as &quot;Prompt Input,&quot; &quot;Prompt Augmentation,&quot; &quot;Call Azure AI Search,&quot; &quot;Compile Search Results,&quot; &quot;Prepare chunks for Open AI,&quot; &quot;Call Open AI with chunks and context,&quot; &quot;Compile Response,&quot; &quot;Structure and returns results,&quot; and &quot;Stores the output.&quot; Each branch is color-coded to differentiate processes." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 5-2</span>
<p>IDFE solution <span
id="641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm21">map</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB174 .pagebreak aria-label="174"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The Figure
[[5-2](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Fig2)]{.InternalRef} shows the IDFE
solution mind map which lays down all important activities/tasks
necessary for our solution which we will cover in this chapter. The core
problem or concept is at the center, and all the tasks are organized
between two primary dimensions. The left side includes all the data
processing activities mentioned and the right side covers all activities
related to leveraging generative AI such as all the prompt processing
activities including searching and querying LLM. This is useful as it
gives a high-level view of major tasks involved in the solution at any
point in time.

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par131 .Para}
In one of the previous chapters of this book, we covered various
enterprise architecture patterns and understood their role in building
scalable, modular systems. As we move toward implementation, we are
reiterating a few of those in relevance to our solution context. Table
[[5-6](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab6)]{.InternalRef} captures some
of the relevant patterns for this solution.

::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab6 .Table}
  Pattern                         Description                                                                                                Relevance
  ------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Microservice architecture       Breaks applications into small independently deployable services                                           Enables modular components such as data ingestion, prompt management, and LLM interactions to evolve independently
  Event-driven architecture       Uses event queues and message brokers to decouple components                                               Enables triggering data flow extraction module based on user input
  Layered architecture            Separating application logic into distinct layers such as data layer, business logic, presentation layer   LLM Layer to manage all interactions call
  Service-oriented architecture   Uses reusable services that communicate over APIs                                                          Enables easy interaction with external tools such CRMs, metadata catalogs
  Serverless architecture         Uses cloud functions for autoscaling and cost efficiency                                                   Suited for real-time prompt processing to manage bursty workloads or on demand inferencing

  : [Table 5-6]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Relevant design patterns for the
  [solution]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm22} {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB175 .pagebreak aria-label="175"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Each plays a significant role, and choice must be
made depending on the integration and scalability requirements as well
as existing enterprise setups.

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par133 .Para}
A step-by-step approach must be followed to architect an
enterprise-grade solution. Here are key steps illustrated using our IDFE
[solution]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm23}[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm24}
for reference:

::: UnorderedList
- **Selection of the architecture pattern**: Evaluate patterns for
  scalability, fault tolerance, and extensibility. Consider cloud
  native, microservice, or hybrid approaches. We will follow a
  microservice architecture with event-driven processing for IDFE
  enabling loosely coupled modules to independently handle the assigned
  tasks such as ingestion, LLM-based extraction.

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par135 .Para}
  **Selection of technology stack**: Choosing the right database, cloud
  infra and framework optimizes performance, cost, and maintainability.
  It should be based on enterprise standards and the solution
  requirements. Here is the tech stack for IDFE solution:

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Cloud infrastructure[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}Azure

  - Compute layer[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}Azure
    Kubernetes (AKS)

  - Storage[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}Azure Blob Storage

  - Vector DB[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}Azure Search
    Service

  - LLM foundation model[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}Azure
    OpenAI-4o

  - Secret management[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}Azure
    Key Vault

  - Backend database[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}Azure SQL
    server

  - PIIs handling[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}Azure
    Language Service
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par144 .Para}
  []{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB176 .pagebreak aria-label="176"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}Implement security, compliance, and governance

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Security must be built from the ground up and must not be treated as
    an afterthought. Define RBAC, encrypt the data at rest and in
    transit, and implement logging and monitoring metrics to maintain a
    full audit trail.

  - In our IDFE solution, only selected users are added to AD groups
    that have access to Blob Storage to download the output files. Every
    time raw/processed data is stored on Blob Storage, it is encrypted.
    Also, it remains so while fetching it into the code.

  - All queries and user logs are maintained on the SQL server.
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par148 .Para}
  Interoperability with other enterprise systems

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Ensure seamless integration with existing systems such as CRM, ERP,
    data lakes, and visualization tool leveraging REST APIs and message
    queues for communication. The IDFE solution exposes REST API
    enabling governance applications to consume the generated insights
    in real time.
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par150 .Para}
  Deployment and monitoring strategy

  ::: UnorderedList
  - A production-grade solution requires automated and observable
    deployments. Implement continuous integration and deployment
    pipelines. Use monitoring tools for performance tracking.

  - []{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB177 .pagebreak aria-label="177"
    role="doc-pagebreak"}We have automated the deployment process of
    IDFE solution by implementing CI/CD pipelines on Azure DevOps, using
    Azure Git for code integration and versioning.
  :::
  ::::
:::
::::

Together, these design decisions ensure enterprise ready
production-grade solutions. In the next section, we will walk you
through the solution overview.

::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec8Heading"}
#### Solution Overview: IDFE (Intelligent Data Flow Explorer) {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec8Heading .Heading}

As we discussed at the start of the chapter, ability to trace the data
journey is critical to ensure data quality, enable impact analysis, and
help us remain compliant in a regulated environment, supports AI
adoption, and helps maintain business transparency. In an enterprise and
complex environment where data flows across multiple [distributed
systems]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm25}, it becomes extremely
crucial to understand the complete life cycle. In this section, we will
provide an overview of the IDFE solution implementation that is designed
to supports complex, multisystem landscape where data originates from
distributed sources, undergoes numerous transformations, and is consumed
by reporting systems, analytical platforms, or dashboards.

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par155 .Para}
The primary goal of the solution is to help

::: UnorderedList
- Trace the journey of data elements across databases, tables, views,
  and procedures

- Automate the extraction of data flow from SQL based DDL and DML
  scripts across business units

- Capture data transformations logic including calculations,
  aggregations, etc.

- Support compliance and impact analysis requirements
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par160 .Para}
Table [[5-7](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab7)]{.InternalRef} showcases
the key capabilities of the solution.

::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab7 .Table}
  Capability                   Description
  ---------------------------- -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Automated SQL parsing        Extraction data flow metadata from DDL and DML scripts
  Incremental data ingestion   Ability to process new data anytime
  Azure search integration     Indexes metadata into Azure Search to enable hybrid (semantic + vector) retrieval
  Contextual enrichment        Enhancing output with business context
  RBAC and auditing            Enables fine-grained access control and auditability of data interactions
  Data privacy                 Identified and mask PII using Azure Language Service
  Safety and security          Encryption, decryption, and controlled private network access enabled
  API integration              RESTful APIs for downstream applications to query and consume the output data

  : [Table 5-7]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Capabilties of the [solution]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm26}
  {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par161 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB178 .pagebreak aria-label="178"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The solution follows a modular workflow, and it
comprises of following key stages:

::: UnorderedList
- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par162 .Para}
  Ingestion

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Collects DDL and DML scripts from multiple source systems

  - Supports both full and partial updates
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par165 .Para}
  Parsing and metadata extraction

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Uses custom parser to analyze SQL scripts

  - Identifies objects such as tables, views, procedures, joins, and
    their dependencies
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par168 .Para}
  Storage and indexing

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Transforms extracted metadata into a structured format

  - Indexes into Azure Cognitive Search using a document schema
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par171 .Para}
  Query and consumption layer

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Exposes the output via search APIs and a GenAI layer enabling
    business users and systems to query the data flow output generated
    and retrieve insights
  :::
  ::::
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par173 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB179 .pagebreak aria-label="179"
role="doc-pagebreak"}The solution is built with following nonfunctional
requirements in mind:

::: UnorderedList
- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par174 .Para}
  Security:

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Integration of AAD (Azure Active Directory).

  - RBAC on Azure Blobs.

  - Encryption of contents before saving it into Blob Storage.

  - PIIs masked using Azure Language Service.
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par179 .Para}
  Reliability:

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Retry logic has been applied to handle situations like zero results
    from search service.

  - Input query augmentation by using Lucene syntax such as "INTO \~5
    table_name" to ensure that only those instances are searched for in
    Azure Search Service which have the word "INTO" in proximity of five
    words of the searched "table_name." It would look for places where
    "Insert Into table1" or "Select Into
    table [[5-1](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab1)]{.InternalRef}" are
    present rather than "Select \* from table1".

  - FQNs to bring the table name into consistent fully qualified format
    such as "database_name.schema_name.table_name." In case search query
    returns zero matching records, then this fully qualified name (FQN)
    is systematically reduced to two segment formats such as
    "schema_name.table_name."

  - At least three attempts are being made to GenAI LLM model to avoid
    any rate limit error or timeout errors. Delays are also implemented
    between two consecutive LLM calls.
  :::
  ::::

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par184 .Para}
  Scalability:

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Scalable ingestion pipeline and Azure Search for large-scale
    indexing.

  - Compliance.

  - Logging, tagging, and history of access are being maintained.
  :::
  ::::
:::
::::

[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB180 .pagebreak aria-label="180"
role="doc-pagebreak"}With a clear understanding of the deliverables of
the solution, we now move to the architecture section where we will
cover the solutions core components and their interactions.
:::::::::::

::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec9Heading"}
#### Detailed Architecture of the Solution {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec9Heading .Heading}

<figure id="Fig3" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_MO3" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_5_Chapter/641294_1_En_5_Fig3_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Flow chart illustrating a data processing system with two main layers: &quot;One Contextual Layer&quot; and &quot;Consumption Layer.&quot; The Contextual Layer includes data sources, ingestion through scripts, and processing involving encryption, masking, text analytics, embedding, and indexing. The Consumption Layer involves query processing, filtering, augmentation, retrieval, prompt engineering, and output generation using Azure GPT-4. Outputs are validated, formatted, and presented in Excel and JSON formats. Icons at the bottom represent technologies like OpenAI, Docker, and FastAPI." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 5-3</span>
<p>Architecture of the <span
id="641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm27">solution</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

Let's now walk through each
[component]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm28} of the detailed
architecture, shown in Figure
[[5-3](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Fig3)]{.InternalRef}, to understand
and analyze in detail.

**Data Source and Ingestion**

The solution begins with ingestion of database dump files (DDL and DML
scripts) from various SQL based systems such as T-SQL, Oracle PL/SQL,
etc. These scripts comprise of stored procedures, views, functions,
table schemas, triggers, packages, packages body definition, etc., which
are critical to understand the data flow. To maintain processing
efficiency, files of similar type from a database are kept and ingested
as one file, e.g., procedures from database 1 into one file, functions
in another file, and so on. This approach improves parsing accuracy and
performance as it enables predictable input type format at one time.
This process supports both batch file upload and live connection to the
source systems.

**Data Processing Layers**

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par193 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB181 .pagebreak aria-label="181"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Once the database files are ingested, the solution
processes and stores the data in Azure BLOB
Storage[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}based medallion
architecture. This comprises of four layers:

::: UnorderedList
- **Raw layer**: Stores the original raw SQL scripts as received from
  the source systems. This serves as the immutable source of truth,
  enabling reprocessing if needed.

- **Masked layer**: Sensitive data such as personally identifiable
  [information]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm29} (PII) are
  identified and masked using the Azure Language Service. This is
  further encrypted and stored in this layer.

- **Segmented layer**: The scripts consist of a mix of objects of
  different types (procedures, functions, views, etc.) and size. To make
  processing efficient, each needs to be clearly segregated and chunked
  to ensure that each is stored in files not exceeding more than 2MB. As
  an exception, if any object exceeds the size of 2MB threshold, then
  also it's stored fully as one single file.

- **Analytical layer (SQLToText)**: Metadata extraction and other
  structured representations of the object are created and stored in
  this layer.
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par198 .Para}
The key operations which are performed within this component are as
follows:

::: UnorderedList
- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par199 .Para}
  Chunking for processing

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Large scripts are broken into smaller 2MB file sizes to avoid
    truncation during processing.

  - This ensures granular indexing and retrieval.
  :::
  ::::

- Cleaning and segmentation

- Masking of PII data

- Removing nonessential comments such as PRINT, DECLARE, and SET
  statements

- Extracting metadata

- Tokenizing tables, views, functions, procedures, etc.

- Embedding and indexing
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par208 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB182 .pagebreak aria-label="182"
role="doc-pagebreak"}After segmentation, each SQL object is transformed
into an embedding representation for retrieval, as shown in Table
[[5-8](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab8)]{.InternalRef} below. Azure
OpenAI's "text-embedding-3-large" model (256 dimensions) is used here as
it captures the semantic meaning of the SQL code in vector form. The
table below represents an indicative transformation each SQL object will
pass through.

::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab8 .Table}
  Object   Tokenized form   Embedded representation
  -------- ---------------- ---------------------------
  Proc1    \[18,22,97\]     \[0.578,1.516,2.98, ...\]
  Proc2    \[17,23,77\]     \[0.123,0.375,0.78, ...\]
  Proc3    \[29,32,17\]     \[2.934,1.675,1.2\]

  : [Table 5-8]{.CaptionNumber}\
  [Tokenized and embedded
  representation]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm30} {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

These are indexed in Azure cognitive search which supports hybrid search
to improve query recall and relevance. Hybrid search is a combination of
semantic search and keyword-based search. Semantic search happens via
embeddings for capturing conceptual matches and intent, whereas
keyword-based search is performed via Lucene for precision on exact
terms or syntax.

**Query Processing, Response Generation, and Consumption layer**

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par211 .Para}
The final component enables the end users to interact with the processed
data both using API as well as natural language querying. RESTful APIs
expose the indexed metadata for downstream integration with BI tools,
reporting dashboards, or governance platforms. GenAI-powered natural
language interface enables users to ask contextual questions such as
"What reports will be impacted by changes in Table A?". A user query
such as "Find the impact of Proc1" is processed in two stages:

::: UnorderedList
- Azure AI Search retrieves relevant SQL objects using hybrid search
  (vector and text search).

- Azure OpenAI LLM processes the search results and reconstructs the
  data flow and its impact.
:::
::::

The final output is a blend of metadata, traceable data paths, and
business context. Results are formatted for human readability or
JSON-based consumption based on where the information is requested from.

The solution follows a modular design approach, with an architecture
that is scalable, secure, and optimized for incremental data processing.
This ensures seamless []{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB183 .pagebreak
aria-label="183" role="doc-pagebreak"}integration of new data into the
existing index to enhance functionality without disrupting the existing
operations. Now that we have understanding of the various components,
next we will deep dive into the data pipeline that powers the ingestion,
transformation, and storage of the SQL objects.
:::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10 .section .Section2 .RenderAsSection2 aria-labelledby="Sec10Heading"}
### Data Pipeline Implementation {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec10Heading .Heading}

A robust data pipeline [must]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm31} be
implemented as it forms the operational backbone which helps with
structured movement, transformation, and management of data from
different sources to the designated end storage. At its core, a [data
pipeline]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm32} is an automated workflow
that extracts, processes, and loads data from multiple sources to a
target system of choice in a methodological, reliable, and efficient
manner.

A well-architected data pipeline ensures high-quality, contextually
enriched data is available for AI-powered applications. By following a
[structured approach]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm33} from
ingestion to vectorization, enterprises can enable seamless integration
with LLMs, search engines, and decision support systems while ensuring
scalability, security, and compliance.

Let us begin with a broad overview of essential stages within an
enterprise-grade data processing layer, which is responsible for
preparing enterprise data for structured analysis and retrieval.

The Figure [[5-4](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Fig4)]{.InternalRef}
below shows a quick view of these essential stages of the data pipeline,
from ingestion to vectorization for AI consumption.

<figure id="Fig4" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_MO4" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_5_Chapter/641294_1_En_5_Fig4_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Flow chart illustrating enterprise-wide data processing and indexing. The process begins with data extraction from various sources like Excel, SQL Server, and others, facilitated by connectors. Data enters the Data Lake, undergoing stages such as cleaning, transformation, validation, and enrichment. The processed data is categorized into audio/video, config, and tabular files. These are vectorized and indexed into respective indices. The final output is utilized by downstream applications, each consuming different indices." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 5-4</span>
<p>Data pipeline <span
id="641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm34">implementation
details</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec11Heading"}
#### 1. Data Ingestion and Source Integration {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec11Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par220 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB184 .pagebreak aria-label="184"
role="doc-pagebreak"}[Data
ingestion]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm35} is the
[process]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm36} of collecting raw data
from multiple diverse enterprise repositories and integrating it into a
centralized storage system for downstream processing. The type of
sources may include

::: UnorderedList
- **Structured data:** Relational databases such as SQL Server,
  PostgreSQL, and Oracle

- **Semistructured data:** Formats like spreadsheets, JSON, and XML

- **Unstructured data:** Free text documents, audio, video, and log
  files
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par224 .Para}
**Connectors and ETL tools** play a crucial role in data ingestion by
automating the extraction and loading of data. Key considerations
include

::: UnorderedList
- Support for both **batch and streaming ingestion** for real-time
  processing

- Implementation of **Change Data Capture (CDC)** for incremental
  updates

- Ensure schema validation and integrity checks at extraction
:::
::::

Common tools and platforms: **Azure Data Factory, Apache NiFi, Kafka,
and Snowflake**
:::::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec12Heading"}
#### 2. Data Lake Architecture (Raw Storage---Bronze Layer) {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec12Heading .Heading}

As the data is ingested, it's
[stored]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm37}[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm38}
in its original form in a centralized data lake, ensuring that the
original dataset remains accessible for reprocessing; this is helpful in
case of any downstream errors or pipeline changes and ensures
traceability and auditability. This forms the bronze layer of the
medallion architecture serving as the foundational unprocessed storage
tier.

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par230 .Para}
Best practices:

::: UnorderedList
- Maintain **source-wise partitioning** to streamline retrieval

- Store data in **open formats** (Parquet, Avro) for flexibility and
  performance

- [Implement]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm39}[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm40}
  fine-grained **access control and encryption** for data security
:::
::::

Common raw storage options: **Azure Data Lake Storage (ADLS Gen 2),
Amazon S3, and Google Cloud Storage**
:::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec13Heading"}
#### 3. Data Transformation and Standardization (Silver Layer) {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec13Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par235 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB185 .pagebreak aria-label="185"
role="doc-pagebreak"}After the raw data is securely stored in the bronze
layer, next [stage]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm41} is to clean,
standardize, and transform it for downstream
consumption[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}this forms the
silver layer. At this layer, data undergoes a series of transformation
(shown in Table
[[5-9](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab9)]{.InternalRef}) to improve its
quality, consistency, and usability ensuring that the downstream
applications work with high-quality, unified datasets across the
enterprise. The silver layer includes various data processing
activities.

::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab9 .Table}
  Processing step   Description                                                                      Techniques used
  ----------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------
  Data cleaning     Removes duplicates, handles missing values and any inconsistencies               Deduplication and null handling
  Standardization   Converts data into a uniform format                                              Data formatting and case normalization
  Normalization     Ensuring consistency across datasets                                             Min-max scaling, one-hot encoding
  Harmonization     Aligns data from different sources to common schema                              Schema mapping, master data management
  Transformation    Converts raw data into structured formats                                        Feature engineering and aggregations
  Integration       Combines data from multiple sources                                              Joins, data fusion
  Validation        Checks for accuracy and completeness                                             Schema validation
  Enrichment        Adds external context/metadata extraction                                        Tagging, encoding
  Anonymization     Protects sensitive information                                                   Pseudo anonymization
  Aggregation       Summarizes data for analytical use                                               Grouping, window functions
  Cataloging        Maintains [metadata]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm43} for discoverability   Data catalog tools such as Azure Purview, Collibra

  : [Table 5-9]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Data processing steps and techniques
  [used]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm42} {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::
:::::

::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec14Heading"}
#### 4. Curated Data Storage (Gold Layer) {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec14Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par236 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB186 .pagebreak aria-label="186"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Following transformation, cleaning, and
standardization, data is [moved]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm44}
to the gold layer, where it is structured for efficient querying and
retrieval. Types of curated outputs:

::: UnorderedList
- **Audio/video transformed files**: Processed multimedia data for
  AI-based search

- **Config transformed files**: Metadata and structured configurations

- **Tabular cleaned files**: Well-structured data for analytics and
  indexing
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par240 .Para}
Best practices include

::: UnorderedList
- Storing data in **columnar formats** (Parquet, ORC) for
  high-performance querying

- Implementing **partitioning and indexing** to speed up access

- Defining **retention policies** to manage
  [historical]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm45} data
:::
::::
:::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15 .section .Section3 .RenderAsSection3 aria-labelledby="Sec15Heading"}
#### 5. Vectorization and Indexing {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Sec15Heading .Heading}

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par244 .Para}
The final stage in the data pipeline involves
[preparing]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm46} data for LLM-based
consumption through a combination of vectorization and indexing.

::: UnorderedList
- **Vectorization:** Converts structured and unstructured data into
  vector embeddings for similarity-based search

- **Indexing:** Organizes these embeddings into a vector database or
  hybrid search engine (e.g., Azure Cognitive Search) for efficient
  retrieval.
:::
::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par247 .Para}
Best practices:

::: UnorderedList
- Leverage dimensionally
  [optimized]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm47} embedding models
  (e.g., 256D or 768D) for a balance of performance and precision.

- To improve relevance during search, apply metadata filtering and
  scoring.

- Wherever possible, use hybrid search strategies combining semantic and
  symbolic (keyword-based) matching.

- For incremental updates, implement index refresh mechanisms.
:::
::::

[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB187 .pagebreak aria-label="187"
role="doc-pagebreak"}In the context of our IDFE solution, a
well-architected data pipeline (Figure
[[5-5](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Fig5)]{.InternalRef}) enables
efficient data retrieval, contextualization, and vectorization, ensuring
high-quality inputs for large language models (LLMs) and other AI-based
applications.

<figure id="Fig5" class="Figure">
<div id="641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_MO5" class="MediaObject">
<img src="images/641294_1_En_5_Chapter/641294_1_En_5_Fig5_HTML.jpg"
class="ImageStyle3"
alt="Flow chart illustrating a data pipeline process. It begins with &quot;Data Sources,&quot; connected to &quot;Data Ingestion&quot; via a connector symbol. The process continues to a &quot;Data Lake&quot; containing three stages: &quot;Raw Storage&quot; (Bronze Layer), &quot;Standardized &amp; Transformed Data&quot; (Silver Layer), and &quot;Curated Data&quot; (Gold Layer). The final step is &quot;Embedding &amp; Vectorization.&quot; The chart is labeled &quot;Data Pipeline&quot; at the bottom." />
</div>
<figcaption><div class="CaptionContent">
<span class="CaptionNumber">Figure 5-5</span>
<p>Data pipeline <span
id="641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm48">high-level block
diagram</span></p>
</div></figcaption>
</figure>

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par253 .Para}
To operationalize whatever we discussed so far, the IDFE solution
includes the following key
[components]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm49}[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm50},
as shown in Table
[[5-10](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab10)]{.InternalRef}.

::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab10 .Table}
  Component          Function                                                         Example in IDFE solution
  ------------------ ---------------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Data sources       Source systems that feed the data into the downstream pipeline   SQL/Oracle databases
  Ingestion layer    Connects with the source systems and loads the raw data          One-time batch ingestion of SQL/Oracle DDL and DML scripts
  Processing layer   Transforms, masks, cleans, and standardizes data                 SQL parsing, masking of PII, metadata extraction, and contextualization
  Storage layer      Stores processed data for querying and analysis                  Azure Search Service

  : [Table 5-10]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Different components in IDFE
  [solution]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm51}[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm52}
  {.TableStyle5}
:::
::::

::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par254 .Para}
The transformations in our processing layer include the following
[stages]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm53}:

::: UnorderedList
- **Raw zone**: The [data]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm54} from
  data sources are fetched, encrypted, and then stored in this zone. The
  source connector service (like connection to SQL/Oracle through
  connection string) is established and all DDL (Data Definition
  Language) and DML (Data Manipulation Language) scripts are extracted
  into Azure Blob Storage (Bronze layer equivalent).

- []{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB188 .pagebreak aria-label="188"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}**Masking zone**: There are
  [lots]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm55} of hard-coded email
  addresses, phone numbers, key people names, etc., engraved with the
  DDLs and DMLs. They must be masked before any further processing
  happens to them. We make use of Azure Language Service, part of the
  Azure Cognitive service [suite]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm56},
  to mask personally identifiable
  [information]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm57} (PIIs), such as
  shown in Table
  [[5-11](#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab11)]{.InternalRef} below.
:::

::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Tab11 .Table}
  Category                 Identified PII types
  ------------------------ ---------------------------------------------------------------
  Contact details          Full name, email address, phone number, office address
  Government identifiers   Passport number, national ID number, agent identifier details

  : [Table 5-11]{.CaptionNumber}\
  Different PII types witnessed in IDFE
  [solution]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm58}[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm59}
  {.TableStyle5}
:::
:::::

:::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par257 .Para}
The scripts are downloaded from raw zone, decrypted, and then anonymized
using the Azure Language Service. The result is then encrypted again and
stored in another folder called "masking"
[zone]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm60}.

::: UnorderedList
- **Segmented zone**: In this zone, the data is chunked and
  [segmented]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm61} for granular
  processing. The masked data from the masking zone is downloaded,
  decrypted, and chunked. We applied custom chunking strategies tailored
  specifically for SQL scripts instead of relying on predefined methods
  such as fixed length, delimiter, or stop-words based segmentations.
  Objects such as procedures, functions, and views were separated and
  stored in individual files categorized by type and maximum file size
  of 2MB. In scenarios where a single object exceeded the 2MB threshold,
  it's extracted in full and stored in a dedicated file overriding the
  size limit. This logic ensures that no object is truncated midway
  while maintaining completeness and semantic coherence. This approach
  allows multiple smaller SQL scripts to be chunked and bundled into one
  single file of maximum size 2MB, and for any large object a separate
  file ensures, it's stored in full. Regular expressions were used to
  detect logical endings, preventing objects from getting truncated
  midway. Each chunked file is then encrypted and stored in this
  segmented zone.

- :::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par259 .Para}
  []{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB189 .pagebreak aria-label="189"
  role="doc-pagebreak"}**SQLToText zone**: This [SQLToText
  zone]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm62} serves as the silver layer
  in our data pipeline architecture where cleaned, standardized, and
  enriched SQL objects are prepared for downstream indexing and
  retrieval. This layer consolidates and implements the key
  transformation steps which we discussed to make the data AI-ready.

  ::: UnorderedList
  - Standardization such as lower casing entire text.

  - Harmonization such as setting the same schema for all object.

  - Transformations such as removing commented lines of code, removing
    SQL/ORACLE stop-words such as SET NULLS, TRY...CATCH, SET. etc.,
    introducing consistent token after end of every object etc.

  - Anonymization such as masking of all PIIs.

  - Enrichment such as extracting the important metadata from the
    scripts is all done in this layer. Important metadata of any script
    involves fetching any other object that is being used within the
    current object such as other procedures which are being called or
    all tables involved, any function call, etc. It also consists of
    details on any common table expression which is defined within the
    script of the [object]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm63} or
    names of any dynamic query statement used within it.
  :::
  ::::
:::
::::

After completing the above mentioned four steps transformation process,
each SQL object along with its extracted metadata is stored in Azure
Search Index. [Azure Search Index]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm64}
helps index these details for faster retrieval. We are using
"text-embedding-3-large" model from Azure OpenAI to generate embeddings
for the SQL scripts text body, chosen for its balance between
performance and efficiency. Also, the embedding dimensions have been
limited to 256 as this is a "code" based solution and tokens proximity
helps improve the retrieval accuracy.These DDL and DML scripts are
extracted from various databases and schemas and are rarely provided as
a single batch. In real-world enterprise environment businesses
continuously introduce new tables, views, stored procedures, and schemas
as the systems evolve. To maintain an up-to-date data flow map, our
custom data pipeline is designed to handle incremental data processing
efficiently. The new objects are rerun through the same processing
pipeline as discussed above from ingestion and masking to chunking and
enrichment and the resulting new []{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB190
.pagebreak aria-label="190" role="doc-pagebreak"}metadata and embeddings
gets appended to the existing Azure Search Service index. This
incremental indexing approach ensures that our system remains
synchronized with the ever changing and evolving environments,
continuously enhancing the completeness and accuracy.

All these steps collectively contribute to building a "**contextual
layer**"[---]{.EmphasisFontCategoryNonProportional}a
[semantically]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm65} rich, structured
representation of the data landscape. This becomes the foundation for
multiple downstream generative AI applications. In our solution, we used
the retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
[approach]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm66} where these contextual
powers the retrieval component ensuring that the LLM is always grounded
with up-to-date domain knowledge.

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par267 .Para}
Below, we present pseudocode snippets (abstracted implementations) for
some of the critical data processing stages discussed above. These code
blocks illustrate how various
[components]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm67} such as chunking,
masking, metadata extraction, and indexing are orchestrated to build the
[contextual layer]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm68} effectively:

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PC1 .ProgramCode}
::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\## START
:::

::: FixedLine
IMPORT necessary standard libraries
:::

::: FixedLine
IMPORT required cloud service libraries
:::

::: FixedLine
IMPORT custom utility modules (e.g., StorageManager, Security, NLP,
Embedding, etc.)
:::
:::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
DISABLE warnings
:::
::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
STEP 1: SETUP ENVIRONMENT
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION setup_environment:
:::

::: FixedLine
    LOAD environment variables
:::

::: FixedLine
    INITIALIZE credential and secret manager
:::

::: FixedLine
    RETURN environment config and secrets client
:::
::::::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
STEP 2: LOAD CONFIGURATION
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION get_config(secret_client, use_case):
:::

::: FixedLine
    FOR the selected use case:
:::

::: FixedLine
        FETCH relevant secrets like script lists, index name, token
limits, keys
:::

::: FixedLine
    RETURN combined configuration dictionary
:::
::::::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
STEP 3: INITIALIZE SERVICES
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION initialize_services(config):
:::

::: FixedLine
    INITIALIZE storage manager with cloud credentials
:::

::: FixedLine
    INITIALIZE encryption/decryption security service
:::

::: FixedLine
    RETURN service handles (e.g., storage, security, blob client)
:::
::::::::::

::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
STEP 4: DATA MASKING
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION mask_raw_files(rai_module, storage, security, blob_client,
input_folder, output_folder):
:::

::: FixedLine
    FOR each file in input_folder:
:::

::: FixedLine
        DOWNLOAD and DECRYPT file
:::

::: FixedLine
        APPLY masking using RAI module
:::

::: FixedLine
        ENCRYPT masked output
:::

::: FixedLine
        UPLOAD masked file to output_folder
:::

::: FixedLine
    RETURN list of any unprocessed files
:::
:::::::::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
STEP 5: FILE SEGMENTATION
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION segment_files(segmentation_module, storage, security,
blob_client, input_folder, output_folder):
:::

::: FixedLine
    FOR each masked file:
:::

::: FixedLine
        DOWNLOAD and DECRYPT
:::

::: FixedLine
        SPLIT into smaller chunks
:::

::: FixedLine
        ENCRYPT each chunk
:::

::: FixedLine
        UPLOAD chunks to output_folder
:::
::::::::::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
STEP 6: SQL TO TEXT CLEANING
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION clean_segmented_files(cleaner_module, storage, security,
blob_client, input_folder, output_folder):
:::

::: FixedLine
    FOR each segmented chunk:
:::

::: FixedLine
        DECRYPT content
:::

::: FixedLine
        CLEAN and CONVERT SQL to natural text
:::

::: FixedLine
        ENCRYPT and UPLOAD cleaned output
:::
:::::::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
STEP 7: NLP PROCESSING & EXTRACTION
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION perform_text_analytics(nlp_module, storage, security,
blob_client, input_folder, use_case):
:::

::: FixedLine
    FOR each cleaned file:
:::

::: FixedLine
        DECRYPT content
:::

::: FixedLine
        EXTRACT metadata (e.g., object name, text body)
:::

::: FixedLine
        OPTIONAL: IDENTIFY relationships, tags, lineage, etc.
:::

::: FixedLine
    RETURN structured dataframe with extracted insights
:::
::::::::::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
STEP 8: SAVE TO SEARCH INDEX
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION save_to_search_index(dataframe, config, use_case, storage,
blob_client):
:::

::: FixedLine
    INITIALIZE search index module
:::

::: FixedLine
    CREATE index schema based on use_case
:::

::: FixedLine
    UPLOAD dataframe into cloud search engine
:::

::: FixedLine
    SAVE local Excel copy to storage
:::
:::::::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
MAIN EXECUTION LOGIC
:::

::: FixedLine
\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\-\--
:::

::: FixedLine
FUNCTION main:
:::

::: FixedLine
    LOAD environment and secrets
:::

::: FixedLine
    LOAD configuration for the selected use case
:::

::: FixedLine
    INITIALIZE core services
:::
::::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    DEFINE logical folder paths (raw, masked, segmented, cleaned)
:::
::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    INSTANTIATE required processing modules:
:::

::: FixedLine
        - RAI for masking
:::

::: FixedLine
        - Segmentation logic
:::

::: FixedLine
        - SQL to Text converter
:::

::: FixedLine
        - NLP Processor
:::
::::::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    EXECUTE processing steps in sequence:
:::

::: FixedLine
        - MASK raw files
:::

::: FixedLine
        - SEGMENT masked files
:::

::: FixedLine
        - CLEAN segmented chunks
:::

::: FixedLine
        - EXTRACT metadata with NLP
:::

::: FixedLine
        - UPLOAD processed results to search index
:::
:::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
IF script is run directly:
:::

::: FixedLine
    CALL main()
:::
:::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\## END
:::
::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par268 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB193 .pagebreak aria-label="193"
role="doc-pagebreak"}A more Pythonic
[implementation]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm69} of the above
would look as below:

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PC2 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# core_data_pipeline.py
:::
::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from typing import Tuple
:::
:::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# Placeholder for all your core modules
:::

::: FixedLine
from modules.environment import EnvironmentManager
:::

::: FixedLine
from modules.config import ConfigManager
:::

::: FixedLine
from modules.services import ServiceInitializer
:::

::: FixedLine
from modules.masking import MaskingProcessor
:::

::: FixedLine
from modules.segmentation import SegmentationProcessor
:::

::: FixedLine
from modules.cleaning import SQLCleaner
:::

::: FixedLine
from modules.nlp_processing import NLPProcessor
:::

::: FixedLine
from modules.indexing import IndexUploader
:::
::::::::::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class DataPipeline:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env = None
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.config = None
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.services = {}
:::
::::::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def setup_environment(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"Initializes environment variables and credential
store.\"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env, secret_client = EnvironmentManager.load()
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.config = ConfigManager.load(secret_client,
self.env\[\"use_case\"\])
:::
:::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def initialize_services(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"Initializes cloud services like storage, security, and
blob clients.\"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.services = ServiceInitializer.initialize(self.config)
:::
::::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def process_pipeline(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"Executes the full AI data preparation pipeline.\"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        use_case = self.env\[\"use_case\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        folders = self.\_define_paths(use_case)
:::
:::::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        # Instantiate modular processors
:::

::: FixedLine
        masking = MaskingProcessor(self.config, folders)
:::

::: FixedLine
        segmentation = SegmentationProcessor(self.config, folders)
:::

::: FixedLine
        cleaner = SQLCleaner(self.config, folders)
:::

::: FixedLine
        nlp = NLPProcessor(self.config, folders, use_case)
:::

::: FixedLine
        uploader = IndexUploader(self.config, folders, use_case)
:::
:::::::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        # Pipeline Steps
:::

::: FixedLine
        masking.mask_raw_files(self.services)
:::

::: FixedLine
        segmentation.segment_files(self.services)
:::

::: FixedLine
        cleaner.clean_segmented_files(self.services)
:::

::: FixedLine
        processed_df = nlp.extract_and_enrich(self.services)
:::

::: FixedLine
        uploader.upload_to_index(processed_df, self.services)
:::
:::::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_define_paths(self, use_case: str) -\> dict:
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"Defines logical folder paths used in the pipeline.\"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        base = f\"{use_case}\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        return {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"raw\": f\"{base}/RawSQLFiles\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"masked\": f\"{base}/MaskedSQLFiles\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"segmented\": f\"{base}/SegmentedSQLFiles\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"cleaned\": f\"{base}/SQLToText\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::
::::::::::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def run(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        \"\"\"Main execution function.\"\"\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.setup_environment()
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.initialize_services()
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.process_pipeline()
:::
::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
if \_\_name\_\_ == \"\_\_main\_\_\":
:::

::: FixedLine
    pipeline = DataPipeline()
:::

::: FixedLine
    pipeline.run()
:::
::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par269 .Para}
[]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PB195 .pagebreak aria-label="195"
role="doc-pagebreak"}Suggested [code
structure]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm70} is:

:::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PC3 .ProgramCode}
::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
.
:::

::: FixedLine
├── core_data_pipeline.py
:::

::: FixedLine
└── modules/
:::

::: FixedLine
    ├── \_\_init\_\_.py
:::

::: FixedLine
    ├── environment.py  # Handles .env & secret loading
:::

::: FixedLine
    ├── config.py       # Loads configs based on use case
:::

::: FixedLine
    ├── services.py     # Initializes storage, security
:::

::: FixedLine
    ├── masking.py      # File masking logic (RAI)
:::

::: FixedLine
    ├── segmentation.py \# File chunking logic
:::

::: FixedLine
    ├── cleaning.py     # SQL to text cleaning logic
:::

::: FixedLine
    ├── nlp_logic.py    # Text enrichment & extraction
:::

::: FixedLine
    └── indexing.py     # Upload to search index
:::
:::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_Par270 .Para}
Each of these mentioned module functions as a plug-and-play unit in the
following pseudocode, adhering to clean architecture principles. This
design ensures separation of concerns, making it easy to swap
[implementations]{#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_ITerm71} inside each
component without affecting the overall pipeline.

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#641294_1_En_5_Chapter.xhtml_PC4 .ProgramCode}
:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# environment.py
:::
::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import os
:::

::: FixedLine
from dotenv import load_dotenv
:::
:::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class EnvLoader:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, env_file_path=\".env\"):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env_file_path = env_file_path
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env_vars = {}
:::
:::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def load(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        load_dotenv(self.env_file_path)
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env_vars\[\"ENV\"\] = os.getenv(\"ENV\", \"dev\")
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env_vars\[\"AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING\"\] =
os.getenv(\"AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING\")
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env_vars\[\"AZURE_SEARCH_ENDPOINT\"\] =
os.getenv(\"AZURE_SEARCH_ENDPOINT\")
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env_vars\[\"AZURE_SEARCH_KEY\"\] =
os.getenv(\"AZURE_SEARCH_KEY\")
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env_vars\[\"AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY\"\] =
os.getenv(\"AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY\")
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.env_vars\[\"AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT\"\] =
os.getenv(\"AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT\")
:::

::: FixedLine
        # Add more as needed\...
:::
::::::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def get(self, key):
:::

::: FixedLine
        return self.env_vars.get(key)
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def as_dict(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        return self.env_vars
:::
:::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# config.py
:::
::::

::::::::::::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class AppConfig:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, env_vars):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.environment = env_vars.get(\"ENV\", \"dev\")
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.azure_search = {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"endpoint\": env_vars.get(\"AZURE_SEARCH_ENDPOINT\"),
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"key\": env_vars.get(\"AZURE_SEARCH_KEY\"),
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"index_name\": f\"sql-elements-{self.environment}\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.azure_openai = {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"api_key\": env_vars.get(\"AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY\"),
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"endpoint\": env_vars.get(\"AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT\"),
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"deployment\": env_vars.get(\"AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT\",
\"gpt-4\")
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.storage = {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"connection_string\":
env_vars.get(\"AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING\"),
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"input_container\": \"sql-input\",
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"output_container\": \"sql-output\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.use_case = \"DataProvenance\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.vector_enabled = False  # Toggle between semantic vs hybrid
search
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# services.py
:::
::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient
:::

::: FixedLine
from utils.crypto_utils import AESCrypto
:::

::: FixedLine
from utils.storage_utils import StorageUtils
:::

::: FixedLine
from ai_engines.rai import ResponsibleAIEngine
:::

::: FixedLine
from ai_engines.segmenter import SegmenterEngine
:::

::: FixedLine
from ai_engines.cleaner import CleanerEngine
:::

::: FixedLine
from ai_engines.nlp import NLPEngine
:::
::::::::::

::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class ServiceFactory:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, config):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.config = config
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.\_initialize_core_services()
:::
:::::::

::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_initialize_core_services(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.blob_service_client =
BlobServiceClient.from_connection_string(
:::

::: FixedLine
            self.config.storage\[\"connection_string\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        )
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.input_blob_client =
self.blob_service_client.get_container_client(
:::

::: FixedLine
            self.config.storage\[\"input_container\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        )
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.output_blob_client =
self.blob_service_client.get_container_client(
:::

::: FixedLine
            self.config.storage\[\"output_container\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        )
:::
:::::::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        self.crypto = AESCrypto()
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.storage_utils = StorageUtils()
:::
:::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        # Engines (abstracted per use case)
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.rai_engine = ResponsibleAIEngine(self.config.azure_openai)
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.segmenter_engine = SegmenterEngine()
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.cleaner_engine = CleanerEngine()
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.nlp_engine = NLPEngine(self.config.use_case)
:::
::::::::

:::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def get_services(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        return {
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"input_blob_client\": self.input_blob_client,
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"output_blob_client\": self.output_blob_client,
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"crypto\": self.crypto,
:::

::: FixedLine

:::

::: FixedLine
            \"rai_engine\": self.rai_engine,
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"segmenter_esngine\": self.segmenter_engine,
:::

::: FixedLine
            \"cleaner_engine\": self.cleaner_engine,
:::

::: FixedLine

:::

::: FixedLine
        }
:::

:::: LineGroup

\# masking.py
:::
::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class SQLMasker:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, rai_engine, storage_client, security_client,
blob_client, input_folder, output_folder):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.rai = rai_engine
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.storage = storage_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.security = security_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.blob_client = blob_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.input_folder = input_folder
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.output_folder = output_folder
:::
:::::::::::

::::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def mask_all_files(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        blobs = self.\_get_input_files()
:::

::: FixedLine
        for blob in blobs:
:::

::: FixedLine
            try:
:::

::: FixedLine
                decrypted_sql = self.\_decrypt_blob(blob)
:::

::: FixedLine
                masked_sql = self.rai.mask_sensitive_info(decrypted_sql)
:::

::: FixedLine
                encrypted_sql = self.\_encrypt_content(masked_sql)
:::

::: FixedLine
                self.\_upload_masked_blob(blob.name, encrypted_sql)
:::

::: FixedLine
            except Exception as e:
:::

::: FixedLine
                self.\_log_failure(blob.name, e)
:::
:::::::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_get_input_files(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        return
self.blob_client.list_blobs(name_starts_with=self.input_folder)
:::
:::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_decrypt_blob(self, blob):
:::

::: FixedLine
        raw_content = self.storage.download_blob(self.blob_client, blob)
:::

::: FixedLine
        return self.security.decrypt(raw_content.decode(\"utf-8\"))
:::
::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_encrypt_content(self, content):
:::

::: FixedLine
        return self.security.encrypt(content.encode(\"utf-8\"))
:::
:::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_upload_masked_blob(self, original_name, encrypted_blob):
:::

::: FixedLine
        file_name = f\"Masked\_{original_name.split(\'/\')\[-1\]}\"
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.storage.upload_blob(self.blob_client, self.output_folder,
file_name, encrypted_blob)
:::
::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_log_failure(self, blob_name, error):
:::

::: FixedLine
        print(f\"\[ERROR\] Failed masking {blob_name} =\> {error}\")
:::
:::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# segmentation.py
:::
::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class SQLSegmenter:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, segmenter_engine, storage_client,
security_client, blob_client, input_folder, output_folder):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.segmenter = segmenter_engine
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.storage = storage_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.security = security_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.blob_client = blob_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.input_folder = input_folder
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.output_folder = output_folder
:::
:::::::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def segment_files(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        blobs =
self.blob_client.list_blobs(name_starts_with=self.input_folder)
:::

::: FixedLine
        for blob in blobs:
:::

::: FixedLine
            try:
:::

::: FixedLine
                content = self.\_decrypt(blob)
:::

::: FixedLine
                chunks = self.segmenter.split_into_chunks(blob.name,
content)
:::

::: FixedLine
                self.\_upload_chunks(chunks)
:::

::: FixedLine
            except Exception:
:::

::: FixedLine
                continue
:::
::::::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_decrypt(self, blob):
:::

::: FixedLine
        raw = self.storage.download_blob(self.blob_client, blob)
:::

::: FixedLine
        return self.security.decrypt(raw.decode(\"utf-8\"))
:::
::::::

:::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_upload_chunks(self, chunks):
:::

::: FixedLine
        for chunk in chunks:
:::

::: FixedLine
            encrypted = self.security.encrypt(chunk.encode(\"utf-8\"))
:::

::: FixedLine
            file_name = f\"Chunked\_{uuid.uuid4()}.txt\"
:::

::: FixedLine
            self.storage.upload_blob(self.blob_client,
self.output_folder, file_name, encrypted)
:::
::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine

class SQLCleaner:

security_client, blob_client, input_folder, output_folder):

:::::::::::

:::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def clean_segmented_files(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        blobs =
self.blob_client.list_blobs(name_starts_with=self.input_folder)
:::

::: FixedLine
        for blob in blobs:
:::

::: FixedLine
            try:
:::

::: FixedLine
                raw_content = self.\_decrypt_blob(blob)
:::

::: FixedLine
                cleaned_text = self.cleaner.clean(raw_content)
:::

::: FixedLine

:::

::: FixedLine
            except:
:::

::: FixedLine

:::
::::::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_decrypt_blob(self, blob):
:::

::: FixedLine
        raw = self.storage.download_blob(self.blob_client, blob)
:::

::: FixedLine
        return self.security.decrypt(raw.decode(\"utf-8\"))

::::::: LineGroup

    def \_upload_cleaned(self, original_name, cleaned_text):
:::

:::

::: FixedLine

self.security.encrypt(cleaned_text.encode(\"utf-8\"))
:::

::: FixedLine

file_name, encrypted)

\# nlp_processing.py
:::
::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
import pandas as pd
:::
::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class NLPProcessor:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, nlp_engine, storage_client, security_client,
blob_client, input_folder, use_case):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.nlp = nlp_engine
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.storage = storage_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.security = security_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.blob_client = blob_client
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.input_folder = input_folder
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.use_case = use_case
:::
:::::::::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def process_files(self):
:::

::: FixedLine
        records = \[\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        blobs =
self.blob_client.list_blobs(name_starts_with=self.input_folder)
:::
::::::

:::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        for blob in blobs:
:::

::: FixedLine
            try:
:::

::: FixedLine
                text = self.\_decrypt(blob)
:::

::: FixedLine
                scripts = self.\_split_by_go(text)
:::

::: FixedLine
                records.extend(self.\_extract_features(scripts))
:::

::: FixedLine
            except:
:::

::: FixedLine
                continue
:::
::::::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
        df = self.\_structure_dataframe(records)
:::

::: FixedLine
        return df
:::
:::::

:::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_decrypt(self, blob):
:::

::: FixedLine
        raw = self.storage.download_blob(self.blob_client, blob)
:::

::: FixedLine
        return self.security.decrypt(raw)
:::
::::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_split_by_go(self, text):
:::

::: FixedLine
        return text.split(\"go\\n\\n\")
:::
:::::

::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_extract_features(self, scripts):
:::

::: FixedLine
        return \[{\"object_name\": self.nlp.extract_object_name(s),
\"text\": s} for s in scripts\]
:::
:::::

::::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
    def \_structure_dataframe(self, records):
:::

::: FixedLine
        df = pd.DataFrame(records)
:::

::: FixedLine
        df = self.nlp.split_dataframe(df)
:::

::: FixedLine
        df = df\[df\[\"object_name\"\].str.strip() != \"\"\]
:::

::: FixedLine
        df\[\"object_name\"\] =
df\[\"object_name\"\].apply(self.nlp.clean_file_name)
:::

::: FixedLine
        if self.use_case == \" DataProvenance\":
:::

::: FixedLine
            df = self.nlp.determine_other_elements(df)
:::

::: FixedLine
        return df\[df\[\"text\"\].notna()\]
:::
:::::::::::

:::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
\# indexing.py

from io import BytesIO
:::
::::

::::::::: LineGroup
::: FixedLine
class SearchIndexer:
:::

::: FixedLine
    def \_\_init\_\_(self, config, storage_client, blob_client,
use_case):
:::

::: FixedLine
        self.config = config
:::

::: FixedLine

:::

::: FixedLine
        self.blob_client = blob_client
:::

::: FixedLine
